topics.title: Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller; 8051 Pin Diagram
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Difference Between Microprocessor and Microcontroller; 8051 Pin Diagram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a Microprocessor?

A **Microprocessor (µP)** is a single-chip, general-purpose **Central Processing Unit (CPU)** that contains the **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)**, **Control Unit (CU)**, and an array of **registers**. It is purely a processing engine and has **no on-chip memory** (ROM/RAM) and **no on-chip I/O peripherals**. It must be interfaced externally with RAM, ROM, timers, serial ports, and I/O controllers to form a functional system.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:** "A microprocessor is a programmable digital electronic component that incorporates the functions of a CPU on a single IC. It is a multipurpose, clock-driven, register-based device that reads binary instructions from a storage device called memory, accepts binary data as input, processes data according to those instructions, and provides results as output."

### 1.2 What is a Microcontroller?

A **Microcontroller (µC)** is a **single-chip microcomputer** that integrates a **CPU**, **RAM**, **ROM (Program Memory)**, **I/O ports**, **timers/counters**, **serial communication port**, and **interrupt circuitry** all on a single Integrated Circuit (IC) die. It is designed for a **specific, dedicated control application** (an *embedded* application).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** The 8051 is an **8-bit CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer)** microcontroller manufactured originally by **Intel** (1980). It contains:
> - 4 KB on-chip ROM (Program Memory)
> - 128 Bytes on-chip RAM (Data Memory)
> - 4 I/O Ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) – 32 I/O lines
> - Two 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0, T1)
> - Full-duplex UART (Serial Port)
> - 5 Interrupt Sources

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Think of a Microprocessor as the "BRAIN" of a desktop computer.** A brain alone cannot function — it needs eyes, ears, hands, memory, and a nervous system around it to do anything meaningful. The microprocessor is similarly helpless without external RAM, ROM, and I/O chips surrounding it on a motherboard.

**Think of a Microcontroller as a "COMPLETE ORGANISM" — like a single-celled amoeba.** It has everything inside one cell wall: it can think (CPU), remember (RAM/ROM), sense the world (Input ports), and act on it (Output ports). A washing machine controller, a microwave timer, or a car's ECU uses a microcontroller because it needs to be self-contained, low-cost, and reliable.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Mapping:** Your **laptop** uses a **Microprocessor** (Intel Core i7). Your **digital watch, TV remote, and washing machine** use a **Microcontroller** (8051, PIC, AVR, ARM Cortex-M).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture Breakdown

**Microprocessor-Based System Architecture (External Integration Required):**
- **CPU Core:** ALU + Register Array + Control Unit
- **External Memory Bus:** Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus
- **External Chips Needed:** ROM (for program), RAM (for data), I/O port chips (8255), Timer chip (8253), Serial communication chip (8251), Interrupt controller (8259)
- **Result:** Large PCB area, higher cost, higher power consumption, flexible but complex

**Microcontroller-Based System Architecture (Integrated Single Chip):**
- **CPU Core:** Same 8-bit ALU
- **On-Chip Memory:** ROM (Flash/PROM) for code, RAM for variables
- **On-Chip Peripherals:** Parallel I/O ports, Timers, USART, Interrupt controller, ADC (in some variants)
- **External Components Needed (Minimum):** Power supply, Crystal oscillator, Reset circuit
- **Result:** Compact, low-cost, low-power, dedicated to one task

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table

| **Parameter** | **Microprocessor (µP)** | **Microcontroller (µC)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | A CPU on a single chip | A complete computer on a single chip |
| **On-chip Memory** | **None** (RAM, ROM external) | **Present** (ROM + RAM inside) |
| **On-chip Peripherals** | **None** (I/O, Timers external) | **Present** (I/O, Timers, UART inside) |
| **Registers** | Few general-purpose registers (A, B, H, L in 8085) | Many SFRs (Special Function Registers) and GPRs |
| **Instruction Set** | Rich, memory-intensive instructions | Reduced/CISC, optimized for control |
| **Application** | General-purpose computing (PCs, Servers) | Embedded control (Appliances, Automobiles) |
| **Cost** | High (system cost) | Low |
| **Power Consumption** | **High** (e.g., 65W–125W for modern CPUs) | **Low** (mW range) |
| **Clock Speed** | High (GHz range) | Low (typically 12 MHz–200 MHz) |
| **External Bus** | Wide address/data bus exposed | Internal bus; few external pins |
| **Design Effort** | Complex hardware + software | Simpler; mostly software-defined |
| **Examples** | Intel 8085, 8086, Core i7 | Intel 8051, PIC16F877, Atmel ATmega328, ARM Cortex-M0 |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Key Numerical Specs of 8051)

| **Specification** | **Value / Symbol** | **KTU Exam Relevance** |
|---|---|---|
| Data Bus Width | **8 bits** | Internal architecture |
| Address Bus Width | **16 bits** | External memory addressing |
| Maximum External Memory | **64 KB** (Program) + **64 KB** (Data) | $= 2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes |
| On-chip Program ROM | **4 KB** | Internal code storage |
| On-chip Data RAM | **128 Bytes** | Internal data + SFRs |
| Total ROM address space | $0000\text{H} - \text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ | 16-bit PC |
| Operating Frequency | **12 MHz** (crystal) | 1 machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods |
| Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | At 12 MHz → $T_{MC} = 1\ \mu s$ |
| Instruction Cycle | 1, 2, or 4 Machine Cycles | 1-byte instr: 1 MC; 2-byte: 2 MC; MUL/DIV: 4 MC |
| I/O Ports | **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 lines** | P0, P1, P2, P3 |
| Number of Pins | **40 pins** (DIP package) | Pin diagram question |
| Timers/Counters | **2 × 16-bit** | T0, T1 |
| Interrupts | **5 sources, 2 priority levels** | Reset, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, Serial |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Microprocessor-based systems** dominate in **High-Performance Computing (HPC)**, **workstations**, **graphics rendering**, and **servers** where tasks are dynamic and memory-hungry.
- **Microcontroller-based systems** dominate in **IoT edge nodes**, **automotive ECUs (Engine Control Units)**, **medical implants (pacemakers)**, **industrial PLCs**, and **consumer electronics** where deterministic real-time response and low power are critical.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pearl:** When asked "Differentiate," always mention **three core differences** for full marks: (1) **Memory integration**, (2) **Peripheral integration**, and (3) **Application domain (general vs. embedded control)**. A 3-mark question typically expects a small comparison table with at least 4–5 rows.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Mathematical Derivation: 8051 Machine Cycle Timing

The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by **12** to obtain one machine cycle. This is a derivation that frequently appears in KTU numerical questions.

**Given:**
- Crystal oscillator frequency: $f_{osc} = 12\ \text{MHz}$

**Step 1 — Oscillator period (Time for one crystal pulse):**
$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 2 — Substituting the value:**
$$T_{osc} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^6\ \text{Hz}}$$
$$T_{osc} = 0.0833\ \mu s = 83.33\ \text{ns}$$

**Step 3 — One machine cycle consists of 12 oscillator periods:**
$$T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting the value:**
$$T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6}$$
$$T_{MC} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{s}$$
$$T_{MC} = 1\ \mu s$$

**Step 5 — Conclusion:**
At a 12 MHz crystal, **one machine cycle of the 8051 = 1 µs**, meaning the 8051 can execute **1 million machine cycles per second**. Since most single-cycle instructions execute in 1 MC, the effective instruction throughput is ~1 MIPS (Million Instructions Per Second).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Memory Address Space Calculation

The 8051 has a **16-bit Program Counter (PC)** and a **16-bit Data Pointer (DPTR)**. The maximum addressable memory in each space is derived as follows:

**Step 1 — Number of unique addresses with 16-bit bus:**
$$N_{addresses} = 2^{16}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluating the power:**
$$N_{addresses} = 2^{16}$$

**Step 3 — Computing the decimal value:**
$$N_{addresses} = 65{,}536\ \text{locations}$$

**Step 4 — Each location holds 1 byte (8 bits), so total memory:**
$$M_{total} = 65{,}536\ \text{bytes}$$
$$M_{total} = 64\ \text{KB}$$

**Step 5 — Final result applied to 8051 architecture:**
- **Program Memory (ROM) Space:** $0000_{\text{H}}$ to $\text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ = **64 KB**
- **External Data Memory (XRAM) Space:** $0000_{\text{H}}$ to $\text{FFFF}_{\text{H}}$ = **64 KB**
- **Total addressable memory per type:** **64 KB**

### 3.3 Logical Derivation: Why a Microcontroller is Preferred for Embedded Systems

Let $C_{system}$ = Total system cost, $P$ = Power consumed, $A$ = PCB area.

$$\text{Microprocessor System: } C_{system} = C_{CPU} + C_{RAM} + C_{ROM} + C_{I/O} + C_{Timer} + C_{UART}$$

$$\text{Microcontroller System: } C_{system} = C_{\mu C} \approx C_{CPU} + C_{RAM} + C_{ROM} + C_{I/O} + C_{Timer} + C_{UART}\ \text{(integrated)}$$

For a washing machine controller:
- Microprocessor approach: ₹800 (CPU) + ₹200 (ROM) + ₹150 (RAM) + ₹250 (I/O) = **₹1400+**
- Microcontroller approach: Single 8051 chip = **₹80–₹150**

Therefore: $C_{\mu P} \gg C_{\mu C}$, and similarly $P_{\mu P} \gg P_{\mu C}$, justifying the dominance of microcontrollers in dedicated control applications.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Microprocessor vs Microcontroller Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph MP["MICROPROCESSOR SYSTEM (Multi-Chip)"]
        CPU_MP["CPU Core ALU CU Registers"]
        EXT_ROM["External ROM 27C256"]
        EXT_RAM["External RAM 6264"]
        EXT_IO["External I/O 8255"]
        EXT_TIM["External Timer 8253"]
        CPU_MP --- EXT_ROM
        CPU_MP --- EXT_RAM
        CPU_MP --- EXT_IO
        CPU_MP --- EXT_TIM
    end

    subgraph MC["MICROCONTROLLER SYSTEM (Single Chip 8051)"]
        CPU_MC["CPU Core 8-bit"]
        ONROM["On-chip ROM 4KB"]
        ONRAM["On-chip RAM 128B"]
        ONIO["On-chip I/O P0 P1 P2 P3"]
        ONTIM["On-chip Timers T0 T1"]
        ONUART["On-chip UART"]
        CPU_MC --- ONROM
        CPU_MC --- ONRAM
        CPU_MC --- ONIO
        CPU_MC --- ONTIM
        CPU_MC --- ONUART
    end
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Internal Architecture of 8051

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU["CPU ALU and Control Unit"]
    PC["Program Counter 16-bit"]
    ROM["On-chip ROM 4KB Program Memory"]
    RAM["On-chip RAM 128B Data Memory"]
    SFR["Special Function Registers SFRs"]
    P0["Port 0 P0 8-bit"]
    P1["Port 1 P1 8-bit"]
    P2["Port 2 P2 8-bit"]
    P3["Port 3 P3 8-bit with Alternate Functions"]
    T0["Timer Counter 0 16-bit"]
    T1["Timer Counter 1 16-bit"]
    UART["Serial Port UART Full Duplex"]
    INT["Interrupt Controller 5 Sources"]
    OSC["Oscillator Circuit"]

    OSC --> CPU
    CPU --- PC
    CPU --- ROM
    CPU --- RAM
    CPU --- SFR
    CPU --- P0
    CPU --- P1
    CPU --- P2
    CPU --- P3
    CPU --- T0
    CPU --- T1
    CPU --- UART
    CPU --- INT
```

### 4.3 8051 Pin Diagram (40-Pin DIP — Detailed Pin Function Map)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph DIP["8051 40-Pin DIP Package Top View"]
        P1_0["Pin 1 P1.0 T2"]
        P1_1["Pin 2 P1.1 T2EX"]

        P1_3["Pin 4 P1.3"]

        P1_6["Pin 7 P1.6"]
        P1_7["Pin 8 P1.7"]
        RST["Pin 9 RST Reset Input"]
        P3_0["Pin 10 P3.0 RXD Serial Input"]
        P3_1["Pin 11 P3.1 TXD Serial Output"]
        P3_2["Pin 12 P3.2 INT0 External Interrupt 0"]
        P3_3["Pin 13 P3.3 INT1 External Interrupt 1"]
        P3_4["Pin 14 P3.4 T0 Timer 0 Input"]
        P3_5["Pin 15 P3.5 T1 Timer 1 Input"]
        P3_6["Pin 16 P3.6 WR External Write Strobe"]
        P3_7["Pin 17 P3.7 RD External Read Strobe"]
        XT2["Pin 18 XTAL2 Crystal Output"]
        XT1["Pin 19 XTAL1 Crystal Input"]
        GND["Pin 20 VSS Ground"]
        P2_0["Pin 21 P2.0 A8"]
        P2_1["Pin 22 P2.1 A9"]
        P2_2["Pin 23 P2.2 A10"]
        P2_3["Pin 24 P2.3 A11"]
        P2_4["Pin 25 P2.4 A12"]
        P2_5["Pin 26 P2.5 A13"]
        P2_6["Pin 27 P2.6 A14"]
        P2_7["Pin 28 P2.7 A15"]
        PSEN["Pin 29 PSEN Program Store Enable"]
        ALE["Pin 30 ALE Address Latch Enable"]
        EA["Pin 31 EA External Access"]
        P0_7["Pin 32 P0.7 AD7 Multiplexed"]
        P0_6["Pin 33 P0.6 AD6"]
        P0_5["Pin 34 P0.5 AD5"]
        P0_4["Pin 35 P0.4 AD4"]
        P0_3["Pin 36 P0.3 AD3"]
        P0_2["Pin 37 P0.2 AD2"]
        P0_1["Pin 38 P0.1 AD1"]
        P0_0["Pin 39 P0.0 AD0"]
        VCC["Pin 40 VCC +5V Power Supply"]

```

### 4.4 Pin Classification Table (Key for KTU Exam)

| **Pin Group** | **Pin Numbers** | **Function** |
|---|---|---|
| **Power Supply** | **40 (VCC)**, **20 (VSS)** | +5V and Ground |
| **Crystal Oscillator** | **18 (XTAL2)**, **19 (XTAL1)** | Connect to external crystal (typically 12 MHz) |
| **Reset** | **9 (RST)** | High-to-low transition (or held high for 2 MC) resets the chip |
| **Port 0** | **32–39 (P0.0–P0.7)** | 8-bit, open-drain; serves as multiplexed AD0–AD7 (lower address + data bus) for external memory |
| **Port 1** | **1–8 (P1.0–P1.7)** | 8-bit, bidirectional, no alternate function (in basic 8051) |
| **Port 2** | **21–28 (P2.0–P2.7)** | 8-bit, serves as upper address bus A8–A15 for external memory |
| **Port 3** | **10–17 (P3.0–P3.7)** | 8-bit, has **alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |
| **Control Pins** | **29 (PSEN), 30 (ALE), 31 (EA)** | Program Store Enable, Address Latch Enable, External Access |

> [!NOTE]
> **ALE (Pin 30)** latches the lower-byte address from the multiplexed P0 bus into an external 74HC373 latch — this is the mechanism that allows the 8051 to share pins between data and address, saving package cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EA̅ (Pin 31)** = **External Access** — When tied LOW, the 8051 fetches code from **external ROM**. When tied HIGH, it executes from **internal ROM** (up to 4 KB, then switches to external automatically for addresses above $0\text{FFF}_{\text{H}}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Give two examples of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| **Microprocessor** | **Microcontroller** |
|---|---|
| 1. CPU only; no on-chip memory or peripherals | 1. CPU + memory + peripherals on a single chip |
| 2. Used in general-purpose systems (PCs, laptops) | 2. Used in embedded control (washing machine, ECU) |
| 3. Higher cost, higher power consumption | 3. Lower cost, lower power consumption |
| **Examples:** Intel 8085, Intel Core i7 | **Examples:** Intel 8051, PIC16F877A |

*(Award 1 mark for table format, 1 mark for 3 differentiating points, 1 mark for examples.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**List any six features of the Intel 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — any 6 of the following):**
1. 8-bit CPU with 16-bit Program Counter.
2. **4 KB** on-chip Program ROM.
3. **128 Bytes** on-chip Data RAM.
4. Four 8-bit I/O ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 I/O lines.
5. Two 16-bit Timers/Counters (T0, T1).
6. Full-duplex **UART** for serial communication.
7. Five interrupt sources with two priority levels.
8. 64 KB external program + 64 KB external data memory addressing.

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU Model Paper Style]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the pin diagram of the Intel 8051 microcontroller and explain the function of pins **PSEN, ALE, EA̅, RST, XTAL1, and XTAL2**.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Compare microprocessor and microcontroller in detail. Explain why microcontrollers are preferred in embedded systems with a suitable example.

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (a):**

**Pin Diagram:** *(See Section 4.3 of these notes for the 40-pin DIP layout — students must redraw this in the answer sheet with proper pin numbers and labels.)*

**Pin Functions:**

- **PSEN (Pin 29) — Program Store Enable:** This is an **output pin** that becomes active (LOW) when the 8051 fetches a byte of code from **external program memory (ROM)**. It is connected to the Output Enable (OE̅) pin of the external ROM chip. It remains HIGH during internal ROM accesses.

- **ALE (Pin 30) — Address Latch Enable:** The 8051 multiplexes its lower address byte (A0–A7) and data bus (D0–D7) on Port 0 to save pins. **ALE** provides a high-to-low pulse to **latch the lower address** into an external 74HC373/74LS373 latch, separating address and data in time. ALE is also used to clock external peripherals.

- **EA̅ (Pin 31) — External Access:** This is an **input pin**.
  - When **EA̅ = 1 (HIGH):** The 8051 executes code from **internal 4 KB ROM** for addresses $0000_{\text{H}} - 0\text{FFF}_{\text{H}}$; above that, it switches to external ROM.
  - When **EA̅ = 0 (LOW):** The 8051 fetches **all instructions from external ROM** (used in 8031/8052 systems with no internal ROM).

- **RST (Pin 9) — Reset:** A **HIGH on this pin for at least 2 machine cycles** resets the 8051. The PC is loaded with $0000_{\text{H}}$, all SFRs are set to their default values, and Ports 1, 2, 3 are set to FF. A typical reset circuit uses a capacitor and resistor to generate the power-on reset pulse.

- **XTAL1 (Pin 19) and XTAL2 (Pin 18) — Crystal Connections:** These two pins are connected to a **parallel resonant crystal** (typically **12 MHz** for standard 8051) along with two 30 pF trimmer capacitors to ground. The on-chip oscillator circuit generates the clock that drives the CPU. XTAL1 is the input, XTAL2 is the output of the internal inverter amplifier.

*(Award 1 mark for diagram, 6 marks for pin function descriptions — 1 each.)*

---

#### **Model Solution to Q-A (b):**

**Detailed Comparison Table:**

| **Sl. No.** | **Parameter** | **Microprocessor (8085/8086)** | **Microcontroller (8051)** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Memory** | External RAM and ROM needed | On-chip 4KB ROM, 128B RAM |
| 2 | **I/O Ports** | External I/O controller (e.g., 8255) | 4 on-chip 8-bit ports (P0–P3) |
| 3 | **Timers** | External timer chip (e.g., 8253) | 2 on-chip 16-bit timers |
| 4 | **Serial Port** | External UART (e.g., 8251) | On-chip UART |
| 5 | **Interrupts** | External interrupt controller (e.g., 8259) | On-chip interrupt logic with 5 sources |
| 6 | **Bus Structure** | External Address/Data/Control bus | Internal bus, only upper address exposed |
| 7 | **Application** | General-purpose (PC, Workstation) | Specific control (Washing machine, AC) |
| 8 | **Cost** | ₹1000+ system cost | ₹80–₹200 single chip |
| 9 | **Power** | Several watts | Few hundred mW |
| 10 | **Clock** | Several MHz to GHz | Typically 12 MHz |
| 11 | **Size** | Bulky PCB | Compact single-chip module |
| 12 | **Design Complexity** | High hardware design | Low — mostly software development |

**Why microcontrollers are preferred in embedded systems:**

1. **Cost-effectiveness:** In high-volume production (millions of units of a washing machine), saving ₹1000 per unit on the controller is a massive economic advantage.
2. **Size and weight:** A single 40-pin IC is far smaller than a multi-chip 8085 board.
3. **Reliability:** Fewer external connections mean fewer solder joints that can fail.
4. **Low power:** Critical for battery-powered devices (IoT sensors, wearables).
5. **Real-time deterministic response:** On-chip peripherals reduce latency.

**Example:** A **microwave oven controller** requires timing the magnetron, sensing door state, controlling display, and managing keypad input — all these tasks are simple, repetitive, and require no large memory. An 8051-based controller is the ideal choice, making the microwave cheap, compact, and energy-efficient.

*(Award 4 marks for table, 2 marks for "why preferred" reasoning, 1 mark for example.)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the architecture of 8051 with a neat block diagram. List the on-chip resources of 8051.

**(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the function of **Port 0, Port 1, Port 2, and Port 3** of the 8051. Why is Port 0 pulled up externally?

**Brief Model Solution Outline for Q-B:**

**(a)** Block diagram (as in Section 4.2). On-chip resources: 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 4 ports, 2 timers, UART, 2 external interrupts, 1 serial interrupt, oscillator circuit, 16-bit PC, 8-bit DPTR, ALU, 32 GPRs in 4 banks, 128 SFRs.

**(b)** 
- **Port 0 (Pins 32–39):** Open-drain 8-bit bidirectional. Used as **AD0–AD7 multiplexed address/data bus** when external memory is accessed. Requires **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistors** because it has open-drain transistors (no internal pull-up). When used as a general-purpose output, pull-ups are mandatory.
- **Port 1 (Pins 1–8):** Quasi-bidirectional 8-bit port with **internal pull-ups**. No alternate function in basic 8051. Used as general-purpose I/O.
- **Port 2 (Pins 21–28):** Quasi-bidirectional, has internal pull-ups. Used as **A8–A15 (upper address byte)** when accessing external memory; otherwise as general-purpose I/O.
- **Port 3 (Pins 10–17):** Quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-ups. Each pin has an **alternate function**:
  - P3.0 → RXD, P3.1 → TXD, P3.2 → INT0̅, P3.3 → INT1̅, P3.4 → T0, P3.5 → T1, P3.6 → WR̅, P3.7 → RD̅

**Why Port 0 needs external pull-up:** Port 0 outputs use **open-drain MOSFETs** only (no internal pull-up resistor) to allow it to function as a high-impedance input and to enable the multiplexed address/data operation. Without external pull-ups (typically 4.7 kΩ to 10 kΩ connected to VCC), the port cannot drive a logic HIGH — the lines would float.

*(Award 1.5 marks each for Ports 0, 1, 2 and 2.5 marks for Port 3 with alternate functions; 1 mark for pull-up explanation.)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing 8051 with 8085/8086:** The 8051 is a microcontroller; 8085/8086 are microprocessors. Students often mix up their features.
> 2. **Forgetting PSEN and EA̅ in the pin diagram:** Both are **control pins** that are mandatory in any 8051 pin diagram question. Skipping them loses 2 marks.
> 3. **P0 pull-up confusion:** Students often write "P0 has internal pull-ups like other ports" — this is **WRONG**. Only P1, P2, P3 have internal pull-ups.
> 4. **Memory confusion:** Writing "8051 has 64 KB RAM" instead of "64 KB external memory address space" loses the distinction between **internal 128 B RAM** and the **64 KB external addressable space**.
> 5. **Missing alternate functions of P3:** A pin diagram question expects you to write the alternate function (RXD, TXD, etc.) next to each P3 pin. Drawing only "P3.0" without "RXD" is incomplete.
> 6. **Wrong ALE function:** Students often say "ALE is a clock output" — it is **NOT** the system clock. It is a latching strobe for the address bus.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Module 1):**
> - **Microprocessor** = CPU only (no internal memory, no internal I/O); for general-purpose computing.
> - **Microcontroller** = CPU + Memory + I/O + Timers + UART (all on-chip); for embedded control.
> - **8051** = **8-bit CISC** microcontroller with **40 pins** in DIP package.
> - **On-chip memory:** 4 KB ROM + 128 Bytes RAM.
> - **Addressable memory:** 64 KB Program + 64 KB Data (16-bit address bus).
> - **4 I/O ports:** P0, P1, P2, P3 = 32 I/O lines.
> - **P0** is open-drain, **needs external pull-up resistors**, serves as **AD0–AD7**.
> - **P1** has no alternate function (basic 8051).
> - **P2** serves as **A8–A15**.
> - **P3** has **alternate functions**: RXD, TXD, INT0̅, INT1̅, T0, T1, WR̅, RD̅.
> - **ALE (Pin 30)** latches lower address into external latch (74HC373).
> - **PSEN̅ (Pin 29)** enables external program ROM read.
> - **EA̅ (Pin 31)** = 1 → internal ROM; = 0 → external ROM only.
> - **RST (Pin 9)** = HIGH for ≥ 2 machine cycles resets the chip.
> - **XTAL1, XTAL2 (Pins 18, 19)** connect a **12 MHz crystal**.
> - **Machine Cycle** = 12 / f_osc. At 12 MHz, T_MC = **1 µs**.
> - **Timers:** Two 16-bit (T0, T1).
> - **Interrupts:** 5 sources (Reset, INT0̅, INT1̅, T0, T1, Serial Port) with 2 priority levels.
> - **Real-world use:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, automotive dashboards, IoT sensors, industrial automation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 8051 Architecture: I/O Port Structure, Register Organization (Special Function Registers), Memory Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** 8051 Architecture: I/O Port Structure, Register Organization (Special Function Registers), Memory Organization ## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition (KTU Board Standard):** The **Intel 8051** is an **8-bit Harvard architecture CMOS microcontroller** introduced in 1981. It integrates a CPU, **4 KB of on-chip ROM (Program Memory)**, **128 bytes of on-chip RAM (Data Memory)**, **four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports (P0–P3)**, **two 16-bit Timer/Counters**, a **full-duplex UART (Serial Port)**, a **5-source / 2-vector interrupt structure**, and an **on-chip oscillator** on a single chip. It uses a **CISC instruction set** with **255 opcodes** and a **12-clock machine cycle** for the standard variant. **Key Architectural Specifications (Examinable):** - **Program Counter (PC):** 16-bit → addressable program memory = **64 KB** - **Data Pointer (DPTR):** 16-bit → addressable external data memory = **64 KB** - **Internal RAM:** **128 bytes** (00H–7FH) - **SFR Area:** **128 bytes** (80H–FFH) → mapped to upper 128 bytes of internal RAM - **Total addressable internal data space:** **256 bytes** - **Operating Frequency:** typically **12 MHz** crystal → **1 µs machine cycle** --- ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy: The 8051 as a "Smart Office Desk"** > > Imagine the 8051 as a clerk's workstation: > - The **CPU (brain)** is the clerk himself. > - **ROM (Program Memory)** is the *instruction manual* placed on the desk — read-only, never changes. > - **RAM (Data Memory)** is the clerk's *notepad* — where he scribbles temporary calculations. > - **SFRs** are the *specialized tools* on the desk: calculator, stapler, clock, phone — each has a fixed drawer with a unique label. > - **I/O Ports (P0–P3)** are the *four windows* of the office through which the clerk interacts with the outside world. > - **Stack Pointer (SP)** is a marker showing where the clerk last placed a sticky note — to remember tasks in LIFO order. > - **PC (Program Counter)** is the clerk's *finger on the manual* — pointing to which line he is executing. The 8051 is the **mother of all modern microcontrollers**; the ARM Cortex-M cores used in today's IoT devices trace their lineage to the architectural philosophy pioneered here. ### 1.3 Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU > [!IMPORTANT] > The 8051 is a **mandatory KTU module** because it bridges the gap between *theoretical computer architecture* and *practical embedded design*. Direct questions on **I/O port structure, SFR mapping, and memory organization** appear every semester in both **Part A (3 marks)** and **Part B (14 marks)** categories. ## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 I/O Port Structure (Ports 0, 1, 2, 3) — Operational Logic Each of the four 8-bit ports has an identical internal structure composed of: 1. **Port Latch (SFR)** — holds the output data byte. 2. **Output Driver** — drives the physical pin. 3. **Input Buffer** — reads the physical pin state. **Step-by-step port read/write logic:** - To **write** to a port: CPU writes a byte to the port latch SFR; the output driver reflects it on the pin. - To **read a port pin**: CPU sets the latch to `1` first, then reads the *physical pin* through the input buffer (this is why `1` must be written before reading). - To **read the latch**: used for "read-modify-write" instructions (e.g., `ANL P1, A`). #### Port-by-Port Analysis | Port | Pins | Internal Pull-ups | Alternate Function | Drive Strength | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | | **P0** | 32–39 | **None (Open-Drain)** | AD0–AD7 (multiplexed lower address/data bus) | Needs external **10 kΩ pull-ups** | | **P1** | 1–8 | Yes (internal) | None — pure general purpose I/O | Strong | | **P2** | 21–28 | Yes (internal) | A8–A15 (upper address byte) | Strong | | **P3** | 10–17 | Yes (internal) | **Alternate functions** (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) | Strong | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Exam Note:** Port 0 has **NO internal pull-up resistors**. To use P0 as a general-purpose output port (e.g., interfacing LEDs), you **must** connect external pull-up resistors (typically 10 kΩ). Forgetting this is the most common circuit-design error in KTU lab exams. ### 2.2 Port 3 Alternate Function Map (MUST memorize) | P3 Bit | Function | Pin Number | Description | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | P3.0 | **RxD** | 10 | Serial Input | | P3.1 | **TxD** | 11 | Serial Output | | P3.2 | **INT0̄** | 12 | External Interrupt 0 (active low) | | P3.3 | **INT1̄** | 13 | External Interrupt 1 (active low) | | P3.4 | **T0** | 14 | Timer 0 External Input | | P3.5 | **T1** | 15 | Timer 1 External Input | | P3.6 | **WR̄** | 16 | External Data Memory Write Strobe | | P3.7 | **RD̄** | 17 | External Data Memory Read Strobe | > [!NOTE] > The bar over the pin name (e.g., WR̄) indicates **active-low** logic — the function is asserted when the signal is at **0V (logic 0)**. --- ### 2.3 Register Organization — Internal RAM Structure The 8051's **128-byte internal RAM (00H–7FH)** is divided into three distinct regions: #### (a) Register Banks (00H–1FH) — 32 bytes - Four banks of **8 registers each (R0–R7)**. - Only **one bank is active** at a time, selected by bits **RS0 and RS1** in the **PSW (Program Status Word)** register. | RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | 00H–07H | | 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | 08H–0FH | | 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | 10H–17H | | 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | 18H–1FH | - **Default (after RESET):** Bank 0 is active → R0–R7 map to 00H–07H. - **Stack Pointer (SP)** typically initialized to **07H** so the first push uses address **08H** (start of Bank 1). #### (b) Bit-Addressable Area (20H–2FH) — 16 bytes - Contains **128 individually addressable bits** (16 × 8 = 128). - Bit addresses range from **00H to 7FH** (in bit-address space). - Useful for boolean flag storage. > [!NOTE] > **Bit-Address Trick:** For a byte at internal RAM address `X` (where 20H ≤ X ≤ 2FH), the bit `n` (n = 0 to 7) has the bit address: > > $$\text{Bit Address} = (X - 20H) \times 8 + n$$ #### (c) General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH) — 80 bytes - Used for variable storage, stack (above 08H), and scratchpad operations. --- ### 2.4 Special Function Registers (SFR) — Upper 128 Bytes (80H–FFH) > [!IMPORTANT] > **SFRs are mapped to addresses 80H–FFH**, occupying the same address space as the upper half of internal RAM but accessed through **different addressing modes** (direct addressing for SFRs vs. indirect for upper RAM in standard 8051; the 8052 has 256 bytes true internal RAM). #### Major SFR Map (Board Examination Favourite) | SFR | Address | Bit-Addressable | Function | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | **A (ACC)** | **E0H** | ✅ Yes | Accumulator — primary ALU operand | | **B** | **F0H** | ✅ Yes | Used in MUL/DIV instructions | | **PSW** | **D0H** | ✅ Yes | Program Status Word — flags + bank select | | **SP** | **81H** | ❌ No | Stack Pointer (8-bit, default = 07H) | | **DPTR (DPL/DPH)** | **82H / 83H** | ❌ No | 16-bit Data Pointer | | **P0** | **80H** | ✅ Yes | Port 0 latch | | **P1** | **90H** | ✅ Yes | Port 1 latch | | **P2** | **A0H** | ✅ Yes | Port 2 latch | | **P3** | **B0H** | ✅ Yes | Port 3 latch | | **IP** | **B8H** | ✅ Yes | Interrupt Priority | | **IE** | **A8H** | ✅ Yes | Interrupt Enable | | **TMOD** | **89H** | ❌ No | Timer Mode | | **TCON** | **88H** | ✅ Yes | Timer Control | | **TH0, TL0** | **8CH, 8AH** | ❌ No | Timer 0 High/Low | | **TH1, TL1** | **8DH, 8BH** | ❌ No | Timer 1 High/Low | | **SCON** | **98H** | ✅ Yes | Serial Control | | **SBUF** | **99H** | ❌ No | Serial Data Buffer | | **PCON** | **87H** | ❌ No | Power Control | > [!WARNING] > **Address Gap Rule:** SFR addresses 80H–FFH contain *only 21 active SFRs*. The remaining addresses are **unused/reserved** and writing to them has no effect, while reading returns **indeterminate (garbage) values**. A common KTU question asks: "What is the highest addressable internal data memory location?" — Answer: **FFH (256 bytes total)**, but only 128 (00H–7FH) and the 21 SFRs are implemented. --- ### 2.5 Program Status Word (PSW) — Detailed Bit Map > [!NOTE] > **PSW Register (SFR Address D0H)** — 8 bits, fully bit-addressable. | Bit | Symbol | Address | Function | | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | PSW.7 | **CY** | D7H | Carry Flag | | PSW.6 | **AC** | D6H | Auxiliary Carry (for BCD arithmetic) | | PSW.5 | **F0** | D5H | User-Defined Flag 0 (general purpose) | | PSW.4 | **RS1** | D4H | Register Bank Select 1 | | PSW.3 | **RS0** | D3H | Register Bank Select 0 | | PSW.2 | **OV** | D2H | Overflow Flag | | PSW.1 | **—** | D1H | Reserved (undefined) | | PSW.0 | **P** | D0H | Parity Flag (set if A has odd number of 1s) | --- ### 2.6 Memory Organization Summary (Harvard Architecture) The 8051 uses **separate address spaces for code (program) and data**, known as **Harvard architecture**. | Memory Type | Size | Address Range | Read Signal | Write Signal | Overlap Allowed? | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | | Internal Program (ROM) | 4 KB | 0000H–0FFFH | **PSEN̄** | — | No | | External Program (ROM) | up to 60 KB | 1000H–FFFFH | PSEN̄ | — | No | | Internal Data (RAM) | 128 B | 00H–7FH | **RD̄** | **WR̄** | No | | Internal Data (SFR) | 128 B | 80H–FFH | RD̄ | WR̄ | No | | External Data (RAM) | up to 64 KB | 0000H–FFFFH | RD̄ | WR̄ | No | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction:** The same address (e.g., 0000H) can refer to either the first byte of program memory OR external data memory — they are **independent address spaces**. The CPU uses **PSEN̄** to read program memory and **RD̄/WR̄** to access data memory. **Key Memory Locations (Board Exam Favourites):** - **0000H:** Reset vector (start of execution) - **0003H:** External Interrupt 0 (INT0) ISR - **000BH:** Timer 0 Overflow ISR - **0013H:** External Interrupt 1 (INT1) ISR - **001BH:** Timer 1 Overflow ISR - **0023H:** Serial Port ISR --- ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula & Address Cheat Sheet | Concept | Formula / Address | Unit / Note | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Program memory size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | **64 KB**, addressed by 16-bit PC | | External data memory size | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ bytes | **64 KB**, addressed by DPTR | | Internal RAM total | $2^8 = 256$ bytes | 00H–FFH (128 RAM + 128 SFR area) | | Stack initial value (typical) | SP = **07H** | Stack grows **upward** | | Stack growth | New SP = Old SP + 1 (on PUSH) | LIFO order | | Timer count register width | **16-bit** (THx + TLx) | Max count = 65535 | | Machine cycle | $T_{cy} = 12 \div f_{osc}$ | For 12 MHz crystal: **1 µs** | | Bit address (in 20H–2FH) | $(X - 20H) \times 8 + n$ | For byte X, bit n | | Number of register banks | **4** (Bank 0 to 3) | Selected by RS1:RS0 | | Number of bit-addressable SFRs | **11** | P0, P1, P2, P3, PSW, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, ACC, B | | Number of bit-addressable RAM bytes | **16** (20H–2FH) | Total 128 bits | | Total reset vector address | **0000H** | Execution begins here | | Power-on SP value | **07H** | Points to Bank 1 start | | Timer 0 ISR vector | **000BH** | 8-byte slot (000BH–0012H) | --- ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **8051 in Modern Industry:** Despite being from 1981, 8051 cores (e.g., **Silicon Labs C8051F**, **Nuvoton N76E003**, **Atmel AT89C51**) are still used in **washing machines, microwave ovens, automotive ECUs, and IoT sensor nodes** due to their **low cost, deterministic timing, and ultra-low power modes**. - **Port 0 + Port 2 Multiplexing:** The clever multiplexing of P0 as the lower address/data bus and P2 as the upper address bus is what enables the 8051 to interface with **64 KB of external memory using only 16 pins** — a significant design innovation for its era. - **SFR Bit-Addressability:** The ability to set, clear, and test single bits (e.g., `SETB P3.2`) makes real-time control loop code (e.g., for stepper motors) extremely efficient. ## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation — Internal RAM Bit-Address Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem (KTU standard):** Find the bit address of bit 3 of internal RAM byte located at address **2AH**. **Step 1 — Verify the byte is in the bit-addressable region.** The bit-addressable region spans **20H to 2FH**. The byte address is **2AH**. $$20H \leq 2AH \leq 2FH \quad \text{(TRUE — bit-addressable)}$$ **Step 2 — Apply the bit-address formula.** $$\text{Bit Address} = (X - 20H) \times 8 + n$$ Substituting $X = 2AH$ and $n = 3$: $$\text{Bit Address} = (2AH - 20H) \times 8 + 3$$ **Step 3 — Perform the subtraction in hexadecimal.** $$2AH - 20H = 0AH \quad (= 10 \text{ in decimal})$$ **Step 4 — Multiply by 8 (left shift by 3 bits).** $$0AH \times 8 = 50H \quad \text{(since } 10 \times 8 = 80 = 50H\text{)}$$ **Step 5 — Add the bit number.** $$50H + 3 = 53H$$ $$\boxed{\text{Bit Address} = 53H}$$ **Step 6 — Verification (cross-check with bit-address table):** - Byte 20H bits 0–7 → bit addresses 00H–07H - Byte 21H bits 0–7 → 08H–0FH - ... - Byte 2AH bits 0–7 → 50H–57H - Bit 3 of 2AH → **53H** ✓ --- ### 3.2 Derivation — Stack Pointer Operation After Reset and PUSH > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** After power-on RESET, SP = 07H. If the instruction `PUSH ACC` is executed, what is the new SP value, and at which address is the accumulator stored? **Step 1 — Recall the PUSH operation sequence.** The 8051 PUSH instruction: 1. **Increments SP first** (SP = SP + 1). 2. Then stores the operand at the new SP address. **Step 2 — Increment the Stack Pointer.** $$SP_{new} = SP_{old} + 1 = 07H + 1 = 08H$$ **Step 3 — Store ACC at the new SP location.** The accumulator (A = ACC = SFR E0H) is copied to internal RAM address **08H**. **Step 4 — Interpretation.** After this PUSH, the **top of stack is at 08H**, which is exactly the starting address of **Register Bank 1** — meaning subsequent PUSHes will overwrite R0–R7 of Bank 1 unless the programmer reinitializes SP (typically to **30H or higher**) at the start of the program. $$\boxed{SP_{new} = 08H, \quad \text{ACC stored at internal RAM address } 08H}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often write "SP = 07H after push" thinking the decrement-then-store rule of the 8085 applies. The 8051 uses **increment-then-store** (stack grows UP). This single conceptual error can cost **3 marks** in ESE. --- ### 3.3 Derivation — Timer Reload Value Calculation > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Configure Timer 0 in Mode 1 (16-bit) to generate a delay of exactly **1 ms** using a **12 MHz crystal**. **Step 1 — Determine the machine cycle.** $$T_{cy} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s$$ **Step 2 — Determine the required timer count.** For a delay of 1 ms: $$\text{Count} = \frac{\text{Delay}}{T_{cy}} = \frac{1000 \, \mu s}{1 \, \mu s} = 1000 \text{ counts}$$ **Step 3 — Compute the timer initial value.** The timer counts UP from the initial value to **FFFFH**, then overflows to 0000H to trigger the interrupt. Total counts per cycle = 65536. $$\text{Initial Value} = 65536 - 1000 = 64536$$ **Step 4 — Convert 64536 to hexadecimal.** $$64536 \div 16 = 4033 \text{ remainder } 8 \quad \rightarrow \text{low nibble} = 8$$ $$4033 \div 16 = 252 \text{ remainder } 1 \quad \rightarrow 1$$ $$252 \div 16 = 15 \text{ remainder } 12 \quad \rightarrow C$$ $$15 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad \rightarrow F$$ So 64536 = **0xFC18**. **Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0.** $$TL0 = 18H, \quad TH0 = FCH$$ **Step 6 — Final assembly instructions.** ```assembly MOV TMOD, #01H ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit) MOV TL0, #18H ; Load low byte MOV TH0, #0FCH ; Load high byte SETB TR0 ; Start Timer 0 ``` > [!WARNING] > **Board-Examiner Note:** The count formula `Initial Value = Max - Required` is the **#1 most-tested formula** in 8051 timer problems. Memorize it verbatim. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Memory Access Methods | Memory Region | Size | Address Bus Used | Control Signal | Access Instruction Example | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Internal RAM (00H–7FH) | 128 B | None (internal) | None | `MOV A, 30H` | | SFR (80H–FFH) | 128 B (21 used) | None (internal) | None | `MOV A, 90H` (reads P1) | | Internal Program ROM (0000H–0FFFH) | 4 KB | 16-bit (PC) | PSEN̄ | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | | External Program ROM (1000H–FFFFH) | 60 KB | 16-bit (PC) | PSEN̄ | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | | External Data RAM (0000H–FFFFH) | 64 KB | 16-bit (DPTR) | RD̄ / WR̄ | `MOVX A, @DPTR` | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic for KTU:** **"MOVC for Code, MOVX for eXternal data, MOV for everything else."** --- ### 3.5 SFR Address Derivation — Why PSW is at D0H **Step 1 — The 8051 designers reserved addresses 80H–FFH (128 bytes) for SFRs.** **Step 2 — Bit-addressable SFRs must have addresses ending in `0H` or `8H`** (so that each bit gets a unique bit-address from 80H–FFH in the bit-address space). **Step 3 — The PSW register, being fully bit-addressable, is placed at a multiple of 16.** - 80H → P0 - 88H → TCON - 90H → P1 - 98H → SCON - A0H → P2 - A8H → IE - B0H → P3 - B8H → IP - D0H → **PSW** - E0H → A (Accumulator) - F0H → B register This explains why the most important CPU registers are placed at "round" hex addresses. ## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 8051 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU[CPU Unit
8-bit ALU] PC[Program Counter
16-bit] DPTR[Data Pointer DPTR
16-bit DPH DPL] SP[Stack Pointer SP
8-bit] ACC[Accumulator A
SFR E0H] BREGB[B Register
SFR F0H] PSW[PSW Flags
SFR D0H] subgraph PROG_MEM [Program Memory] ROMI[Internal ROM 4KB
0000H-0FFFH] ROME[External ROM
1000H-FFFFH] end subgraph DATA_MEM [Data Memory] RAMI[Internal RAM 128B
00H-7FH] SFRA[SFR Area 128B
80H-FFH] RAME[External RAM 64KB
0000H-FFFFH] end subgraph IO_PORTS [I-O Ports] P0[Port 0 AD0-AD7
Open Drain] P1[Port 1
Pull-up] P2[Port 2 A8-A15
Pull-up] P3[Port 3
Alt Functions] end subgraph TIMERS [Timers and Serial] T0[Timer 0
16-bit] T1[Timer 1
16-bit] UART[UART
Serial Port] end subgraph INT_CTRL [Interrupt Controller] INTC[5 Sources
2 Priority Levels] end OSC[On-chip Oscillator
XTAL1 XTAL2] BUS[Internal Bus
8-bit Data Path] CPU --- BUS BUS --- PROG_MEM BUS --- DATA_MEM BUS --- IO_PORTS BUS --- TIMERS BUS --- INT_CTRL CPU --- PC CPU --- DPTR CPU --- SP CPU --- ACC CPU --- BREGB CPU --- PSW OSC --- CPU ``` --- ### 4.2 Internal RAM Detailed Memory Map ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph RAM00 [Internal RAM 00H-7FH] RB0[00H-07H
Bank 0 R0-R7] RB1[08H-0FH
Bank 1 R0-R7] RB2[10H-17H
Bank 2 R0-R7] RB3[18H-1FH
Bank 3 R0-R7] BIT[20H-2FH
Bit Addressable
128 bits 00H-7FH] GEN[30H-7FH
General Purpose
80 bytes] end subgraph SFR_AREA [SFR Area 80H-FFH] SFR1[80H-9FH
P0 TCON P1 SCON P2] SFR2[A0H-BFH
IE P3 IP] SFR3[C0H-FFH
Reserved ACC PSW B] end RAM00 --> SFR_AREA style RB0 fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1E88E5 style RB1 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00 style RB2 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00 style RB3 fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#FB8C00 style BIT fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#43A047 style GEN fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#8E24AA style SFR_AREA fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#E53935 ``` --- ### 4.3 Port 0 Internal Circuit Schematic (Block-Level) ```mermaid flowchart TB CPU_BUS[Internal Data Bus D0-D7] subgraph LATCH [Port 0 Latch SFR 80H] DFF[D-type Flip-Flop
8 bits] end subgraph OUT_DRV [Output Driver Stage] FET_N[N-channel MOSFET
Pull-down to VSS] EXT_PU[External Pull-up
10k to VCC
REQUIRED] end subgraph IN_BUF [Input Buffer] TRI_STATE[Tri-state Buffer
Active when reading] end PIN[P0.0 Physical Pin] CPU_BUS --> DFF DFF --> FET_N FET_N --> PIN EXT_PU -.-> PIN PIN --> TRI_STATE TRI_STATE --> CPU_BUS style LATCH fill:#E3F2FD style OUT_DRV fill:#FFF8E1 style IN_BUF fill:#F1F8E9 style EXT_PU fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#C62828,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > The dashed red box highlights the **external pull-up resistor** that students often forget. --- ### 4.4 PSW Register Bit Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P7[Bit 7
CY Carry] P6[Bit 6
AC Aux Carry] P5[Bit 5
F0 User Flag] P4[Bit 4
RS1 Bank Sel 1] P3[Bit 3
RS0 Bank Sel 0] P2[Bit 2
OV Overflow] P1[Bit 1
Reserved] P0[Bit 0
P Parity] P7 --- P6 --- P5 --- P4 --- P3 --- P2 --- P1 --- P0 style P7 fill:#FFCDD2 style P4 fill:#C8E6C9 style P3 fill:#C8E6C9 style P2 fill:#FFE0B2 style P0 fill:#E1BEE7 ``` --- ### 4.5 Memory Organization — Harvard vs Von Neumann ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph VN [Von Neumann Architecture - 8085] VNB[Single Address and Data Bus] VNM[Single Memory Space
Code and Data Mixed] VNB --> VNM end subgraph HV [Harvard Architecture - 8051] HVB1[Program Bus 16-bit] HVB2[Data Bus 8-bit] HVM1[Program Memory 64KB
Read via PSEN-bar] HVM2[Data Memory 128B internal
64KB external Read via RD-bar WR-bar] HVB1 --> HVM1 HVB2 --> HVM2 end style VN fill:#FFEBEE style HV fill:#E8F5E9 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Concept Check:** The Harvard architecture allows the 8051 to **simultaneously fetch the next instruction** (from program memory via PSEN̄) **and access data** (from data memory via RD̄/WR̄) — true parallel operation, which is why the 8051 is faster than a Von-Neumann 8085 at the same clock rate. ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **"List any three differences between Harvard architecture and Von Neumann architecture. Which architecture is used in 8051?"** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Harvard (8051) | Von Neumann (8085) | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Memory spaces | **Separate** code and data | **Unified** code and data | | Bus structure | **Two buses** (program + data) | **Single shared bus** | | Simultaneous access | **Possible** (parallel fetch + read) | **Not possible** (bus contention) | | Speed | **Faster** | **Slower** | | Hardware complexity | **Higher** | **Lower** | The 8051 uses **Harvard architecture**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **"What is the function of the PSW register in 8051? Explain the role of RS0 and RS1 bits."** **Model Answer:** The **Program Status Word (PSW, SFR address D0H)** is an 8-bit, bit-addressable register that contains: - **Status flags** reflecting the result of the last ALU operation: **CY (Carry), AC (Auxiliary Carry), OV (Overflow), P (Parity)**. - **Register bank selection bits RS1 and RS0** that choose which of the four register banks (Bank 0–3) is currently active. - **One user-defined flag F0** available for general-purpose use. **Role of RS1:RS0:** - `00` → Bank 0 (addresses 00H–07H) — **default after RESET** - `01` → Bank 1 (addresses 08H–0FH) - `10` → Bank 2 (addresses 10H–17H) - `11` → Bank 3 (addresses 18H–1FH) This allows **fast context switching** in interrupt service routines by simply changing two bits. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – July 2024]** **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **internal architecture of the 8051 microcontroller**. List all four I/O ports and their alternate functions. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Explain the **memory organization of 8051** with a neat diagram. Differentiate between program memory and data memory addressing. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Introduction (1 mark)** > The Intel 8051 is an **8-bit Harvard architecture microcontroller** with the following on-chip resources: 4 KB ROM, 128 bytes RAM, 4 I/O ports, 2 timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupt sources, and an on-chip oscillator. [1 mark] **Step 2 — Block Diagram (3 marks)** [Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram — draw equivalent in exam] Components to label: **CPU, ROM, RAM, SFR, P0–P3, T0/T1, UART, Interrupt Controller, Oscillator, PC, DPTR, SP, ACC, B, PSW.** [3 marks] **Step 3 — I/O Port Summary Table (3 marks)** | Port | Pins | Pull-up | Alternate Function | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | P0 | 32–39 | None | AD0–AD7 | | P1 | 1–8 | Internal | None | | P2 | 21–28 | Internal | A8–A15 | | P3 | 10–17 | Internal | RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD | [3 marks] --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Harvard Architecture Principle (1 mark)** > 8051 uses **separate address spaces** for program and data memory, accessed via different control signals (**PSEN̄** for code, **RD̄/WR̄** for data). [1 mark] **Step 2 — Program Memory Organization (2 marks)** - Total size: **64 KB** (0000H–FFFFH) - Internal: 4 KB (0000H–0FFFH) - External: 60 KB (1000H–FFFFH) - **Reset vector: 0000H** - ISR vectors at 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H [2 marks] **Step 3 — Data Memory Organization (3 marks)** - Total internal data memory: **256 bytes** (00H–FFH) - Lower 128 bytes: 00H–7FH (Register banks + bit-addressable + general purpose) - Upper 128 bytes: 80H–FFH (SFR area) - External data memory: **64 KB** accessed via **MOVX** instructions using **DPTR** [3 marks] **Step 4 — Comparative Table (1 mark)** | Aspect | Program Memory | Data Memory | | :--- | :--- | :--- | | Read signal | PSEN̄ | RD̄ | | Write signal | Not writable | WR̄ | | Pointer | PC | DPTR / R0 / R1 | | Instruction | MOVC | MOV / MOVX | [1 mark] --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **internal RAM structure of 8051**. Show the memory map of the lower 128 bytes including register banks, bit-addressable area, and general-purpose RAM. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** of 8051. Explain the PSW register bit by bit and list the addresses of at least eight major SFRs. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Total Internal RAM = 256 bytes (1 mark)** > 00H–7FH (128 bytes user RAM) + 80H–FFH (128 bytes SFR area) **Step 2 — Lower 128 bytes memory map (4 marks)** | Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose | | :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | | Register Bank 0 | 00H–07H | 8 B | R0–R7 (default active) | | Register Bank 1 | 08H–0FH | 8 B | R0–R7 (alternate) | | Register Bank 2 | 10H–17H | 8 B | R0–R7 (alternate) | | Register Bank 3 | 18H–1FH | 8 B | R0–R7 (alternate) | | Bit-addressable | 20H–2FH | 16 B | 128 individual bits | | General purpose | 30H–7FH | 80 B | User variables / stack | [4 marks] **Step 3 — Bank Selection Mechanism (1 mark)** > RS1:RS0 bits of PSW select the active bank. After reset, RS1:RS0 = 00 → Bank 0 active. **Step 4 — Bit-Addressable Region (1 mark)** > 16 bytes × 8 bits/byte = 128 bits, with bit addresses 00H–7FH in the bit-address space. [1 mark] --- #### Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — SFR Definition (1 mark)** > SFRs are registers that control on-chip peripherals and CPU functions, mapped to addresses 80H–FFH. Only 21 of the 128 addresses are implemented; rest are reserved. **Step 2 — Major SFR Addresses (3 marks)** | SFR | Address | Function | | :--- | :---: | :--- | | ACC | E0H | Accumulator | | B | F0H | B register | | PSW | D0H | Program Status Word | | SP | 81H | Stack Pointer | | DPL/DPH | 82H/83H | Data Pointer | | P0/P1/P2/P3 | 80H/90H/A0H/B0H | Port latches | | TCON | 88H | Timer control | | SCON | 98H | Serial control | | IE | A8H | Interrupt enable | | IP | B8H | Interrupt priority | [3 marks] **Step 3 — PSW Register Bit-by-Bit (3 marks)** [Refer to Section 4.4 Mermaid diagram and Section 2.5 table] - **CY (PSW.7):** Carry from MSB of ALU operation. - **AC (PSW.6):** Auxiliary carry from bit 3 (used in BCD arithmetic). - **F0 (PSW.5):** User-defined general purpose flag. - **RS1:RS0 (PSW.4:PSW.3):** Register bank select. - **OV (PSW.2):** Signed arithmetic overflow. - **PSW.1:** Reserved. - **P (PSW.0):** Parity of accumulator (set if A has odd number of 1s). [3 marks] --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Patterns (Verified from Past Valuation Reports):** > > 1. **Confusing PSW and TCON addresses.** PSW = D0H, TCON = 88H. Mixing these up costs 1–2 marks per occurrence. > 2. **Stating that P0 has internal pull-ups.** It does NOT. External 10 kΩ pull-ups are mandatory. This is a recurring 2-mark deduction. > 3. **Writing "Port 3 has no alternate functions."** Every single P3 pin has an alternate function — this is a textbook trap. > 4. **Drawing the stack growing downward (8085 style).** The 8051 stack grows **upward** — increment-then-store. > 5. **Saying "8051 has 256 bytes of RAM."** It has **128 bytes of user RAM + 128 bytes of SFR area**. The SFR area is not general-purpose RAM. > 6. **Forgetting to mention PSEN̄** when describing program memory access — examiners specifically look for this signal name. > 7. **Confusing DPTR with PC.** PC addresses program memory; DPTR addresses external data memory. They are **independent** 16-bit registers. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Must-Memorize Bullet Points (Print and Stick on Wall):** > > - ✅ 8051 = **8-bit Harvard CMOS** microcontroller, 40-pin DIP, 12 MHz typical clock. > - ✅ **4 KB on-chip ROM, 128 bytes on-chip RAM, 4 I/O ports, 2 timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts.** > - ✅ Machine cycle = **12 oscillator periods** → at 12 MHz crystal, $T_{cy}$ = **1 µs**. > - ✅ **P0 = open-drain** (no pull-up, needs external 10 kΩ); **P1/P2/P3 have internal pull-ups.** > - ✅ **P0 + P2 together** form the **16-bit address bus** for external memory (P0: AD0–AD7, P2: A8–A15). > - ✅ **P3 alternate functions:** P3.0=RxD, P3.1=TxD, P3.2=INT0̄, P3.3=INT1̄, P3.4=T0, P3.5=T1, P3.6=WR̄, P3.7=RD̄. > - ✅ Internal RAM = **256 bytes total** = 128 bytes user (00H–7FH) + 128 bytes SFR (80H–FFH). > - ✅ User RAM split: **Register Banks (00H–1FH, 4 banks × 8 bytes), Bit-Addressable (20H–2FH, 128 bits), General Purpose (30H–7FH, 80 bytes).** > - ✅ **Active register bank** chosen by **PSW bits RS1:RS0**; default after reset = **Bank 0**. > - ✅ **Stack grows upward** (PUSH increments SP first); default SP = **07H**; recommended init = **30H or higher**. > - ✅ **Bit-addressable SFRs** (11 total): P0(80H), TCON(88H), P1(90H), SCON(98H), P2(A0H), IE(A8H), P3(B0H), IP(B8H), PSW(D0H), ACC(E0H), B(F0H). > - ✅ **PSW bits:** CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, —, P. > - ✅ **Program memory = 64 KB** (read via **PSEN̄**); **Data memory = 64 KB external** (read/write via **RD̄/WR̄**, accessed by **MOVX**). > - ✅ **Reset vector = 0000H**; ISR vectors = 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H (each ISR slot = **8 bytes**). > - ✅ **Harvard = separate code/data buses** → true parallel fetch + data access. > - ✅ **Timer reload formula:** `Initial Value = 65,536 − Required Count` (for Mode 1, 16-bit). > - ✅ **Bit address formula:** `(Byte Address − 20H) × 8 + n` (for bytes 20H–2FH). > - ✅ **Mnemonic:** "**MOVC for Code, MOVX for eXternal data, MOV for everything else.**"

topics.title: Instruction Set, Addressing Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set, Addressing Modes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Instruction Set** of a microcontroller is the complete collection of machine-level commands (opcodes) that the processor can decode, interpret, and execute. For the **MCS-51 (8051) family**, the instruction set comprises **111 distinct instructions** that collectively perform data transfer, arithmetic, logical, Boolean, and program-branching operations on 8-bit and 16-bit data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Addressing Mode** is the method by which an instruction specifies the location of its operand(s) — i.e., how the CPU locates the data on which it must operate. The 8051 supports **five (5) fundamental addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, and Indexed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Recipe & Pantry" Model

Imagine you are writing a **cooking recipe** (the *instruction*):

| Recipe Element | Microcontroller Equivalent |
|---|---|
| The verb ("chop", "boil") | **Opcode** (operation to perform) |
| The ingredient name | **Operand** (data involved) |
| The storage location of the ingredient | **Addressing Mode** (how operand is referenced) |

> **Example analogy:** Saying *"add 2 spoons of sugar"* is **immediate addressing** (the value 2 is given directly). Saying *"add sugar from the top shelf, third jar"* is **direct addressing** (a specific location is named). Saying *"add sugar from the jar in your hand"* is **register indirect addressing** (a pointer points to the actual data).

## 1.3 Instruction Size & Encoding in 8051

The 8051 uses a **variable-length instruction format** of **1, 2, or 3 bytes**:

| Byte Count | Composition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **1-byte** | Opcode only (op & operand encoded together) | `INC A`, `NOP` |
| **2-byte** | Opcode + 8-bit operand | `ADD A, #45H` (without 8H) |
| **3-byte** | Opcode + 16-bit address/data | `MOV A, #0FEH`, `LJMP 2000H` |

> [!NOTE]
> Most 8051 instructions are **1 or 2 bytes**, with only a small subset (long jumps, long calls, and 16-bit immediate data moves) consuming **3 bytes**. Program memory is byte-addressable, so 8051 uses a **von Neumann architecture with separate address spaces** (Harvard-style) where code and data memory are physically separated but share the same address bus externally.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of an 8051 Instruction

A generic 8051 instruction is structured as:

```
[ OPCODE BYTE ]   [ OPERAND BYTE 1 ]   [ OPERAND BYTE 2 ]
       ↑                    ↑                      ↑
  Operation code     Operand (low)         Operand (high)
  (1 byte)           (1 byte)              (1 byte, if needed)
```

- **Opcode byte**: Defines what operation to perform (e.g., ADD, MOV, JNZ).
- **Operand byte(s)**: Define the source/destination of data, encoded based on the addressing mode.

## 2.2 The Five Addressing Modes — Logical Breakdown

### Mode 1: Immediate Addressing (`#data`)
- **Logic:** Operand value is *embedded literally* inside the instruction (constant).
- **Storage:** The 8-bit or 16-bit constant follows the opcode in program memory.
- **Symbol:** Prefix **`#`** is mandatory to distinguish from an address.
- **Example:** `MOV A, #25H` → Load the constant `0x25` into accumulator.

### Mode 2: Register Addressing
- **Logic:** Operand resides *inside a CPU register* (R0–R7, A, B, DPTR, C).
- **No memory fetch** is required; encoded in the opcode itself.
- **Example:** `ADD A, R3` → Add contents of register R3 to A.

### Mode 3: Direct Addressing
- **Logic:** Operand specifies the **direct internal RAM address (00H–7FH)** or **SFR address (80H–FFH)**.
- **Storage:** Instruction contains the 8-bit address of the data.
- **Example:** `MOV A, 40H` → Copy contents of internal RAM at address `0x40` into A.

### Mode 4: Register Indirect Addressing
- **Logic:** Operand gives a **pointer register (R0, R1, DPTR, or SP)** that *holds the address* of the actual data.
- **Symbol:** Use the **`@`** prefix to indicate "contents at address".
- **Example:** `MOV A, @R0` → Copy the byte pointed to by R0 into A.
- **Pointer rules:** `R0/R1` → 8-bit address (internal RAM only); `@DPTR` → 16-bit (external data memory).

### Mode 5: Indexed Addressing (Code Memory Only)
- **Logic:** Effective address = **`[PC or DPTR] + [A (offset)]`**. Used exclusively for **look-up tables (LUTs)** in program memory.
- **Instructions:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` and `MOVC A, @A+PC`.
- **Example:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` → Read code-memory byte at address `(DPTR + A)`.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it appears in nearly every KTU Module 1 question paper.**

| # | Addressing Mode | Syntax | Operand Location | Example | Size |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Immediate** | `Mnemonic #data` | Embedded in instruction | `MOV A, #30H` | 2 B |
| 2 | **Register** | `Mnemonic Rn` | CPU register (R0–R7, A, B) | `ADD A, R4` | 1 B |
| 3 | **Direct** | `Mnemonic addr` | Internal RAM / SFR | `MOV 30H, A` | 2–3 B |
| 4 | **Register Indirect** | `Mnemonic @Ri / @DPTR` | Memory pointed by Ri/DPTR | `MOVX @DPTR, A` | 1–2 B |
| 5 | **Indexed** | `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` | Code memory (PC/DPTR+A) | `MOVC A, @A+PC` | 1 B |

### Key Boundary Constants

| Parameter | Value / Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Total 8051 instructions | **111** | — |
| Instruction sizes | **1, 2, or 3** | bytes |
| Opcode length | **8** | bits |
| Internal RAM (direct addressing) | **00H – 7FH (128 B)** | bytes |
| SFR region (direct addressing) | **80H – FFH (128 B)** | bytes |
| Rn registers | **R0 – R7 (4 banks × 8 = 32)** | registers |
| 16-bit pointer registers | **DPTR (DPL+DPH)** | bits |
| Program counter reset | **0000H** | — |
| Maximum program memory | **64 KB (0000H – FFFFH)** | bytes |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Firmware Design:** Choosing the *fastest/shortest* addressing mode for a given task reduces code size and improves deterministic execution — critical in **real-time systems** (automotive ECUs, industrial PLCs).
- **Compiler Code Generation:** The Keil C51 compiler maps C variables to one of the 5 modes based on storage class (`data`, `idata`, `pdata`, `xdata`, `code`).
- **Memory-Mapped I/O:** Direct addressing is the only way to access the **SFRs** (P0, P1, TCON, SCON, etc.) that control peripherals.
- **Look-up Tables (LUTs):** Indexed addressing enables fast retrieval of pre-computed values (e.g., sine-wave tables for DTMF generation, character fonts in LCD drivers).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Walk-Through — Effective Address Calculation

The **Effective Address (EA)** is the actual memory location that the CPU will read from / write to. For each mode, EA is derived differently.

### Mode 1: Immediate Addressing — EA Derivation

> **Example:** `MOV A, #3FH`
> - Step 1: CPU fetches opcode byte `74H` (Mnemonic: MOV A, #data).
> - Step 2: CPU fetches operand byte `3FH` (the immediate data).
> - Step 3: EA is **not a memory location** — the operand IS the data.

$$
\text{Data} = \text{Instruction}_2 = 0x3F
$$

### Mode 2: Register Addressing — EA Derivation

> **Example:** `ADD A, R5`
> - Step 1: Fetch opcode `2D` (the lowest 3 bits `101` select R5).
> - Step 2: The **register file itself is the operand**; no address bus activity.
> - Step 3: EA = R5 (Register Set, current bank).

$$
\text{EA} = R5_{\text{physical}}
$$

### Mode 3: Direct Addressing — EA Derivation

> **Example:** `MOV A, 60H`
> - Step 1: Fetch opcode `E5H`.
> - Step 2: Fetch operand byte `60H` (this IS the address).
> - Step 3: Read internal RAM location `0x60`; copy to A.

$$
\text{EA} = 0x60
$$

$$
A \leftarrow M[0x60]
$$

### Mode 4: Register Indirect — EA Derivation (CRITICAL FOR KTU)

> **Example:** `MOVX A, @DPTR`  *(read external data memory)*
> - Step 1: Fetch opcode `E0H`.
> - Step 2: Use DPTR (DPL + DPH) as a 16-bit address bus.
> - Step 3: EA = DPTR.

$$
\text{EA} = \text{DPTR} = (\text{DPH} \ll 8) \ \vert\ \text{DPL}
$$

> **Example 2:** `MOV A, @R0`  *(read internal RAM)*
> - Step 1: Fetch opcode `E2H` (or `E3H` for `@R1`).
> - Step 2: R0 is an 8-bit pointer; valid range = 00H–7FH.
> - Step 3: EA = (R0).

$$
\text{EA} = (R0) \quad \text{where } 0x00 \leq (R0) \leq 0x7F
$$

### Mode 5: Indexed Addressing — EA Derivation (HIGHEST MARX WEIGHTAGE)

> **Example:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`
> - Step 1: Fetch opcode `E4H`.
> - Step 2: The accumulator A holds an 8-bit offset (0–255).
> - Step 3: DPTR holds the 16-bit base address of the LUT.
> - Step 4: EA = (DPTR) + (A), as a 16-bit addition.

$$
\text{EA} = (DPTR) + (A) = (DPH \ll 8) \ \vert\ \text{DPL} + A
$$

$$
A \leftarrow \text{CodeMemory}[\text{EA}]
$$

> **Example 2:** `MOVC A, @A+PC` (PC-relative)
> - Step 1: PC has already been incremented past the opcode when the next byte is fetched.
> - Step 2: EA = (PC) + (A).

$$
\text{EA} = (PC)_{\text{current}} + (A)
$$

## 3.2 Comparative Analytical Matrix — Speed vs. Memory Footprint

| Mode | Operand Fetch Cycles | Code Size | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| Immediate | 1 | 2 B | Constants, initialization |
| Register | 0 (in opcode) | 1 B | Loops, fast arithmetic |
| Direct | 1 | 2 B | SFR & global variable access |
| Register Indirect | 1 | 1 B | Array/table traversal in RAM |
| Indexed | 1 (16-bit add) | 1 B | LUT in code memory |

## 3.3 Worked Example — Identifying Addressing Mode in a KTU Exam

> **Problem:** Identify the addressing mode of each instruction and state the effective address or value loaded.
> 1. `MOV R3, #55H`
> 2. `MOV A, 70H`
> 3. `MOV A, @R1`
> 4. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`
> 5. `INC R4`

### Step-by-Step Solution

**1. `MOV R3, #55H`**
- The `#` symbol → **Immediate addressing**.
- Value `55H` is loaded into R3 directly from the instruction stream.

**2. `MOV A, 70H`**
- No `#`, no `@`, no `Rn` → **Direct addressing**.
- EA = `0x70`; copy contents of internal RAM location `70H` into A.

**3. `MOV A, @R1`**
- `@` symbol → **Register indirect addressing**.
- EA = (R1); copy contents of RAM at address stored in R1 into A.

**4. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`**
- The `C` suffix indicates code memory; `@A+...` → **Indexed addressing**.
- EA = (DPTR) + (A); read code-memory byte at this 16-bit address.

**5. `INC R4`**
- Operates on a register directly → **Register addressing**.
- No address bus is used; R4 ← R4 + 1.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 CPU Instruction Execution Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Program Counter PC] --> node2[Fetch Opcode from Code Memory]
    node2 --> node3[Decode Opcode]
    node3 --> node4{Addressing Mode?}
    node4 -->|Immediate| node5[Fetch Operand Byte from Code Memory]
    node4 -->|Register| node6[Read from Rn / A / B / DPTR]
    node4 -->|Direct| node7[Fetch 8-bit Address from Code Memory]
    node4 -->|Register Indirect| node8[Use Ri or DPTR as Pointer]
    node4 -->|Indexed| node9[Compute EA = DPTR or PC plus A]
    node5 --> node10[Execute Operation in ALU]
    node6 --> node10
    node7 --> node11[Read Write Internal RAM or SFR]
    node8 --> node11
    node9 --> node12[Read Code Memory LUT]
    node11 --> node10
    node12 --> node10
    node10 --> node13[Update Flags PC and Registers]
    node13 --> node14[Next Instruction Cycle]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Decision Tree — Choosing the Correct Addressing Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[New Instruction] --> q1{Operand starts with hash?}
    q1 -->|Yes| m1[IMMEDIATE addressing]
    q1 -->|No| q2{Operand starts with at symbol?}
    q2 -->|Yes| m2[REGISTER INDIRECT addressing]
    q2 -->|No| q3{Operand is R0 to R7 A or B?}
    q3 -->|Yes| m3[REGISTER addressing]
    q3 -->|No| q4{Instruction is MOVC?}
    q4 -->|Yes| m4[INDEXED addressing]
    q4 -->|No| m5[DIRECT addressing]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Memory Map of 8051 Addressing Regions

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CODE[Program Memory 64KB - MOVC only]
        c1[0000H - Restart Vector]
        c2[0003H - External Int 0]
        c3[000BH - Timer 0]
        c4[0013H - External Int 1]
        c5[001BH - Timer 1]
        c6[0023H - Serial Port]
        c7[0030H onwards - User Code and LUT]
    end

    subgraph IRAM[Internal RAM 128 Bytes]
        i1[00H - 1FH - 4 Register Banks]
        i2[20H - 2FH - Bit Addressable]
        i3[30H - 7FH - General Purpose]
    end

    subgraph SFR[SFR Region 80H - FFH - Direct Addressing only]
        s1[80H - P0 Port]
        s2[82H - DPL]
        s3[83H - DPH]
        s4[88H - TCON]
        s5[A0H - P2 Port]
        s6[E0H - A Accumulator]
    end
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Instruction Pipeline Phases

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Phase 1: FETCH] --> stepB[Phase 2: DECODE]
    stepB --> stepC[Phase 3: OPERAND READ]
    stepC --> stepD[Phase 4: EXECUTE in ALU]
    stepD --> stepE[Phase 5: WRITE BACK to Destination]
    stepE --> stepF[Phase 6: PC INCREMENT and Loop]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
>
> **Q1.** What is an addressing mode? List the addressing modes supported by the 8051 microcontroller.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
An *addressing mode* is the method by which an instruction specifies the location of its operand(s) — i.e., the mechanism used by the CPU to locate the data on which it operates.
The 8051 supports **five addressing modes**:
1. Immediate Addressing
2. Register Addressing
3. Direct Addressing
4. Register Indirect Addressing
5. Indexed Addressing

*(1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing all five.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
>
> **Q2.** Differentiate between **Direct Addressing** and **Register Indirect Addressing** in 8051. Give one example of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Direct Addressing | Register Indirect Addressing |
|---|---|---|
| Address source | 8-bit address is part of instruction | Pointer register (R0/R1/DPTR) holds address |
| Symbol | None | `@` |
| Address range | 00H–7FH (RAM), 80H–FFH (SFR) | 8-bit: 00H–FFH (with R0/R1); 16-bit (with DPTR) |
| Memory type | Internal RAM + SFRs | Internal/External RAM |
| Example | `MOV A, 50H` | `MOVX A, @DPTR` |

*(1 mark per correct point of difference + 1 mark for examples.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU ESE – Model Question Paper, Module 1, Choice A]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Explain the **five addressing modes** of the 8051 microcontroller with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 7-segment display lookup table is stored in code memory starting at address `0200H`. Write an 8051 assembly program to display digit `6` on the display using **indexed addressing**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

*(For full marks, present each mode as: name → syntax → operand location → example → cycle count.)*

1. **Immediate Addressing** — The operand is a constant embedded in the instruction. Indicated by `#`. Example: `MOV A, #25H` loads `25H` into A. Code size: 2 bytes.

2. **Register Addressing** — The operand is in one of the 8051's internal registers (R0–R7, A, B, DPTR). Encoded in the opcode. Example: `ADD A, R3` adds R3 to A. Code size: 1 byte (fastest).

3. **Direct Addressing** — The instruction contains the **8-bit address** of the operand in internal RAM (00H–7FH) or SFR (80H–FFH). Example: `MOV A, 40H` reads RAM[40H] into A. Code size: 2 bytes.

4. **Register Indirect Addressing** — A register (R0, R1, DPTR) acts as a pointer to the operand. Indicated by `@`. Example: `MOV A, @R0` reads the byte at the address held in R0. Useful for arrays.

5. **Indexed Addressing** — Effective address = (DPTR or PC) + A. Used only with `MOVC` to access code-memory LUTs. Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` reads the byte at DPTR+A.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     DPTR, #0200H   ; DPTR ← base address of LUT
        MOV     A,    #06H     ; A ← digit index (6)
        MOVC    A,    @A+DPTR  ; A ← segment code for 6
        MOV     P1,   A        ; Output to Port 1 (display)
HERE:   SJMP    HERE           ; Infinite loop
        END
```

**Explanation (stepwise for KTU valuation):**
1. `MOV DPTR, #0200H` — Loads the 16-bit base address of the LUT using immediate-to-DPTR (3-byte instruction).
2. `MOV A, #06H` — Places offset `6` into the accumulator.
3. `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — Indexed addressing; EA = 0200H + 06H = 0206H; reads the segment code.
4. `MOV P1, A` — Sends the pattern to Port 1 (direct addressing; P1 SFR = 90H).
5. `SJMP HERE` — Halts the program (infinite loop).

> **Memory map of LUT (assumed):**
> 0200H: 0x3F (0), 0201H: 0x06 (1), 0202H: 0x5B (2), 0203H: 0x4F (3), 0204H: 0x66 (4), 0205H: 0x6D (5), **0206H: 0x7D (6)**, 0207H: 0x07 (7), ...

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **internal RAM organization** of the 8051. Which addressing mode(s) can access the bit-addressable region (20H–2FH)? *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program to copy a block of **10 bytes** from internal RAM starting at `30H` to internal RAM starting at `60H` using **register indirect addressing**. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

**Internal RAM (128 bytes) layout:**

| Range | Region | Addressing Modes |
|---|---|---|
| 00H – 1FH | 32 bytes → 4 banks × 8 registers (R0–R7) | Register, Register Indirect, Direct |
| 20H – 2FH | 16 bytes (128 bits) → Bit-addressable | Direct, Register Indirect |
| 30H – 7FH | 80 bytes → General-purpose RAM | Direct, Register Indirect |

- **Register addressing** accesses only the 32 byte locations (00H–1FH).
- **Bit-addressable region (20H–2FH):** can be accessed using *Direct addressing* (byte-level) or specific bit-manipulation instructions like `SETB 07H` (Direct-bit), `CLR 2FH.7`.
- **Register indirect** with R0/R1 can also access 20H–2FH in byte form.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        MOV     R0,    #30H     ; Source pointer
        MOV     R1,    #60H     ; Destination pointer
        MOV     R2,    #0AH     ; Counter = 10
LOOP:   MOV     A,     @R0      ; A ← M[R0]    (register indirect)
        MOV     @R1,   A        ; M[R1] ← A    (register indirect)
        INC     R0              ; Source++
        INC     R1              ; Dest++
        DJNZ    R2,    LOOP     ; Decrement R2, jump if ≠ 0
STOP:   SJMP    STOP
        END
```

**Stepwise explanation:**
- R0, R1 used as **pointers** (register indirect mode, indicated by `@`).
- R2 acts as a counter; `DJNZ` (Decrement and Jump if Not Zero) is a **loop control** instruction that uses register addressing implicitly.
- Each iteration copies one byte, increments both pointers, and decrements the counter.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the `#` symbol in immediate addressing** — writing `MOV A, 30H` instead of `MOV A, #30H` changes the *meaning* from "load constant 30H" to "load contents of RAM[30H]". A 1-mark penalty per occurrence.
> 2. **Confusing `@R0` with `R0`** — `@R0` means "memory at address in R0"; `R0` is a register. Examiners check the `@` symbol carefully.
> 3. **Wrong pointer width** — R0/R1 are 8-bit (max address FFH); DPTR is 16-bit. Using `@R0` for external memory access is a **compile/runtime error** in 8051.
> 4. **MOVC vs. MOVX** — `MOVC` reads **code memory** (for LUTs); `MOVX` reads **external data memory**. Mixing them up loses 2–3 marks instantly.
> 5. **Indexed addressing operand order** — Always `@A+DPTR` (A is the **offset**); reversing to `@DPTR+A` is **syntactically wrong** in 8051 assembly.
> 6. **Not specifying SFR addresses** — For P1 = 90H, TCON = 88H, etc., examiners expect the **hex address** to be mentioned for full credit.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Instruction Set & Addressing Modes**

- ✅ 8051 has **111 instructions**, all **1, 2, or 3 bytes** long, executed in **1, 2, or 4 machine cycles**.
- ✅ There are **5 addressing modes**: Immediate, Register, Direct, Register Indirect, Indexed.
- ✅ Immediate mode uses **`#`**, Register Indirect uses **`@`**, Indexed is only for `MOVC`.
- ✅ **Register addressing** is the fastest (no memory access); **Indexed** is the most powerful (16-bit LUT access).
- ✅ **Direct addressing** is the *only* way to access SFRs (P0, P1, TCON, SCON, IE, IP, etc.).
- ✅ `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` is the standard idiom for **look-up table** access in code memory.
- ✅ `MOVX` family is used for **external data memory**; `MOV` family is for **internal RAM + SFRs**.
- ✅ **DPTR** is the only 16-bit pointer usable in register indirect mode for 16-bit external addresses.
- ✅ Internal RAM regions: `00H–1FH` (registers), `20H–2FH` (bit-addressable), `30H–7FH` (general purpose).
- ✅ **Flag bits** (CY, AC, OV, P) are updated **only by arithmetic/logic instructions**, not by data-transfer instructions.
- ✅ For KTU numerical problems: always show the **EA calculation** (e.g., EA = DPTR + A) — step-marks are awarded for this.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Instruction Set, Addressing Modes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Simple Assembly Language Programs: Arithmetic Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division), Block Data Transfer from One Internal Memory Location to Another
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Simple Assembly Language Programs: Arithmetic Operations (Addition, Subtraction, Multiplication, Division), Block Data Transfer from One Internal Memory Location to Another

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Assembly Language Programming** for the **Intel 8086 microprocessor** is the process of writing low-level symbolic instructions (mnemonics) that map directly to the machine's native **Instruction Set Architecture (ISA)**. In the KTU 2024 scheme, *Simple Assembly Language Programs* refer to sequential, non-interrupt-driven programs that perform **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU)** operations or **Data Movement** tasks using the **General Purpose Registers (GPRs)** of the 8086.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 Definition Box**
> A *Simple Assembly Language Program* is a linear sequence of 8086 mnemonics (using MASM/TASM syntax) that performs a single computational task — such as addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, or block movement — without the use of subroutines, macros, or hardware interrupts. All operands are typically declared in the `.DATA` segment and accessed via **direct**, **register**, or **register-indirect addressing modes**.

The **Arithmetic Instruction Set** of the 8086 consists of the following primary mnemonics:

- **ADD** — Unsigned/Signed binary addition
