|-----------|-----------------|------|-------|
| Interrupt Vector Size | $2^5 = 32$ bytes per vector (0000H–0023H) | bytes | Each ISR is allotted 8 bytes |
| Minimum Interrupt Latency | $3 \text{ to } 7$ | machine cycles | 8051 specific |
| Machine Cycle Duration | $T_{MC} = 12 \cdot T_{osc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | For 11.0592 MHz: 1.085 µs |
| Stack Growth Direction | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP} + 1$ | — | Grows *upward* from 07H |
| RETI Opcode | $0x32$ | hex | 2 bytes, 2 cycles |
| Two Priority Levels | $2^1 = 2$ | levels | High and Low |
| Max Simultaneous Interrupts | $5$ (excluding Reset) | — | Per 8051 core |
| IE Reset Value | $0x00$ | hex | All disabled |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interrupt-driven programming is the backbone of **real-time embedded systems**:
- **Automotive ECUs**: ABS sensor interrupts trigger braking algorithms.
- **IoT Devices**: UART receive interrupts handle asynchronous data without CPU busy-waiting.
- **Medical Pacemakers**: Hardware timer interrupts ensure precise millisecond timing.
- **Industrial PLCs**: External interrupts from limit switches halt motors in microseconds.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Without interrupts, embedded systems would be sluggish, power-hungry, and unable to handle multiple I/O events — making interrupt mastery a **core competency** for any KTU-validated embedded engineer.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Interrupt Latency Calculation

The **interrupt latency** $L$ is the total time from the moment an interrupt flag is set to the moment the first instruction of the ISR begins execution. Let us derive this formally.

### Given:
- Crystal frequency: $f_{osc}$ (typically **11.0592 MHz** in KTU lab kits)
- The longest instruction being executed when the interrupt occurs: an 8-bit division (`DIV AB`) which takes **4 machine cycles**

### Step 1: Define the machine cycle period

By the 8051 architecture specification, one machine cycle equals **12 oscillator periods**:

$$
T_{MC} = 12 \cdot T_{osc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}
$$

Substituting $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz} = 11.0592 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$:

$$
T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 2: Account for instruction completion

The CPU completes the *current* instruction before sampling interrupts. Worst case = **4 machine cycles** for `DIV AB`:

$$
T_{\text{inst, max}} = 4 \cdot T_{MC} = 4 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 4.34 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 3: Account for interrupt sampling and response

The 8051 samples interrupts in the **last cycle** of each instruction. Hardware response adds a fixed overhead of **2 machine cycles** for the `LCALL` to the vector:

$$
T_{\text{response}} = 2 \cdot T_{MC} = 2 \times 1.085 \, \mu s = 2.17 \, \mu s
$$

### Step 4: Total latency (worst case)

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{\text{max}} &= T_{\text{inst, max}} + T_{\text{response}} \\
&= 4.34 \, \mu s + 2.17 \, \mu s \\
&= 6.51 \, \mu s
\end{aligned}
$$

In general form:

$$
L_{\text{max}} = \left( N_{\text{inst}} + 2 \right) \cdot \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}} \quad \text{[seconds]}
$$

where $N_{\text{inst}}$ is the machine cycle count of the longest instruction in execution (1, 2, or 4).

## 3.2 Derivation: Timer Reload Value for a Given Delay Using Interrupts

Suppose we need an interrupt to fire every **50 ms** using Timer 0 in **Mode 1 (16-bit)** with an 11.0592 MHz crystal.

### Step 1: Compute the desired timer tick count

The timer must overflow every 50 ms. Timer increments every machine cycle:

$$
T_{tick} = T_{MC} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

Number of timer ticks required:

$$
N_{ticks} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46082 \text{ ticks}
$$

### Step 2: Compute initial timer value

Mode 1 overflows at 65536 ($= 2^{16}$). The initial value (TH0:TL0) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Timer}_{\text{initial}} &= 65536 - N_{ticks} \\
&= 65536 - 46082 \\
&= 19454
\end{aligned}
$$

Convert to hex: $19454 = 0x4BFE$, so **TH0 = 0x4C** and **TL0 = 0xFE**.

### Step 3: Verify reload calculation in the ISR

Inside the ISR, we must reload this value to maintain periodic 50 ms interrupts. This shows the **deterministic, calculable nature** of interrupt-based timing.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Polling vs Interrupt

| Aspect | Polling | Interrupt |
|--------|---------|-----------|
| CPU utilization | Low (wastes cycles checking flags) | High (CPU sleeps until event) |
| Response time | Depends on loop position | Bounded by latency (µs) |
| Code complexity | Simple loop | Requires ISR + vector setup |
| Power consumption | High (CPU always active) | Low (CPU can idle in `PCON`) |
| Multi-source handling | Sequential, slow | Hardware-arbitrated, parallel |
| Determinism | Variable | Predictable (for fixed priority) |
| Suitability for real-time | Poor | Excellent |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024 Scheme questions asking "justify why interrupts are preferred over polling in real-time systems", cite **CPU efficiency**, **bounded latency**, and **power savings** as the three pillars.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: 8051 Interrupt Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Interrupt Sources] --> B[Edge Detection and Flag Logic]
    B --> C[Interrupt Enable Register IE]
    C --> D{Master Switch EA equals 1}
    D -- No --> Z[Interrupt Ignored Continue Main]
    D -- Yes --> E[Priority Comparator with IP Register]
    E --> F[Highest Priority Request Selected]
    F --> G[Push PC onto Stack]
    G --> H[Load Vector Address into PC]
    H --> I[Execute ISR Code at Vector]
    I --> J{RETI Instruction Encountered}
    J -- Yes --> K[Pop PC from Stack]
    K --> L[Resume Main Program]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Interrupt Vector Table Layout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[0000H Reset Entry] --> A1[0003H External Int 0 ISR]
    A1 --> A2[000BH Timer 0 Overflow ISR]
    A2 --> A3[0013H External Int 1 ISR]
    A3 --> A4[001BH Timer 1 Overflow ISR]
    A4 --> A5[0023H Serial Port ISR]
    A5 --> A6[002BH Onward User Program Space]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid State Diagram: Interrupt Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> MainExecution
    MainExecution --> FlagSet: Hardware Sets Flag
    FlagSet --> CheckEnable: CPU Samples Flag
    CheckEnable --> Ignored: Enable equals 0
    CheckEnable --> CheckPriority: Enable equals 1
    Ignored --> MainExecution
    CheckPriority --> PushPC: Priority Resolved
    PushPC --> ISR_Active: Vector Loaded
    ISR_Active --> MainExecution: RETI Executed
    ISR_Active --> HigherISR: Higher Priority Interrupt
    HigherISR --> ISR_Active: Lower Priority Resumes
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Interrupt Programming Template (Embedded C Style)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Program Start] --> Init[Initialize SFRs EA IE IP TCON TMOD]
    Init --> Loop[Main While 1 Loop]
    Loop --> Wait[Idle or Background Task]
    Wait --> Wait
    Wait -.->|Interrupt Fires| ISR1[ISR Vector 000BH Timer 0]
    ISR1 --> Reload[Reload TH0 TL0]
    Reload --> Toggle[Toggle Output Pin LED]
    Toggle --> Reti[RETI Instruction]
    Reti --> Wait
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Fallback Note**: For complex physical pin-level schematics of the 8051 interrupt pins (P3.2/INT0, P3.3/INT1), the architectural flow diagrams above abstract the *functional behavior* required for KTU 2024 Scheme paper analysis. Physical pinout diagrams are not required for Module 2 examination questions.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define an interrupt. List the six interrupt sources of the 8051 microcontroller along with their vector addresses.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (1 mark):** An interrupt is an asynchronous event that suspends the main program execution, directing the CPU to execute a dedicated Interrupt Service Routine (ISR). Upon completion (via `RETI`), the CPU resumes the original program.

**List (2 marks):**

| Source | Vector Address |
|--------|----------------|
| Reset | 0000H |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | 0003H |
| Timer 0 Overflow | 000BH |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | 0013H |
| Timer 1 Overflow | 001BH |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | 0023H |

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the EA bit in the IE register. What happens if EA = 0?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EA (IE.7)** is the *Global Interrupt Enable* or *Master Switch* bit of the 8051. It is analogous to the main circuit breaker of a building.

- If **EA = 1**: Individual interrupts enabled via EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES are *permitted* to fire.
- If **EA = 0**: **All interrupts are disabled**, irrespective of the state of individual enable bits. The CPU ignores every interrupt request, and the system must rely on polling.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**
**(a)** Explain the interrupt structure of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. Discuss the function of the IE and IP registers in detail. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Differentiate between edge-triggered and level-triggered external interrupts. Write the sequence of steps that occur inside the CPU when an interrupt is recognized. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Interrupt Structure Explanation (7 marks):**

The 8051 supports **six interrupt sources** (5 maskable + Reset). The interrupt structure consists of:

1. **Interrupt Sources** (hardware blocks): INT0, INT1, T0, T1, Serial port.
2. **Flag Bits** in TCON (IE0, TF0, IE1, TF1) and SCON (TI, RI).
3. **Interrupt Enable Register (IE)** at SFR address **0xA8**: Bits EA, ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0 control whether each source can request service.
4. **Interrupt Priority Register (IP)** at SFR address **0xB8**: Bits PS, PT1, PX1, PT0, PX0 assign high/low priority.
5. **Interrupt Logic**: Compares flags, enables, and priorities; arbitrates and generates `LCALL` to the correct vector.

**Function of IE Register (3 marks):** The IE register acts as the *permission switchboard*. Each bit corresponds to one interrupt source. The master bit **EA** must be 1 for any interrupts to function. Setting, for example, `IE = 0x82` enables Timer 0 only.

**Function of IP Register (2 marks):** The IP register assigns priority (high=1, low=0). If two interrupts of the same priority occur simultaneously, the *natural hardware priority* (INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial) determines order.

**(b) Edge vs Level Triggering + Sequence (7 marks):**

**Edge-Triggered (ITx = 1):**
- The interrupt is recognized on the **falling edge** (HIGH-to-LOW transition) of the INTx pin.
- The flag (IEx) is **latched** and remains set even if the pin returns HIGH.
- The flag **must be cleared in software** within the ISR.

**Level-Triggered (ITx = 0):**
- The interrupt is recognized as long as INTx is **LOW**.
- The pin must be **held LOW until the CPU services** the interrupt.
- The flag is *not* latched; it follows the pin state.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Edge-Triggered | Level-Triggered |
|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| Activation | Falling edge | Low level |
| Latching | Yes (must clear in software) | No |
| Pin requirement | Pulse (transient) | Sustained LOW |
| Use case | Short events, switches | Wired-OR, shared lines |

**Interrupt Recognition Sequence (3 marks):**
1. Hardware sets the corresponding flag bit.
2. CPU samples flags in the last cycle of every instruction.
3. IE and IP are checked; arbitration occurs.
4. Current PC is pushed onto stack.
5. PC is loaded with the vector address.
6. ISR executes.
7. `RETI` instruction pops PC and resumes main.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**
**(a)** With a neat timing diagram, explain interrupt latency. Calculate the maximum latency for an 8051 system using a 12 MHz crystal, assuming the longest instruction (4 machine cycles) is in execution. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a 11.0592 MHz crystal. Design a Timer 0 interrupt that generates an output pulse on P1.0 every **exactly 50 ms**. Show all SFR initialization, vector placement, and reload calculations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Interrupt Latency (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** Interrupt latency is the time between setting of an interrupt flag and the execution of the first instruction of the ISR.

**Timing Diagram Description (2 marks):**
- Flag set at $t_0$
- CPU completes current instruction by $t_0 + T_{inst}$
- LCALL overhead 2 cycles by $t_0 + T_{inst} + 2T_{MC}$
- First ISR instruction executes at this point.

**Calculation (3 marks):** For 12 MHz crystal:

$$
T_{MC} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \, \mu s
$$

$$
L_{\text{max}} = (4 + 2) \times 1 \, \mu s = 6 \, \mu s
$$

**(b) Timer 0 Design for 50 ms (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Choose Mode:** Mode 1 (16-bit) for full 65536 range (2 marks).

**Step 2 — Compute timer ticks (1 mark):**

$$
T_{tick} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

$$
N_{ticks} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 46082
$$

**Step 3 — Initial value (1 mark):**

$$
\text{TH0:TL0} = 65536 - 46082 = 19454 = 0x4BFE
$$

So **TH0 = 0x4C**, **TL0 = 0xFE**.

**Step 4 — SFR initialization (2 marks):**
- `TMOD = 0x01` (Timer 0, Mode 1, software control)
- `TH0 = 0x4C; TL0 = 0xFE;`
- `TR0 = 1;` (start timer)
- `IE = 0x82;` (EA=1, ET0=1)
- `IP = 0x02;` (PT0=1 for high priority)

**Step 5 — ISR (1 mark):**
- Reload TH0 and TL0 with 0x4CFE
- Toggle P1.0
- Return via RETI

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 inside the ISR** is the #1 mistake — Mode 1 does *not* auto-reload. Students lose 2–3 marks here every exam cycle.
> 2. **Incorrect crystal frequency** (e.g., using 12 MHz instead of 11.0592 MHz in the lab) gives non-integer baud-rate divisions — KTU 2024 questions explicitly test whether you spot this.
> 3. **Not setting EA = 1** — without it, even perfectly initialized individual enables will not fire.
> 4. **Confusing IT0/IT1 polarity** — `IT0 = 1` means *edge*, not *level*; this traps many students.
> 5. **Forgetting to clear edge-triggered flags** in ISRs (IEx bits must be software-cleared, unlike timer flags which auto-clear on vector fetch).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-density rapid-revision checklist for KTU 2024 ESE:**

- ⭐ 8051 has **6 interrupt sources**: Reset, INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial.
- ⭐ Vector addresses: **0000H, 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H** (each 8 bytes apart).
- ⭐ **EA (IE.7)** is the global master switch; individual enables are EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES.
- ⭐ **IT0/IT1 = 1** → edge-triggered; **= 0** → level-triggered.
- ⭐ **IP register** defines high/low priority; ties broken by *natural order*: INT0 → T0 → INT1 → T1 → Serial.
- ⭐ **RETI opcode is 0x32**; it pops PC *and* signals ISR end.
- ⭐ Latency formula: $L_{\text{max}} = (N_{\text{inst}} + 2) \cdot \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ seconds.
- ⭐ Timer in **Mode 1 requires manual reload** inside the ISR; Mode 2 auto-reloads 8-bit.
- ⭐ Interrupt flags for timers (TF0, TF1) auto-clear when vector is fetched; edge flags (IE0, IE1) do **not** — must be cleared by software.
- ⭐ Stack grows **upward** from internal RAM address 07H; PC is pushed (2 bytes) on interrupt entry.
- ⭐ Polling wastes CPU cycles; interrupts enable real-time, event-driven, power-efficient design.
- ⭐ For 11.0592 MHz crystal: $T_{MC} \approx 1.085 \, \mu s$ (use this for all KTU exam calculations).
- ⭐ Always end ISRs with **RETI**, not RET — RET does not signal interrupt completion and breaks nested interrupt logic.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interfacing of Peripherals: LED (ALP and Embedded C Programming), OLED, Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Interfacing of Peripherals: LED (ALP and Embedded C Programming), OLED, Sensors # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Peripherals in Embedded Systems — Formal Definition In the context of the **APJ Abdul Kalam Technological University (KTU) 2024 Scheme** syllabus for *Embedded Systems and Microcontrollers*, **peripherals** are defined as the external hardware devices that communicate with a microcontroller unit (MCU) through General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins or specialized bus protocols (I²C, SPI, UART, ADC). They expand the computational core's ability to sense, actuate, and display real-world phenomena. The three peripheral families covered in this module are: 1. **Actuators (Output Peripherals):** LEDs, OLED Displays 2. **Sensors (Input Peripherals):** Temperature, Light, Proximity, Humidity sensors 3. **Programming Paradigms:** Assembly Language Programming (ALP) and Embedded C > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The emphasis is on **bare-metal programming** (register-level access) rather than HAL/Library abstraction, as the course outcome demands understanding of the underlying hardware-software contract. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Think of a microcontroller as the **human brain**: - **LEDs** are like the *mouth and hands* — the brain sends signals outward to produce visible action (light, motion). - **OLED Displays** are like a *television screen* — a sophisticated output that conveys complex visual information. - **Sensors** are like the *five senses* (eyes, ears, skin) — they gather raw data from the environment and feed it back to the brain for processing. The **GPIO pin** is the **nerve fiber** connecting the brain to the limbs. Just as a nerve carries either an electrical pulse (signal) or no pulse, a GPIO pin is either driven **HIGH (Logic 1, typically 3.3 V)** or **LOW (Logic 0, typically 0 V)**. ## 1.3 LED — The Light Emitting Diode An **LED (Light Emitting Diode)** is a semiconductor p-n junction device that emits photons when forward-biased. In embedded systems, LEDs serve as the simplest visual debugging tool and the canonical "Hello World" of hardware. > [!NOTE] > **Forward Voltage (Vf):** A red LED typically drops **1.8 V – 2.2 V**, while blue/white LEDs drop **3.0 V – 3.6 V**. > **Forward Current (If):** Standard indicator LEDs operate at **10 mA – 20 mA**. ## 1.4 OLED — Organic Light Emitting Diode An **OLED display** is a flat-panel display technology where each pixel is an organic compound that emits light when current flows through it. In KTU-relevant microcontrollers, the most common OLED module is the **0.96-inch SSD1306-based 128×64 monochrome display**, which interfaces via **I²C (2-wire)** or **SPI (4-wire)**. ## 1.5 Sensors — The Sensory Organs of Embedded Systems A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, light, pressure, distance) into an electrical signal (voltage, current, resistance, or digital word). The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically focuses on: - **Analog sensors** (e.g., LM35 temperature sensor) → require **ADC** - **Digital sensors** (e.g., DHT11, HC-SR04) → use **serial protocols** # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 LED Interfacing — Theoretical Foundation ### 2.1.1 Electrical Characteristics An LED is a **non-ohmic** device. Connecting it directly to a microcontroller pin without current limiting will result in: - Excessive current draw - LED burnout - Potential MCU pin damage A **current-limiting resistor (R)** must be placed in series with the LED. ### 2.1.2 GPIO Pin Configuration for LED Modern ARM Cortex-M MCUs (e.g., LPC2148, STM32) require: 1. **Pin Function Select Register** — configure pin as GPIO (not alternate function) 2. **Pin Direction Register** — set as Output 3. **Pin Value Register** — drive HIGH or LOW ### 2.1.3 Active-High vs Active-Ligh Configuration - **Active-High:** LED anode connects to MCU pin via resistor; cathode to GND. Logic 1 = ON. - **Active-Low:** LED cathode connects to MCU pin; anode to Vcc via resistor. Logic 0 = ON. ## 2.2 Assembly Language Programming (ALP) for LED ALP provides **direct register-level control** with zero abstraction. The two key instructions for GPIO manipulation in ARM7 (LPC2148) are: - **LDR** — Load Register from memory - **STR** — Store Register to memory The relevant registers in LPC2148 are: - **IODIR0** (Port 0 Direction Register, offset `0xE0028008`) - **IOSET0** (Port 0 Output Set Register, offset `0xE0028004`) - **IOCLR0** (Port 0 Output Clear Register, offset `0xE002800C`) ## 2.3 Embedded C Programming for LED Embedded C uses **memory-mapped I/O** through pointer-based register access. The canonical structure includes: - `#define` macros for register addresses - Volatile pointer declarations to prevent compiler optimization - Structured loops for blinking patterns ## 2.4 OLED — SSD1306 Architecture The **SSD1306** is a single-chip CMOS OLED/PLED driver with controller for organic/polymer light emitting diode dot-matrix graphic display system. Key features: - **Resolution:** 128 × 64 pixels - **GDDRAM (Graphic Display Data RAM):** 128 × 64 bits = **1024 bytes** - **Interface:** I²C (default address **0x3C** or **0x3D**) / SPI / Parallel - **Page Addressing Mode:** RAM is divided into **8 pages** of 128 bytes each The **I²C protocol** uses two open-drain lines: - **SDA (Serial Data)** - **SCL (Serial Clock)** With pull-up resistors (typically **4.7 kΩ**). ## 2.5 Sensor Interfacing — Analog vs Digital ### 2.5.1 Analog Sensors — LM35 Temperature Sensor - **Output:** Analog voltage linearly proportional to temperature - **Scale Factor:** **10 mV / °C** - **Range:** 0 °C to +150 °C - **Accuracy:** ±0.5 °C (typical) - **Supply:** +4 V to +30 V ### 2.5.2 Digital Sensors — HC-SR04 Ultrasonic Sensor - **Operating Voltage:** 5 V DC - **Operating Current:** **15 mA** - **Range:** 2 cm – 400 cm - **Accuracy:** 3 mm - **Principle:** Measures time-of-flight of ultrasonic burst (40 kHz) ## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table contains **exam-critical formulas**. Master these for guaranteed marks in numerical/derivation questions. | # | Formula / Concept | Description | Standard Units | |---|-------------------|-------------|----------------| | 1 | $R = \dfrac{V_{cc} - V_f}{I_f}$ | Current-limiting resistor for LED | $V$ in Volts, $I$ in Amps, $R$ in $\Omega$ | | 2 | $P_{LED} = V_f \times I_f$ | Power dissipated by LED | Watts (W) | | 3 | $P_R = I_f^2 \times R$ | Power dissipated by resistor | Watts (W) | | 4 | $D = \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \times 2^{n}$ | ADC digital output code | $D$ is dimensionless, $n$ = ADC resolution bits | | 5 | $V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^{n}} \times V_{ref}$ | Reconstructing analog input | Volts (V) | | 6 | $T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{10 \text{ mV}}$ | LM35 temperature conversion | Degrees Celsius | | 7 | $T(^{\circ}F) = \dfrac{9}{5} \times T(^{\circ}C) + 32$ | Celsius to Fahrenheit | Degrees Fahrenheit | | 8 | $d = \dfrac{t \times v}{2}$ | Ultrasonic distance (time-of-flight) | $d$ in meters, $t$ in seconds, $v$ = 343 m/s | | 9 | $f_{SCL} = \dfrac{f_{PCLK}}{(PELI + 2)}$ | I²C clock frequency (LPC2148) | Hertz (Hz) | | 10 | $f_{blink} = \dfrac{1}{2 \times T_{delay}}$ | LED blink frequency | Hertz (Hz) | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** Throughout these notes, mathematical absolute value and OR conditions are written using `\vert` or `\mid` (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) to preserve the integrity of the markdown table syntax above. ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **LED Arrays:** Traffic signals, automotive dashboards, status indicators in industrial PLCs - **OLED Displays:** Smart watches, medical device HMIs, IoT sensor readout screens - **LM35 + MCU:** HVAC controllers, cold-chain物流 monitoring, weather stations - **HC-SR04:** Robotics obstacle avoidance, parking sensors, liquid level measurement in tanks # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Current-Limiting Resistor Value for LED **Problem Statement:** A red LED ($V_f = 2.0 \text{ V}$, $I_f = 15 \text{ mA}$) is connected to an LPC2148 GPIO pin (logic HIGH = **3.3 V**). Calculate the required series resistor value and its power rating. ### Step 1: Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL) Around the series loop (Vcc → Resistor → LED → GND): $$V_{cc} = V_R + V_f$$ ### Step 2: Solve for Voltage Drop Across Resistor Substituting $V_{cc} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ and $V_f = 2.0 \text{ V}$: $$V_R = V_{cc} - V_f = 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.0 \text{ V} = 1.3 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 3: Apply Ohm's Law to Find Resistance Using $V_R = I_f \times R$ and rearranging: $$R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f}$$ ### Step 4: Substitute Values (Convert mA to A) $I_f = 15 \text{ mA} = 0.015 \text{ A}$ $$R = \dfrac{1.3 \text{ V}}{0.015 \text{ A}}$$ $$R = 86.67 \, \Omega$$ ### Step 5: Apply Engineering Tolerance Rule Standard resistor values come in **E12 series** (10% tolerance). The nearest standard value is **82 Ω** or **100 Ω**. We select **82 Ω** to ensure $I_f$ does not exceed 15 mA. ### Step 6: Calculate Actual LED Current with Chosen Resistor $$I_{f,actual} = \dfrac{1.3 \text{ V}}{82 \, \Omega} = 15.85 \text{ mA}$$ ### Step 7: Calculate Resistor Power Rating (Safety Margin 2×) $$P_R = I_f^2 \times R = (0.01585)^2 \times 82 = 0.0206 \text{ W} = 20.6 \text{ mW}$$ Using a **2× safety margin**: $P_{rating} \geq 2 \times 20.6 \text{ mW} = 41.2 \text{ mW}$. Select a standard **1/4 W (250 mW)** resistor. > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** $R = 82 \, \Omega$, Power Rating = **1/4 W** --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2: LM35 Temperature Reading via 10-bit ADC **Problem Statement:** An LM35 is connected to channel AD0.1 of an LPC2148 (10-bit ADC, $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$). The ADC returns the digital code **D = 512**. Calculate the temperature in °C and °F. ### Step 1: Reconstruct Analog Voltage from ADC Code The ADC output for a 10-bit converter ranges from **0** to **1023**. $$V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^{n}} \times V_{ref}$$ $$V_{in} = \dfrac{512}{2^{10}} \times 3.3 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{in} = \dfrac{512}{1024} \times 3.3 \text{ V} = 0.5 \times 3.3 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{in} = 1.65 \text{ V}$$ ### Step 2: Apply LM35 Scale Factor LM35 outputs **10 mV per °C**: $$T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{V_{out}}{10 \text{ mV}} = \dfrac{1.65 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ V/}^{\circ}\text{C}}$$ $$T(^{\circ}C) = 165^{\circ}\text{C}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Validation Check:** The maximum LM35 output for 150 °C is 1.5 V. A reading of 1.65 V implies **165 °C**, which is **OUT OF RANGE**. Either the sensor is faulty or the ADC reference is mismatched. ### Step 3: Convert to Fahrenheit (Assuming Valid Reading) $$T(^{\circ}F) = \dfrac{9}{5} \times T(^{\circ}C) + 32$$ $$T(^{\circ}F) = 1.8 \times 165 + 32 = 297 + 32$$ $$T(^{\circ}F) = 329^{\circ}\text{F}$$ --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Ultrasonic Distance Measurement **Problem Statement:** An HC-SR04 returns an echo pulse of width **t = 588 μs**. Calculate the distance to the object. ### Step 1: Recall the Speed of Sound at 20 °C $$v = 343 \text{ m/s}$$ ### Step 2: Apply the Time-of-Flight Equation The factor of 2 accounts for the round trip (transmit → reflect → receive): $$d = \dfrac{t \times v}{2}$$ ### Step 3: Substitute Values $t = 588 \, \mu s = 588 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s}$ $$d = \dfrac{(588 \times 10^{-6}) \times 343}{2}$$ $$d = \dfrac{0.201684}{2}$$ $$d = 0.100842 \text{ m}$$ ### Step 4: Express in Centimeters $$d \approx 10.08 \text{ cm}$$ --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Programming Paradigm Comparison | Parameter | ALP (Assembly Language) | Embedded C | |-----------|-------------------------|------------| | **Abstraction Level** | Zero (register-level) | Low (memory-mapped I/O) | | **Code Size** | Compact (4–8 bytes per instruction) | Larger (compiled output) | | **Execution Speed** | Fastest possible | Near-optimal with `-O2` | | **Readability** | Poor (mnemonic-dependent) | High (structured) | | **Portability** | MCU-specific | Moderate (header redefinition) | | **Debugging Ease** | Difficult | Easier with IDE breakpoints | | **KTU Exam Frequency** | Medium (theory questions) | High (algorithm + code) | | **Learning Curve** | Steep | Moderate | ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Sensor Comparison | Parameter | LM35 (Analog) | DHT11 (Digital) | HC-SR04 (Digital) | |-----------|---------------|------------------|-------------------| | **Quantity Measured** | Temperature | Temp + Humidity | Distance | | **Output Type** | Analog Voltage | Single-wire digital | PWM pulse width | | **Resolution** | Infinite (continuous) | 1 °C, 1% RH | 3 mm | | **Interface** | ADC Channel | GPIO + Timing | 2 GPIOs (Trig/Echo) | | **Accuracy** | ±0.5 °C | ±2 °C, ±5% RH | ±3 mm | | **Cost (INR)** | ₹40 | ₹90 | ₹80 | | **Range** | 0–150 °C | 0–50 °C, 20–90% RH | 2–400 cm | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LED Interfacing — Block-Level Functional Architecture The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the data flow and electrical topology of an LED interfaced to a microcontroller GPIO pin. ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU["ARM Cortex M
Microcontroller"] --> REG1["IODIR Register
Set Pin as Output"] REG1 --> REG2["IOSET Register
Drive Logic HIGH"] REG2 --> PIN["GPIO Pin P0.0
3.3 V Output"] PIN --> RES["Current Limiting
Resistor R = 82 Ohm"] RES --> LED["LED Anode
Vf = 2.0 V"] LED --> GND["Cathode to
Ground GND"] REG2 -.Control.-> DELAY["Software Delay
Loop"] DELAY --> REG3["IOCLR Register
Drive Logic LOW"] REG3 --> PIN ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** The MCU writes to the IOSET register to illuminate the LED. A software delay loop holds the state. The MCU then writes to the IOCLR register to extinguish the LED. This cycle creates the **blinking** effect. ## 4.2 OLED SSD1306 I²C Interfacing — Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph MCU["ARM Cortex M Microcontroller"] I2C0["I2C0 Controller"] GPIO_SDA["P0.3 SDA Pin"] GPIO_SCL["P0.2 SCL Pin"] end subgraph OLED["SSD1306 OLED Module"] DRV["SSD1306 Driver IC"] GDDRAM["GDDRAM 1024 bytes
8 Pages x 128 bytes"] PANEL["128 x 64 OLED
Pixel Matrix"] end I2C0 --> GPIO_SDA I2C0 --> GPIO_SCL GPIO_SDA --Pull up 4.7k to 3.3V.--> SDA_LINE["SDA Line
Open Drain"] GPIO_SCL --Pull up 4.7k to 3.3V.--> SCL_LINE["SCL Line
Open Drain"] SDA_LINE --> DRV SCL_LINE --> DRV DRV --> GDDRAM GDDRAM --> PANEL ``` ## 4.3 Sensor Interfacing — Unified Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph PHYS["Physical World"] TEMP["Temperature"] LIGHT["Light Intensity"] DIST["Distance to Object"] end subgraph SENS["Sensor Layer"] LM35["LM35 Analog
10 mV per C"] LDR["LDR Voltage Divider
Analog"] HCSR["HC-SR04 Digital
PWM Echo"] end subgraph MCU["Microcontroller"] ADC["10 bit ADC
Vref 3.3 V"] CAPT["Capture Timer
for PWM"] PROC["CPU Core
Computation"] end subgraph OUT["Output Peripherals"] LEDOUT["LED Indicator"] OLEDOUT["OLED Display"] UART_OUT["UART to PC"] end TEMP --> LM35 --> ADC --> PROC LIGHT --> LDR --> ADC --> PROC DIST --> HCSR --> CAPT --> PROC PROC --> LEDOUT PROC --> OLEDOUT PROC --> UART_OUT ``` ## 4.4 LED Blinking — Program Flow (Finite State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> INIT INIT --> CONFIG_GPIO: Set P0.0 as Output CONFIG_GPIO --> LED_OFF: IOSET cleared LED_OFF --> DELAY_ON: 500 ms wait DELAY_ON --> LED_ON: IOSET bit 0 LED_ON --> DELAY_OFF: 500 ms wait DELAY_OFF --> LED_OFF: IOCLR bit 0 DELAY_OFF --> [*]: System halt ``` > [!NOTE] > **State Machine Interpretation:** This is an infinite-loop FSM unless the `[*] --> DELAY_OFF` transition is taken (system halt). In practice, the loop back from `LED_ON` to `DELAY_OFF` is implemented via an unconditional branch in ALP or a `while(1)` in C. ## 4.5 OLED Page Addressing — Memory Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph GDDRAM["GD1306 GDDRAM Layout"] P0["Page 0
Col 0 to 127
Rows 0 to 7"] P1["Page 1
Rows 8 to 15"] P2["Page 2
Rows 16 to 23"] P3["Page 3
Rows 24 to 31"] P4["Page 4
Rows 32 to 39"] P5["Page 5
Rows 40 to 47"] P6["Page 6
Rows 48 to 55"] P7["Page 7
Rows 56 to 63"] end P0 --> P1 --> P2 --> P3 --> P4 --> P5 --> P6 --> P7 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Exam Tip:** In **Page Addressing Mode**, the column counter auto-increments, but the page counter does NOT. To move to a new page, you must send the `B0h` command again. This is a common bug area in student code. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: LED Current-Limiting Resistor **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Q: Design an LED interfacing circuit with an LPC2148 GPIO pin (logic HIGH = 3.3 V) for a green LED with $V_f = 2.2 \text{ V}$ and $I_f = 10 \text{ mA}$. Calculate the series resistance value.** **Model Answer:** Applying KVL around the loop: $$V_{cc} = V_R + V_f$$ $$V_R = 3.3 \text{ V} - 2.2 \text{ V} = 1.1 \text{ V}$$ By Ohm's Law: $$R = \dfrac{V_R}{I_f} = \dfrac{1.1 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ mA}} = 110 \, \Omega$$ **Nearest E12 standard value:** $R = 100 \, \Omega$ (preferred) or $R = 120 \, \Omega$. **Actual current with 100 Ω:** $$I_{actual} = \dfrac{1.1 \text{ V}}{100 \, \Omega} = 11 \text{ mA} \, (\text{within safe limit})$$ --- ### Question 2: OLED SSD1306 I²C Address **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Q: What is the default I²C slave address of the SSD1306 OLED display? Name the two lines used in I²C communication and state the typical pull-up resistor value.** **Model Answer:** - **Default I²C Address:** `0x3C` (7-bit format) — sometimes `0x3D` for secondary address - **Two I²C Lines:** SDA (Serial Data) and SCL (Serial Clock) - **Pull-up Resistor Value:** Typically **4.7 kΩ** to Vcc (3.3 V or 5 V) --- ## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice) ### Question A: LED Blinking with Assembly Language and Embedded C **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** #### (a) Write an ARM7 (LPC2148) ALP program to blink an LED connected to P0.0 with a 500 ms delay. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** ```assembly AREA LED_BLINK, CODE, READONLY ENTRY EXPORT __main __main ; Step 1: Configure P0.0 as Output LDR R0, =IODIR0_ADDR ; Load IODIR0 address LDR R1, =0x00000001 ; Set bit 0 for output STR R1, [R0] ; Store to IODIR0 LOOP ; Step 2: Turn LED ON (Drive HIGH) LDR R0, =IOSET0_ADDR LDR R1, =0x00000001 STR R1, [R0] ; Step 3: 500 ms delay (software loop) LDR R2, =DELAY_COUNT DELAY1 LDR R3, =DELAY_COUNT DELAY2 SUBS R3, R3, #1 BNE DELAY2 SUBS R2, R2, #1 BNE DELAY1 ; Step 4: Turn LED OFF (Drive LOW) LDR R0, =IOCLR0_ADDR LDR R1, =0x00000001 STR R1, [R0] ; Step 5: 500 ms delay LDR R2, =DELAY_COUNT DELAY3 LDR R3, =DELAY_COUNT DELAY4 SUBS R3, R3, #1 BNE DELAY4 SUBS R2, R2, #1 BNE DELAY3 ; Step 6: Repeat B LOOP IODIR0_ADDR EQU 0xE0028008 IOSET0_ADDR EQU 0xE0028004 IOCLR0_ADDR EQU 0xE002800C DELAY_COUNT EQU 0x0007FFFF ; Calibrated for 500 ms @ 60 MHz END ``` **Key Instruction Explanation (for marks):** - `LDR R0, =address` → Loads a 32-bit constant (literal pool access) - `STR R1, [R0]` → Stores R1 into the memory location pointed by R0 - `SUBS` / `BNE` → Decrement-and-branch pattern implements the software delay loop #### (b) Rewrite the same program in Embedded C using register-level access. (7 Marks) **Model Answer:** ```c #include /* Header for LPC2148 register definitions */ #define LED_PIN (1 << 0) /* P0.0 mask */ #define DELAY_VAL 500000 /* Calibrated for ~500 ms @ 60 MHz */ void delay_ms(unsigned int count) { unsigned int i, j; for (i = 0; i < count; i++) { for (j = 0; j < 50; j++) { /* Empty nested loop for software delay */ } } } int main(void) { /* Step 1: Configure P0.0 as Output */ IODIR0 |= LED_PIN; /* Step 2: Infinite loop for blinking */ while (1) { IOSET0 = LED_PIN; /* Turn LED ON */ delay_ms(DELAY_VAL); /* Wait 500 ms */ IOCLR0 = LED_PIN; /* Turn LED OFF */ delay_ms(DELAY_VAL); /* Wait 500 ms */ } return 0; /* Never reached */ } ``` **Explanation of key constructs:** | Construct | Purpose | |-----------|---------| | `IODIR0 \|= LED_PIN` | Read-Modify-Write; sets P0.0 as output | | `IOSET0 = LED_PIN` | Atomic set; only bit 0 is affected | | `IOCLR0 = LED_PIN` | Atomic clear; only bit 0 is affected | | `volatile` qualifier (in header) | Prevents compiler from optimizing out GPIO writes | --- ### Question B: Sensor Interfacing and OLED Display **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 | Module 2 | 14 Marks]** #### (a) Explain the interfacing of an LM35 temperature sensor with LPC2148. Show the ADC configuration steps and derive the temperature from a 10-bit ADC reading of 410. Assume $V_{ref} = 3.3 \text{ V}$. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Step 1: Hardware Interfacing** LM35 Pin 1 (Vcc) → +5 V supply LM35 Pin 2 (Vout) → LPC2148 ADC pin P0.28 (AD0.1) LM35 Pin 3 (GND) → Common Ground **Step 2: ADC Register Configuration in Embedded C** ```c #include void ADC_Init(void) { PINSEL1 = (PINSEL1 & ~(3 << 24)) | (1 << 24); /* P0.28 as AD0.1 */ AD0CR = (1 << 1) /* Select AD0.1 channel */ | (4 << 8) /* Clock divider: PCLK/5 */ | (1 << 21); /* Power up ADC */ } unsigned int ADC_Read(void) { AD0CR |= (1 << 24); /* Start conversion now */ while (!(AD0GDR & (1 << 31))); /* Wait for DONE flag */ return (AD0GDR >> 6) & 0x3FF; /* Extract 10-bit result */ } float LM35_ReadTemperature(void) { unsigned int digital = ADC_Read(); float voltage = (digital * 3.3f) / 1024.0f; float tempC = voltage / 0.010f; /* 10 mV per °C */ return tempC; } ``` **Step 3: Derive Temperature from D = 410** Reconstruct analog voltage: $$V_{in} = \dfrac{D}{2^{n}} \times V_{ref} = \dfrac{410}{1024} \times 3.3 \text{ V}$$ $$V_{in} = 0.4004 \times 3.3 = 1.3213 \text{ V}$$ Apply LM35 scale factor: $$T(^{\circ}C) = \dfrac{V_{in}}{10 \text{ mV}} = \dfrac{1.3213 \text{ V}}{0.010 \text{ V/}^{\circ}\text{C}}$$ $$T(^{\circ}C) = 132.13^{\circ}\text{C}$$ **Validation:** This is within the LM35's 0–150 °C range, so the reading is **valid**. #### (b) Draw and explain the SSD1306 OLED page addressing mode. How is a single character 'A' displayed at Page 2, Column 10? (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Page Addressing Mode Architecture:** The 128×64 GDDRAM is divided into **8 pages** (Page 0 to Page 7), each containing 8 vertical pixel rows. Each page has 128 bytes (one byte = 8 vertical pixels in a column). **Steps to Display 'A' at Page 2, Column 10:** | Step | Command (Hex) | Description | |------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | `0xB2` | Set Page Address to Page 2 (B0h + page number) | | 2 | `0x00` (lower nibble) | Set Column Address low nibble = 0 | | 3 | `0x10` (upper nibble) | Set Column Address high nibble = 1 → Column = `0x10` + `0x0A` = **26** | > [!NOTE] > **Correction:** To place at Column 10, send `0x0A` (low) and `0x10` (high), giving final column address = `(0x10 << 4) | 0x0A` = **0x1A** = **26** (not 10, because SSD1306 uses 0-indexed 128-column addressing with split high/low nibble command protocol). **Step 4: Send ASCII Lookup for 'A'** The ASCII value of 'A' is `0x41`. Using a **5×7 font lookup table** (typically stored in code memory): ```c const unsigned char font5x7['A' - 32][5] = { /* ... 5-byte column pattern ... */ }; ``` Send 5 bytes of column data via I²C to the OLED. The SSD1306 auto-increments the column pointer after each byte. **I²C Transmission Format:** ``` [Start] [Slave Addr 0x3C + Write] [Control Byte 0x40 = Data Stream] [Byte 1] [Byte 2] [Byte 3] [Byte 4] [Byte 5] [Stop] ``` --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Wrong IODIR Addressing:** Writing to `IOSET0` without first configuring `IODIR0` — LED never lights. *(–2 marks)* > 2. **Confusing IOSET vs IOCLR:** Using `IOSET` to "turn OFF" an LED — IOSET only sets bits; it cannot clear them. Use `IOCLR` for OFF. *(–2 marks)* > 3. **Forgetting the 10 mV/°C factor:** Writing `T = Vout × 1000` instead of `T = Vout / 0.010`. *(–3 marks)* > 4. **Ignoring the factor of 2 in ultrasonic:** Computing `d = t × v` instead of `d = (t × v) / 2`. *(–2 marks)* > 5. **No `volatile` qualifier:** Compiler optimizes away GPIO writes. *(–1 mark in C code)* > 6. **Wrong page command:** Using `0xB0 + 1 = 0xB1` for Page 1 — correct; but many students use `0xB1` thinking it means "column 1" (it doesn't!). *(–2 marks)* ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:** - [x] **LED resistor formula:** $R = (V_{cc} - V_f) / I_f$ — must convert mA to A - [x] **LPC2148 GPIO registers:** IODIR (direction), IOSET (set), IOCLR (clear), IOPIN (read) - [x] **ALP instructions:** LDR, STR, SUBS, BNE, B (branch) — minimum required - [x] **Embedded C header:** `` provides register definitions - [x] **ADC formula:** $D = (V_{in} / V_{ref}) \times 2^n$ — where n is resolution (10 for LPC2148) - [x] **LM35 scale:** 10 mV/°C, range 0–150 °C, ±0.5 °C accuracy - [x] **HC-SR04 range:** 2–400 cm, formula: $d = (t \times 343) / 2$ - [x] **OLED controller:** SSD1306, 128×64, I²C address `0x3C`, 4.7 kΩ pull-up - [x] **OLED pages:** 8 pages × 128 bytes = 1024 bytes GDDRAM - [x] **Page command:** `0xB0 + page_number` (Page 0 to Page 7) - [x] **I²C lines:** SDA (data) + SCL (clock), both open-drain with pull-ups - [x] **Active-High LED:** Logic 1 = ON, Logic 0 = OFF (most common configuration) - [x] **Delay loops in ALP:** Use nested `SUBS` + `BNE` pattern; calibrate with cycle count - [x] **C delay:** Nested `for` loops with `volatile` to prevent optimization - [x] **Sensor types:** Analog (ADC needed) vs Digital (GPIO or protocol) - [x] **KTU 2024 weightage:** Module 2 typically carries 14–20 marks in ESE paper --- **END OF NOTES — MODULE 2: INTERFACING OF PERIPHERALS**

topics.title: Simple Switch and Keyboard Interfacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Simple Switch and Keyboard Interfacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Simple Switch and Keyboard Interfacing** is the foundational technique in embedded system design where electromechanical input devices (momentary push-button switches and multi-key matrix keypads) are electrically and logically connected to a microcontroller's General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) pins to provide human-machine interaction (HMI). The interface handles **electrical conditioning** (pull-up/pull-down biasing), **mechanical noise suppression** (debouncing), and **logical encoding** (key identification through row-column scanning algorithms).

In strict KTU terminology, a **switch** is a *single-bit input transducer* (1-bit input device) that changes logic state upon actuation, whereas a **keyboard (keypad)** is an *array of switches* organized in a **row-column matrix topology** to minimize the GPIO pin count of the host microcontroller.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Memorize This):** "A matrix keyboard is a hardware arrangement of keys in rows and columns such that $N + M$ I/O lines can scan $N \times M$ keys, thereby achieving **I/O pin optimization**."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a cashier in a busy supermarket with **only 2 hands and 10 fingers** (limited GPIO pins). Instead of hiring 100 assistants to handle 100 different products (one pin per key — wasteful), you arrange the products in a **grid of shelves** (matrix). Now you can identify any product by knowing just its **row number and column number** (2-dimensional addressing). The cashier (microcontroller) sends a signal down each row and listens to each column — a technique called **scanning**.

**Real-World Analogy: The Elevator Button Panel**
- The elevator has 16 buttons arranged in a **4×4 matrix**.
- The microcontroller uses **4 row lines + 4 column lines = 8 wires** instead of 16 separate wires.
- When you press "5", the controller detects the intersection of row 2 and column 2.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Insight — The Debouncing Problem:** When you flick a light switch, you hear a *single click*, but electrically the contacts actually *bounce* against each other for **5–20 milliseconds** before settling. A microcontroller running at **72 MHz** can sample this bounce **1.4 million times** in that brief window, "seeing" multiple fake presses. This is why **debouncing** is a compulsory step in KTU board problems.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

The following values are **industry-standard** and frequently appear in KTU numerical questions:

| Parameter | Standard Value | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical Bounce Time** | **5 ms – 20 ms** | Determines debounce delay |
| **Pull-up Resistor Value** | **10 kΩ** (internal/external) | Standard CMOS biasing |
| **Matrix Scan Cycle** | **< 20 ms** | Imperceptible to human user |
| **Logic LOW Voltage (V_IL)** | **0 V – 0.8 V** | Active-low key press |
| **Logic HIGH Voltage (V_IH)** | **2.0 V – 5.0 V** | Default idle state |
| **Human Reaction Time** | **~ 200 ms** | Minimum key-hold duration |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** Always state the bounce time range (5–20 ms) and your chosen debounce delay (typically **10 ms** or **20 ms**) explicitly in exam answers for full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Theory: The Three Pillars of Switch Interfacing

### Pillar 1: Electrical Biasing (Pull-Up / Pull-Down)

A microcontroller's input pin is a **high-impedance CMOS gate** (input leakage current ~ **±1 µA**). If left *floating* (unconnected), it acts like an antenna, picking up electromagnetic noise and producing random logic transitions. To prevent this, we **bias** the pin to a known state using a resistor.

**Pull-Up Configuration (Active-Low Logic):**
- A resistor connects the pin to **V_CC (+5 V)**.
- Default (switch OPEN) → Pin reads **HIGH (1)**.
- Switch CLOSED → Pin is shorted to **GND (0 V)** → Pin reads **LOW (0)**.
- This is the **most common** configuration in KTU textbooks and ARM/AVR microcontrollers because most modern MCUs have *internal* pull-ups.

**Pull-Down Configuration (Active-High Logic):**
- A resistor connects the pin to **GND**.
- Default (switch OPEN) → Pin reads **LOW (0)**.
- Switch CLOSED → Pin is pulled to **V_CC** → Pin reads **HIGH (1)**.
- Less common because MCUs typically do not have internal pull-downs.

### Pillar 2: Switch Debouncing (Software & Hardware)

**Hardware Debouncing (RC Filter + Schmitt Trigger):**
- A **Resistor-Capacitor (RC) low-pass filter** smooths the bouncing waveform.
- Time constant: $\tau = R \times C$
- Schmitt trigger hysteresis ($V_{T+} - V_{T-}$) ensures a single clean transition.

**Software Debouncing (Delay-based):**
- On detecting an edge, the CPU waits for **> bounce time** (e.g., **20 ms**) and re-samples the pin.
- Two algorithms: **Polling-based delay** (blocking) and **Interrupt + Timer-based** (non-blocking).

### Pillar 3: Matrix Keyboard Scanning Algorithm

For a **4×4 keypad** with 16 keys:
- **8 I/O pins** total (4 rows + 4 columns), instead of 16.
- **Row lines = Outputs**, **Column lines = Inputs (with pull-ups)**.
- Algorithm (Reverse/Row-Scan Method):
  1. Drive all rows **LOW** simultaneously.
  2. Read column lines — if any column is **LOW**, a key is pressed.
  3. To identify *which* key: drive one row **LOW** at a time (others HIGH).
  4. When the column line goes LOW, the **intersection** identifies the key.

## 2.2 The I/O Optimization Formula (High-Yield KTU Formula)

The central formula governing matrix keyboard design is:

$$
N_{\text{pins}} = R + C
$$

$$
N_{\text{keys}} = R \times C
$$

Where:
- $R$ = number of row lines
- $C$ = number of column lines
- $N_{\text{pins}}$ = total GPIO pins consumed
- $N_{\text{keys}}$ = total number of keys supported

**Efficiency Ratio (Pins Saved):**

$$
\text{Pins Saved} = N_{\text{keys}} - N_{\text{pins}} = (R \times C) - (R + C)
$$

**Savings Percentage:**

$$
\eta = \frac{(R \times C) - (R + C)}{R \times C} \times 100 \%
$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation / Rule | Units | Exam Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Matrix pins required | $N_{\text{pins}} = R + C$ | pins | Direct calculation |
| Matrix keys supported | $N_{\text{keys}} = R \times C$ | keys | Direct calculation |
| Debounce delay (RC) | $t_d = 5 \tau = 5 R C$ | seconds | RC filter design |
| Debounce delay (SW) | $t_d > t_{\text{bounce}}$ | ms | Software loop |
| Scan time per row | $T_{\text{scan}} = R \times t_{\text{delay}}$ | ms | Full keyboard scan |
| Human perceptible limit | $T_{\text{total}} < 20 \text{ ms}$ | ms | Real-time constraint |
| Pull-up current | $I_{PU} = \frac{V_{CC}}{R_{PU}}$ | mA | Power calculation |
| Keystroke events/sec | $\text{Events} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{scan}}}$ | Hz | Throughput analysis |
| **Scan time constraint** | $R \times t_{\text{delay}} \le 20 \text{ ms}$ | ms | KTU numerical problem |
| **Minimum row count** | $R \ge \sqrt{N_{\text{keys}}}$ | rows | Optimal matrix sizing |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** The condition $R \times t_{\text{delay}} \le 20$ ms is the **single most-tested numerical constraint** in Module 2 keyboard problems. If you remember this, you can solve 90% of ESE questions on this topic.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Calculators & Microwave Ovens:** Use 4×4 or 4×5 matrix keypads with the exact scanning algorithm described above.
- **Mobile Phones:** Modern touchscreens replaced physical matrices, but the underlying **row-column sensing** principle is identical in resistive touch panels.
- **Industrial PLCs:** Use **8×8 matrix keypads** to manage 64 operator inputs with only 16 wires — critical in factory automation.
- **ATMs & Point-of-Sale Terminals:** Pin pads use specialized **keyboard encoder ICs (MM74C922, 8279)** to offload scanning from the main CPU.
- **Automotive Dashboards:** Steering-wheel buttons and infotainment controls use matrix scanning to reduce wiring harness weight.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Minimum I/O Pins for a Given Number of Keys

**Problem Statement:** Find the **minimum number of GPIO pins** required to interface a keypad of $N$ keys using the matrix topology.

**Step 1: Define the Optimization Goal**
We want to minimize the total number of pins $N_{\text{pins}} = R + C$ subject to the constraint that $R \times C \ge N$ (the matrix must hold at least $N$ keys).

**Step 2: Use the AM-GM Inequality (Arithmetic Mean ≥ Geometric Mean)**
For any two positive real numbers $R$ and $C$:

$$
\frac{R + C}{2} \ge \sqrt{R \times C}
$$

**Step 3: Rearrange the Inequality**

$$
R + C \ge 2\sqrt{R \times C}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the Constraint $R \times C = N$**

$$
R + C \ge 2\sqrt{N}
$$

**Step 5: Equality Condition**
Equality in AM-GM holds **if and only if** $R = C$. Therefore, the optimal matrix is **square** (e.g., for 16 keys, use 4×4, not 2×8).

**Step 6: Final Optimal Result**

$$
N_{\text{pins, min}} = 2 \lceil \sqrt{N} \rceil
$$

where $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ is the ceiling function (round up to next integer).

**Textual Interpretation:** The minimum pin count is achieved by a square matrix, and equals twice the square root of the number of keys. For $N = 16$: $N_{\text{pins, min}} = 2\sqrt{16} = 8$ pins. For $N = 12$: $N_{\text{pins, min}} = 2\sqrt{12} \approx 6.93 \rightarrow 8$ pins (4×3 matrix).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Interfacing Methods

The following decision matrix is the **definitive comparison** that KTU examiners expect for 7-mark questions:

| Parameter | Direct (One-to-One) | Matrix (Row-Column) | Encoded (Using 8279 / 74C922) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pins per key** | 1 | $\frac{1}{R} + \frac{1}{C}$ | 1 (data line) + strobe |
| **Total pins (16 keys)** | 16 | 8 | 4 (data bus) |
| **Software complexity** | Very low (read pin) | High (scan algorithm) | Very low (read data bus) |
| **CPU overhead** | Negligible | High (continuous scan) | Low (interrupt-driven) |
| **Ghosting possible?** | No | Yes (in 3+ key press) | No (hardware handles it) |
| **Hardware cost** | Low | Lowest | High (extra IC) |
| **Debouncing** | External | External + Software | Built-in (hardware) |
| **KTU exam preference** | Rarely asked | **MOST COMMON** | Occasionally asked |
| **Best for** | ≤ 4 keys | 5–64 keys | > 64 keys / industrial |

## 3.3 Detailed Numerical: RC Debounce Filter Design

**Problem:** Design an RC debounce filter to suppress mechanical switch bounce. Given that the maximum bounce time $t_{\text{bounce,max}} = 20$ ms, choose $R$ and $C$.

**Step 1: Write the Debounce Condition**
For the capacitor voltage to charge from 0 V to 63.2% of $V_{CC}$, we need:

$$
V_C(t) = V_{CC} \left(1 - e^{-t/RC}\right)
$$

**Step 2: Set the Time Constant**
The standard design rule is that the time constant should be **at least 1/5th** of the maximum bounce time, so the signal is fully settled by the end of the bounce period.

$$
RC \le \frac{t_{\text{bounce,max}}}{5} = \frac{20 \text{ ms}}{5} = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Choose a Standard Capacitor Value**
Let $C = 0.1 \text{ µF} = 10^{-7} \text{ F}$ (a common, non-polarized ceramic value).

**Step 4: Solve for R**

$$
R = \frac{4 \text{ ms}}{0.1 \text{ µF}} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-3}}{10^{-7}} = 4 \times 10^{4} \text{ Ω} = 40 \text{ kΩ}
$$

**Step 5: Choose Closest Standard Resistor**
Standard E12 series value closest to 40 kΩ: **$R = 39 \text{ kΩ}$** (or use **$R = 47 \text{ kΩ}$** for safety margin).

**Step 6: Verify the Time Constant**

$$
\tau = 39 \text{ kΩ} \times 0.1 \text{ µF} = 3.9 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
5\tau = 19.5 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark \text{ (Debounce successful)}
$$

## 3.4 Detailed Numerical: 4×4 Matrix Keyboard Scan Time

**Problem:** A 4×4 matrix keypad is scanned by an 8051 microcontroller. The software debounce delay per row is $t_{\text{delay}} = 5$ ms. Determine whether the scan is real-time compliant (i.e., human-imperceptible).

**Step 1: Identify the Parameters**
$R = 4$ rows, $t_{\text{delay}} = 5$ ms per row.

**Step 2: Calculate Total Scan Time**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = R \times t_{\text{delay}} = 4 \times 5 \text{ ms} = 20 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the Real-Time Constraint**

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = 20 \text{ ms} \le 20 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark
$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**
The scan is **borderline-compliant**. To be safe, designers typically reduce $t_{\text{delay}}$ to **4 ms**, giving:

$$
T_{\text{scan}} = 4 \times 4 \text{ ms} = 16 \text{ ms} < 20 \text{ ms}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** When asked "is the scan time acceptable?", always show the inequality $T_{\text{scan}} \le 20$ ms **explicitly**. Examiners award marks for this comparison.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: Single Switch Interfacing with Pull-Up Resistor (Block-Level Schematic)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the electrical connection between a mechanical push-button switch, a pull-up resistor, and a microcontroller GPIO pin. This is the **most fundamental circuit** in Module 2 and appears in nearly every KTU question paper.

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[VCC +5V] -->|R_pullup 10kΩ| B[GPIO Pin PA0]
    B --> C[MICROCONTROLLER INPUT]
    D[GND 0V] -->|Switch SW1| B
    E[Mechanical Bounce] -.->|5 to 20 ms noise| D
    F[Software Debounce] -->|delay routine| C

    style A fill:#1a5c2e,stroke:#0d3a1a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#ffcc00,stroke:#b88a00,color:#000000
    style C fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0a1a4a,color:#ffffff
    style D fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#3a0d0d,color:#ffffff
    style E fill:#cc4400,stroke:#802a00,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#4a4a4a,stroke:#2a2a2a,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **VCC (5 V)** feeds a **10 kΩ pull-up resistor** into the GPIO pin.
- The **switch SW1**, when **pressed (closed)**, shorts the pin to **GND (0 V)**, producing a logic **LOW** at the microcontroller input.
- The **mechanical bounce** is the electrical noise artifact that must be filtered by the **software debounce** routine.
- Default state (switch open): Pin = **HIGH**. Pressed state: Pin = **LOW**.

## 4.2 Diagram 2: 4×4 Matrix Keypad Architecture (Row-Column Topology)

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph ROWS [ROW LINES - OUTPUTS from MCU]
        R0[R0 GPIO PB0]
        R1[R1 GPIO PB1]
        R2[R2 GPIO PB2]
        R3[R3 GPIO PB3]
    end

    subgraph COLS [COLUMN LINES - INPUTS to MCU with pull-ups]
        C0[C0 GPIO PB4]
        C1[C1 GPIO PB5]
        C2[C2 GPIO PB6]
        C3[C3 GPIO PB7]
    end

    subgraph KEYS [4x4 KEY MATRIX - 16 KEYS]
        K00[KEY 1] 
        K01[KEY 2]
        K02[KEY 3]
        K03[KEY A]
        K10[KEY 4]
        K11[KEY 5]
        K12[KEY 6]
        K13[KEY B]
        K20[KEY 7]
        K21[KEY 8]
        K22[KEY 9]
        K23[KEY C]
        K30[KEY STAR]
        K31[KEY 0]
        K32[KEY HASH]
        K33[KEY D]
    end

    R0 --- K00
    R0 --- K01
    R0 --- K02
    R0 --- K03
    R1 --- K10
    R1 --- K11
    R1 --- K12
    R1 --- K13
    R2 --- K20
    R2 --- K21
    R2 --- K22
    R2 --- K23
    R3 --- K30
    R3 --- K31
    R3 --- K32
    R3 --- K33

    K00 --- C0
    K01 --- C1
    K02 --- C2
    K03 --- C3
    K10 --- C0
    K11 --- C1
    K12 --- C2
    K13 --- C3
    K20 --- C0
    K21 --- C1
    K22 --- C2
    K23 --- C3
    K30 --- C0
    K31 --- C1
    K32 --- C2
    K33 --- C3

    style ROWS fill:#1a5c2e,stroke:#0d3a1a,color:#ffffff
    style COLS fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0a1a4a,color:#ffffff
    style KEYS fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#3a0d0d,color:#ffffff
    style R0 fill:#2d8659,color:#ffffff
    style R1 fill:#2d8659,color:#ffffff
    style R2 fill:#2d8659,color:#ffffff
    style R3 fill:#2d8659,color:#ffffff
    style C0 fill:#3b5fbe,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#3b5fbe,color:#ffffff
    style C2 fill:#3b5fbe,color:#ffffff
    style C3 fill:#3b5fbe,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- **4 Row lines** (R0–R3) are configured as **outputs** from the microcontroller.
- **4 Column lines** (C0–C3) are configured as **inputs** with **internal pull-ups** enabled.
- Each **key** is a switch at the intersection of a row and column.
- When MCU drives R0 = LOW and reads C1 = LOW, it concludes **Key 2** (row 0, column 1) is pressed.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Keyboard Scanning Algorithm Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A([START - Power On]) --> B[Configure ROWS as OUTPUT LOW]
    B --> C[Configure COLS as INPUT with pull-up]
    C --> D[Initialize scan counter i = 0]
    D --> E{Read ALL columns}
    E -->|All HIGH| F[No key pressed]
    E -->|Any LOW| G[KEY PRESS DETECTED - Call Debounce]
    G --> H[Wait 20 ms delay]
    H --> I{Re-verify key still pressed}
    I -->|NO - was bounce| F
    I -->|YES - real key| J[Set i = 0 to begin row scan]
    J --> K[Drive ROW i LOW, all others HIGH]
    K --> L{Read all columns}
    L -->|All HIGH| M[i is less than 4]
    L -->|Column j is LOW| N[Record position i, j]
    M -->|YES| O[Increment i = i + 1]
    O --> K
    M -->|NO| P[Scan complete - return key code]
    N --> P
    P --> Q[Lookup table - convert to ASCII or value]
    Q --> R([END - Process key event])
    F --> D

    style A fill:#1a5c2e,stroke:#0d3a1a,color:#ffffff
    style B fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0a1a4a,color:#ffffff
    style C fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0a1a4a,color:#ffffff
    style G fill:#cc4400,stroke:#802a00,color:#ffffff
    style H fill:#cc8800,stroke:#805200,color:#ffffff
    style N fill:#2d8659,color:#ffffff
    style P fill:#2d8659,color:#ffffff
    style R fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#3a0d0d,color:#ffffff
    style F fill:#5c1a1a,stroke:#3a0d0d,color:#ffffff
```

**Reading the Flowchart:**
- The algorithm uses a **two-stage scan**: first detect *any* key press, then identify *which* key.
- The **debounce** step (orange node) is the **critical bottleneck** that prevents false triggers.
- The **nested loop** (outer over rows, inner over columns) is the standard KTU textbook approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Adaptation Note:** Since the topic involves physical electrical schematics (resistors, switches, IC pins), the diagrams above have been adapted to **block-level functional flow diagrams** that preserve the engineering signal flow without attempting to render literal circuit symbols — which is the KTU-recommended approach for documentation.
