| 10 | Sensor fusion (EKF) | $\hat{x}_{k \vert k} = \hat{x}_{k \vert k-1} + K_k(z_k - h(\hat{x}_{k \vert k-1}))$ | Robotics, Auto (ADAS) | $K_k$ = Kalman gain, $z_k$ = measurement |
| 11 | ASIL risk classification | $\text{ASIL} = f(\text{Severity}, \text{Exposure}, \text{Controllability})$ | Automotive | discrete levels A, B, C, D |
| 12 | Repeatability (robot) | $\text{Repeatability} = \pm k \sigma$ | Robotics | $k$ = coverage factor (typically 2 or 3) |

> [!IMPORTANT]

> - Equation (1) and (3) are used by **AUTOSAR architects** to size ECU CPU load.
> - Equation (4) is the **backbone of every motor controller** in a robot arm and every throttle body controller in a car.
> - Equation (7) is used to compute **worst-case bus load** in CAN networks (must stay **< 70%** for margin).
> - Equation (8) is the *fundamental CMOS power equation*, used by every low-power MCU designer.
> - Equation (11) is mandated by **ISO 26262** and dictates whether *single-core* or *lockstep dual-core* is required.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Real-Time Schedulability Test (Rate Monotonic Analysis)

We derive the **Liu & Layland utilisation bound** for a system of *n* periodic tasks under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. This derivation is *high-yield* for KTU as it appears in both theory and numerical problems.

### Setup

We have *n* periodic tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n$ with:
- Period $T_i$
- Worst-Case Execution Time $C_i$
- Priority assigned by *shorter period = higher priority* (Rate Monotonic).

A **critical instant** is the time at which all tasks are released simultaneously — this is the worst case for any task's response time.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the Worst-Case Response Time**

For a task $\tau_k$ released at a critical instant, the worst-case response time $R_k$ is the sum of:
- Its own execution time $C_k$, plus
- The interference from all higher-priority tasks that release within $[0, R_k]$.

The number of times a higher-priority task $\tau_j$ (with $T_j < T_k$) can preempt $\tau_k$ in an interval of length $R_k$ is at most:

$$\left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

So the total interference from $\tau_j$ is:

$$I_j(R_k) = C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

**Step 2 — Formulate the Response-Time Equation**

Summing over all higher-priority tasks and adding the task's own execution:

$$R_k = C_k + \sum_{j : T_j < T_k} C_j \cdot \left\lceil \frac{R_k}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Solvability Condition**

The task $\tau_k$ is **schedulable** if and only if $R_k \le T_k$ (its deadline equals its period under RMS).

**Step 4 — Sum Over All Tasks for a Utilisation Bound**

For the **sufficient (but not necessary) Liu–Layland bound**, consider the worst-case total utilisation at the critical instant for an *n*-task system where each task is about to miss its deadline (i.e., the CPU is fully utilised at every scheduling point).

By a *transform* argument (see Liu & Layland, 1973), the worst-case scenario is when the system is "just barely schedulable" with utilisation bound:

$$U_{bound}(n) = n \cdot \left( 2^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Limit**

Using L'Hôpital's rule on $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ as $n \to \infty$:

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n \cdot \left( 2^{\frac{1}{n}} - 1 \right) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}$$

Let $u = 1/n$, so as $n \to \infty$, $u \to 0$:

$$= \lim_{u \to 0} \frac{2^u - 1}{u}$$

Applying L'Hôpital (differentiate numerator and denominator w.r.t. $u$):

$$= \lim_{u \to 0} \frac{2^u \cdot \ln 2}{1} = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation**

The bound is **monotonically decreasing in *n***, starting from $U_{bound}(1) = 1$ and asymptoting to $\ln 2 \approx 0.6931$. For a small *n*, the bound is generous; for many tasks, the bound becomes **0.693**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example for KTU 2024**
> *Q:* A robotic system has 3 tasks under RMS with periods $T_1 = 5$ ms, $T_2 = 10$ ms, $T_3 = 20$ ms and WCETs $C_1 = 1$ ms, $C_2 = 2$ ms, $C_3 = 3$ ms. Is it schedulable?
>
> *A:*
> - Total utilisation $U = 1/5 + 2/10 + 3/20 = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.15 = 0.55$.
> - Liu–Layland bound for $n=3$: $U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 = 0.7797$.
> - Since $U = 0.55 \le U_{bound} = 0.7797$, the system is **schedulable**. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — CAN Bus Frame Time and Worst-Case Latency

The **Controller Area Network (CAN)** is the dominant automotive bus. We derive the **worst-case transmission time of a CAN frame** and the **bus loading**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Anatomy of a CAN 2.0A Frame**

A standard CAN frame consists of the following bit fields:

| Field | Bits |
|---|---|
| Start of Frame (SOF) | 1 |
| Arbitration (11-bit ID) | 11 |
| RTR | 1 |
| Control (IDE, r0, DLC 4 bits) | 4 |
| Data (0–8 bytes) | $8N$ (where $N$ = data bytes) |
| CRC | 15 |
| CRC Delimiter | 1 |
| ACK + Delimiter | 2 |
| End of Frame (EOF) | 7 |
| **Total** | $44 + 8N$ |

**Step 2 — Frame Transmission Time**

The transmission time at bit rate $f_{CAN}$ is:

$$T_{frame} = \frac{44 + 8N}{f_{CAN}}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Bus Latency**

For a high-priority message with $N$ data bytes, the **worst-case latency** under error-free conditions is:

$$T_{latency} = T_{frame}^{self} + \sum_{j \in H} T_{frame}^{j} \cdot \left\lceil \frac{T_{latency}}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

where $H$ is the set of higher-priority messages and $T_j$ is their period.

**Step 4 — Bus Utilisation**

For $m$ periodic messages with periods $T_i$:

$$U_{bus} = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \frac{T_{frame}^{i}}{T_i} < 0.7 \quad \text{(recommended 70\% rule for margin)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example for KTU 2024**
> *Q:* An automotive CAN bus runs at 500 kbps. An ECU broadcasts an 8-byte engine-speed message every 10 ms. Calculate the frame time and bus loading.
>
> *A:*
> - Bits per frame: $44 + 8 \times 8 = 44 + 64 = 108$ bits.
> - Frame time: $T_{frame} = 108 / (500 \times 10^3) = 2.16 \times 10^{-4}$ s $= 0.216$ ms.
> - Bus load from this message: $0.216 / 10 = 0.0216 = 2.16\%$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — CMOS Dynamic Power & Battery Life

The **dominant power consumption** of any CMOS-based embedded system is the *dynamic switching power*.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Energy of a Single Transition**

When a CMOS gate switches from 0→1 (or 1→0), the output capacitance $C_L$ is charged (or discharged) through the supply $V_{dd}$, dissipating energy:

$$E_{switch} = \frac{1}{2} C_L V_{dd}^2$$

Half of this energy is stored in the capacitor; the other half is dissipated in the pull-up (or pull-down) network. On the *next* transition, the stored energy is also dissipated. So per clock cycle (with both transitions):

$$E_{cycle} = C_L V_{dd}^2$$

**Step 2 — Power for a Switching Frequency $f$**

If the gate switches at frequency $f$ (Hz), the dynamic power is:

$$P_{dynamic} = C_L \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f$$

For a chip with total switched capacitance $C_{total}$ and clock $f_{clk}$:

$$P_{chip} = \alpha \cdot C_{total} \cdot V_{dd}^2 \cdot f_{clk}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **activity factor** ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$).

**Step 3 — Battery Life for Duty-Cycled Consumer Device**

A consumer IoT device spends fraction $\alpha$ of time active (consuming $P_{active}$) and $(1-\alpha)$ in sleep (consuming $P_{sleep}$). Given battery energy $E_{bat}$:

$$T_{life} = \frac{E_{bat}}{\alpha \cdot P_{active} + (1-\alpha) \cdot P_{sleep}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerical Example for KTU 2024**
> *Q:* A fitness tracker has $C_{total} = 10$ nF, $V_{dd} = 1.8$ V, $f_{clk} = 50$ MHz, $\alpha = 0.001$ (1 ms active per second). $P_{sleep} = 10$ µW. Battery is 300 mAh at 3.7 V.
>
> *A:*
> - $P_{active} = 0.001 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-9} \cdot (1.8)^2 \cdot 50 \times 10^6 = 0.001 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 3.24 \cdot 5 \times 10^7$
> - $= 0.001 \cdot 10^{-8} \cdot 1.62 \times 10^8 = 0.001 \cdot 1.62 = 1.62 \times 10^{-3}$ W = **1.62 mW**.
> - $P_{avg} = 0.001 \cdot 1.62 \times 10^{-3} + 0.999 \cdot 10 \times 10^{-6}$
> - $= 1.62 \times 10^{-6} + 9.99 \times 10^{-6} = 11.61 \times 10^{-6}$ W = **11.61 µW**.
> - $E_{bat} = 300 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 3.7 \cdot 3600 = 3996$ J.
> - $T_{life} = 3996 / (11.61 \times 10^{-6}) = 3.44 \times 10^{8}$ s $\approx$ **10.9 years** of continuous operation.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Application of Formulas to Each Domain

| Formula | Consumer Electronics Use | Robotics Use | Automotive Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **$C \le D \le T$** | Audio buffer underrun must be < 200 ms | Servo loop: $C=0.5$ ms, $T=1$ ms | Airbag: $D=10$ ms from crash detection |
| **$U_{bound}(n)$** | Multi-task smart TV (UI, audio, video) | Multi-axis robot with 6 joints | Multi-ECU gateway with 20+ messages |
| **$T_{frame}$ (CAN)** | Not typical (BLE/Wi-Fi used) | CANopen drive communication | **Primary use** (500 kbps classical CAN) |
| **$P = \alpha C V^2 f$** | Maximise battery life in wearables | Heatsink sizing per joint | ECU thermal envelope (Tj < 150 °C) |
| **PID** | Thermostat (washing machine) | **Joint position/velocity control** | Throttle, brake-by-wire, lane-keep |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The "Sense-Compute-Actuate" Loop (Universal)

This diagram represents the *common pattern* of every embedded system application across all three domains.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHYS[Physical World] -->|Stimulus| SENS[Sensors Layer\nTemp, IMU, Camera, LiDAR, Encoder]
    SENS -->|Analog/Digital| ACQ[Signal Conditioning\nand ADC]
    ACQ -->|Samples| FW[Embedded Processing\nMCU / MPU / SoC]
    FW -->|PWM / GPIO| ACT[Actuator Drivers\nMotor, Solenoid, Display]
    ACT -->|Motion / Output| PHYS
    FW <-->|I2C, SPI, UART, CAN| NET[Communication Network]
    NET <-->|CAN, Wi-Fi, BLE, Eth| EXT[External ECU / Cloud / HMI]
    FW -->|Logs / Telemetry| LOG[Non-Volatile Storage\nFlash, EEPROM, SD]
    FW -->|Safety Checks| SAFE[Watchdog + BIST + ECC]
    SAFE -->|Reset if fault| FW
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading this diagram** — Start at **PHYS** (top-left), follow arrows clockwise. The feedback loop is *closed* through the physical world. This is the canonical **closed-loop control** structure of every embedded system.

This is the most exam-relevant block diagram for the OECPT721 syllabus. A modern ECU contains the following functional blocks.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PWR[Power Supply]
        VBAT[12V Battery] --> TVS[TVS + Filter]
        TVS --> REG[5V/3.3V LDO + DC-DC]

        S1[Crankshaft Position] --> DSP1[DSP Front-end]
        S2[Cam Position] --> DSP1
        S3[MAP / MAF] --> AFE[Analog Front End]
        S4[O2 Sensor] --> AFE
        DSP1 --> MCU
        AFE --> ADC[12-bit SAR ADC]
        ADC --> MCU
    end
    subgraph CTRL[Processing Core ASIL-D]
        MCU[AURIX TC2xx\nLockstep TriCore] --> F1[Firmware Task 1: 1 ms]
        MCU --> F2[Firmware Task 2: 10 ms]
        MCU --> F3[Firmware Task 3: 100 ms]
        MCU --> WDT[Windowed Watchdog]
        MCU --> ECC[ECC on Flash and RAM]
    end
    subgraph COMM[Communication]
        MCU --> CAN1[CAN-FD 2 Mbps]
        MCU --> CAN2[CAN 500 kbps]
        MCU --> LIN[LIN 19.2 kbps]
        MCU --> ETH[100BASE-T1 Eth]
    end
    subgraph ACT[Actuator Outputs]
        MCU --> DRV1[Injector Driver]

    end
    style CTRL fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,color:#000

    style ACT fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **How to Read This Block Diagram for the Exam**
> 1. **PWR** block (top-left) shows the power conditioning chain from the noisy 12 V battery to clean 1.2/1.8/3.3 V rails.
> 2. **SENSE** block shows *two paths*: digital crank/cam sensors (DSP front-end) and analog MAP/MAF/O2 sensors (AFE + ADC).
> 3. **CTRL** block is the *heart* — a lockstep dual-core MCU running three periodic tasks (1 ms, 10 ms, 100 ms) under AUTOSAR OS. Watchdog and ECC ensure ISO 26262 compliance.
> 4. **COMM** block shows the multi-protocol gateway (CAN-FD + CAN + LIN + Automotive Ethernet).
> 5. **ACT** block drives the engine's actuators (injectors, ignition, throttle) and the OBD-II diagnostic port.

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Real-Time Control in a Robotic Arm

This diagram illustrates the **1 kHz control loop** of a single joint of an industrial robotic arm.

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant T as Timer (1 kHz)
    participant ISR as ISR Handler
    participant CTRL as PID Controller
    participant DRV as PWM Driver
    participant M as BLDC Motor
    participant E as Encoder
    participant SAFE as Safety Monitor
    T->>ISR: Trigger every 1 ms
    ISR->>E: Read position (SPI)
    E-->>ISR: Angle = 45.2 deg
    ISR->>CTRL: Compute error e = ref - angle
    CTRL->>CTRL: PID update u(t)
    CTRL->>DRV: Set PWM duty = u
    DRV->>M: Drive 3-phase FOC
    M-->>E: Shaft rotates
    ISR->>SAFE: Check over-current, over-temp
    alt Fault Detected
        SAFE-->>DRV: Disable PWM (STO)
    else Normal
        SAFE-->>ISR: OK
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway from the Sequence Diagram**
> The entire 1 ms loop must complete within **less than the loop period** (typically < 800 µs to leave 200 µs margin). This is the essence of a **hard real-time robotic system**.

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]

> *"List any **six** real-world applications of embedded systems and identify the **key engineering constraint** in each."*

**Model Answer (6 × 0.5 = 3 marks — the standard KTU 3-mark pattern):**

| # | Application | Domain | Key Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Smart washing machine | Consumer | BOM cost (₹80 ceiling for controller) |
| 2 | Fitness band / smartwatch | Consumer | Battery life (5–7 days) |
| 3 | 6-axis industrial robot | Robotics | 1 kHz deterministic control loop |
| 4 | Quadrotor drone | Robotics | Sensor fusion @ 500 Hz, low weight |
| 5 | Engine ECU | Automotive | ISO 26262 ASIL-D, 15-year life |
| 6 | Airbag controller | Automotive | ≤ 10 ms deployment latency, ASIL-D |

### Question A.2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

> *"Differentiate between **soft real-time** and **hard real-time** systems, giving **one example** from consumer electronics and **one** from automotive embedded systems."*

**Model Answer:**

- **Soft real-time system**: Missing a deadline degrades *quality* but does *not* cause system failure. *Example:* A smart TV's audio/video decoder — a missed 20 ms video frame causes a glitch but not a malfunction.
- **Hard real-time system**: Missing a deadline causes *catastrophic failure* (loss of life, money, or equipment). *Example:* An automotive airbag control unit (ACU) — failing to deploy within 10 ms after a crash is fatal.
- **Key difference:** Hard real-time requires **deterministic schedulability proofs** (RMS, deadline-monotonic analysis) and **WCET bounds**, while soft real-time allows **statistical guarantees** (average latency, jitter budget).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module 3 Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern Reminder**
> Module 3 questions in the End-Semester Examination (ESE) carry **14 marks** with an **internal choice** (the student attempts either Q-A or Q-B). Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Below, two independent alternatives are provided.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — July 2024]

**Q-A (a)** *With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a typical **automotive engine control unit (ECU)**. Discuss the role of the AURIX-class lockstep MCU, the CAN/CAN-FD communication interfaces, and the power supply chain. Highlight how ISO 26262 ASIL-D is satisfied. [7 marks]*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Block Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to **Section 4.3** above. The block diagram shows PWR → SENSE → CTRL → COMM → ACT.

**2. AURIX Lockstep MCU (1.5 marks):**
- The Infineon AURIX TC2xx/TC3xx family provides **triple-core lockstep** (e.g., TC3xx: 3 lockstep pairs).
- Lockstep means **two cores execute the same code** and a hardware comparator checks for divergence within one clock cycle.
- A divergence triggers an *immediate interrupt* and brings the system to a **safe state** (ISO 26262 requires < 10 ms fault reaction time for ASIL-D).
- ECC on SRAM and Flash memories detects and corrects single-bit errors.

**3. CAN / CAN-FD Interfaces (1.5 marks):**
- Classical CAN 2.0B at **500 kbps** for body and chassis networks.
- CAN-FD at **2 Mbps (data phase)** for powertrain and ADAS domains.
- The ECU typically has **3–6 CAN channels** plus **1–2 LIN** and **1 Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)** for high-bandwidth ADAS sensor data.

**4. Power Supply Chain (1 mark):**
- 12 V battery → TVS clamp (handles **load-dump up to 35 V** per ISO 7637-2) → common-mode choke → DC-DC buck (5 V/3.3 V) → LDO (1.2 V/1.8 V) for MCU core.
- Reverse-battery protection via P-MOSFET.

**5. ISO 26262 ASIL-D Compliance (1 mark):**
- **ASIL-D** = highest automotive integrity level, applied to systems whose failure can cause *life-threatening or fatal* injury without reasonable controllability.
- Achieved via: lockstep, ECC, watchdog (windowed), end-to-end CRC on communication, BIST (built-in self-test) at startup, diverse software, and **FMEA (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)** documentation.

---

**Q-A (b)** *Compute the **worst-case execution time budget** for a 1 kHz robotic servo loop. The system uses a Cortex-M4F MCU at 168 MHz. The control algorithm requires 45,000 cycles for PID, 8,000 cycles for encoder read, 6,500 cycles for PWM update, and 4,500 cycles for safety check. Comment on the feasibility. [7 marks]*

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Total Cycles per Iteration (1 mark):**
$$C_{cycles} = 45{,}000 + 8{,}000 + 6{,}500 + 4{,}500 = 64{,}000 \text{ cycles}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to Time (1 mark):**
$$C = \frac{64{,}000}{168 \times 10^6} = 3.81 \times 10^{-4} \text{ s} = 0.381 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Loop Period (1 mark):**
$$T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{1000} = 1 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — CPU Utilisation of this Task (1 mark):**
$$U_{servo} = \frac{C}{T} = \frac{0.381}{1} = 0.381 = 38.1\%$$

**Step 5 — Margin and Feasibility (2 marks):**
- Available slack: $T - C = 1 - 0.381 = 0.619$ ms.
- **Headroom = 61.9%** — the servo task alone uses 38.1% of one core, leaving 61.9% for other tasks (communication, trajectory planner, HMI).
- The system is **feasible** with **comfortable margin** (>50% slack is the design rule of thumb in industrial robotics).

**Step 6 — Verdict (1 mark):** ✅ *Feasible. A typical 6-axis robot runs six such servos on one MCU if their utilisation sums to ≤ 70% (Liu–Layland bound for $n=6$: $6(2^{1/6} - 1) = 6 \cdot 0.1225 = 0.735$). Six servos × 38.1% = 228% — *exceeds* the bound, so a multi-core MCU or a dedicated DSP co-processor would be needed for a full 6-DOF system.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Model Paper]

**Q-B (a)** *Explain with examples how **embedded systems are used in consumer electronics**. Discuss the hardware and software architecture of a **smart washing machine** with emphasis on the fuzzy-logic wash-cycle algorithm. [7 marks]*

**Model Answer:**

**1. Examples of Consumer Electronics Using Embedded Systems (2 marks):**
- Smart TV (Quad-core SoC, RTOS + Linux, HDMI, Wi-Fi, voice control)
- Smart refrigerator (Cortex-M3, temperature sensors, inverter compressor control)
- Microwave oven (8-bit 8051, magnetron PWM, keypad, weight sensor)
- Smart speaker (Cortex-A, Linux, beamforming microphone array, BLE/Wi-Fi)
- Fitness band (Cortex-M0, IMU, heart-rate sensor, BLE)

**2. Smart Washing Machine — Hardware Architecture (2 marks):**
- **MCU**: 8-bit/32-bit (e.g., Renesas RL78 or STM32F0), clocked at 32 MHz.
- **Sensors**: Water level (pressure transducer), load weight (strain gauge), temperature (NTC), door-open switch, motor speed (tachogenerator/encoder), vibration (3-axis accelerometer).
- **Actuators**: 3-phase BLDC motor driver (FOC algorithm), water inlet valves (2), drain pump, door lock (PWM), buzzer, 7-segment or LCD display, keypad.
- **Power**: Triac-based mains switching with zero-cross detection for motor soft-start.
- **Communication**: Optional — Wi-Fi/BLE module for IoT (LG ThinQ, Samsung SmartThings).

**3. Software Architecture (2 marks):**
- **Bare-metal super-loop** or **FreeRTOS** with 4–5 tasks:
  1. Keypad scan (10 ms)
  2. Sensor read (100 ms)
  3. Wash cycle state machine (1 s)
  4. Display refresh (50 ms)
  5. Motor FOC computation (1 ms, ISR-driven)
- **Wash cycle state machine**: Idle → Fill → Wash (with sub-states: soak, agitation, rinse, spin) → Done.
- **Fuzzy-logic algorithm (1 mark):**
  - **Inputs (fuzzification)**: Load weight, fabric type, dirt level, water temperature.
  - **Membership functions**: e.g., "load" is *Small* (0–2 kg), *Medium* (2–5 kg), *Large* (5–8 kg).
  - **Rule base** (sample):
    - IF load is *Large* AND dirt is *Heavy* THEN wash time is *Long* (45 min), agitation is *Strong*, water level is *High*.
  - **Defuzzification**: Centroid method → outputs are wash time, agitation strength, water level, spin RPM.
  - The fuzzy logic runs on the MCU and adapts *dynamically* to load sensing, unlike a fixed-timer washing machine.

---

**Q-B (b)** *With a block diagram, describe the architecture of a **modern automotive Advanced Driver-Assistance System (ADAS)**. Discuss the role of **sensor fusion**, **ASIL classification**, and **Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1)**. [7 marks]*

**Model Answer:**

**1. ADAS Block Diagram (2 marks):**

```
[Front Camera] ─┐
[Side Radar] ────┤
[Ultrasonic] ────┼──→ [Sensor Fusion ECU (ASIL-D)] ──→ [Brake Actuator]
[LiDAR] ─────────┤                                  ──→ [Steer-by-Wire]
[Rear Camera] ───┤                                  ──→ [HMI / Cluster]
[IMU/GPS] ───────┘                                   ──→ [V2X]
```

The **Sensor Fusion ECU** is a high-performance multi-core SoC (e.g., NVIDIA Orin, Mobileye EyeQ6, NXP S32G).

**2. Sensor Fusion (2 marks):**
- **Multi-modal fusion** of camera (60 fps, 2 MP), radar (77 GHz, 200 m range), LiDAR (10 Hz, 100k points/s), and IMU (1 kHz).
- **Object-level fusion**: Each sensor produces a list of objects (vehicles, pedestrians, lanes). The fusion ECU correlates objects across sensors (Kalman filter / JPDA tracker) and produces a **single, higher-confidence world model**.
- **EKF (Extended Kalman Filter)** or **particle filter** runs at 50–100 Hz, estimating position, velocity, and classification of every tracked object.
- Benefit: **Redundancy** — if a camera is blinded by sun, the radar still detects the lead vehicle.

**3. ASIL Classification (1.5 marks):**
- **AEB (Autonomous Emergency Braking)**: **ASIL-D** (failure to brake = fatal collision).
- **ACC (Adaptive Cruise Control)**: **ASIL-B** to **ASIL-C** (driver is attentive, controllable).
- **Lane Keeping Assist (LKA)**: **ASIL-B**.
- **Parking Assist**: **QM** (Quality Managed, no ASIL).
- Higher ASIL demands more rigorous development process (V-model), more verification, and lockstep hardware.

**4. Automotive Ethernet (100BASE-T1) (1.5 marks):**
- **100BASE-T1** = IEEE 802.3bw, 100 Mbps over a **single unshielded twisted pair**.
- Replaces CAN in high-bandwidth domains (ADAS, infotainment, diagnostics).
- **Advantages over CAN**: 200× higher bandwidth, supports TCP/IP and SOME/IP, deterministic via TSN (Time-Sensitive Networking, IEEE 802.1Qbv).
- Used for: camera data (raw or compressed), LiDAR point clouds, V2X (Vehicle-to-Everything), OTA updates.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on This Topic**
> 1. **Writing generic examples without stating the constraint** — "embedded systems are used in washing machines" *without* mentioning BOM cost, real-time, or safety scores 0/3. **Always state the *engineering constraint*** alongside the example.
> 2. **Confusing ASIL levels with SIL levels** — ASIL is for *automotive* (ISO 26262); SIL is for *industrial* (IEC 61508). Mixing them is a **2-mark penalty** in a 14-mark question.
> 3. **Forgetting the unit** in the PID/CAN-frame answer — examiners deduct 0.5 marks for each missing unit (e.g., "frame time = 216" *without* "µs" or "ms").
> 4. **Not drawing the block diagram** in a 7-mark architectural question — the diagram is worth **2 marks minimum** in KTU 2024. A *labelled* diagram with PWR / SENSE / CTRL / ACT blocks always scores full marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "Why"** — merely listing "ISO 26262" without explaining *what it achieves* (functional safety, ASIL-D, lockstep) loses 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Confusing RMS (Rate Monotonic Scheduling) with RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer)** — these are completely different; RMS is a real-time scheduling algorithm, RISC is a CPU architecture.
> 7. **Ignoring the Liu–Layland bound** — when asked about feasibility, students often compute *only* the task utilisation and forget to compare it with $U_{bound}(n)$. This loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> This recap covers the **single most exam-relevant** content for the topic *"Applications of Embedded Systems: Consumer Electronics, Robotics, Automobiles"* under KTU 2024 OECPT721 Module 3.

### A. Universal Concepts (All Three Domains)
- **Sense → Compute → Actuate** loop is the universal pattern.
- The real-time constraint $C \le D \le T$ must hold for every periodic task.
- The Liu–Layland bound $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ asymptotes to $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- Total utilisation $U = \sum C_i / T_i \le 1$ for *exact* schedulability.
- CMOS dynamic power $P = \alpha C V_{dd}^2 f$.

### B. Consumer Electronics — Key Takeaways
- **Primary KPI: BOM cost** (often < ₹500 for the controller).
- **Soft real-time** (audio/video glitches are tolerable; no fatalities).
- **Examples**: Smart TV, washing machine, microwave, fitness band, smart speaker.
- **Fuzzy-logic** wash cycles are a canonical example of embedded intelligence.
- **Power budget**: 0.1–5 W; deep-sleep currents of µA matter.
- **Communication**: Wi-Fi, BLE, I²C, SPI.

### C. Robotics — Key Takeaways
- **Primary KPI: Determinism** (1 kHz control loop, sub-ms latency).
- **Hard real-time**, often with **RTOS** (FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RT-Linux).
- **Examples**: Industrial 6-DOF arm, drone, surgical robot, AGV, humanoid.
- **Sensor fusion** with EKF is mandatory for autonomous navigation.
- **PID** (Proportional-Integral-Derivative) is the workhorse of motion control.
- **Communication**: EtherCAT, CANopen, TSN.
- **Standards**: ISO 10218 (industrial), ISO/TS 15066 (collaborative).

### D. Automobiles — Key Takeaways
- **Primary KPI: Functional safety** (ISO 26262, ASIL-A → ASIL-D).
- **ASIL-D** is required for airbag, steer-by-wire, AEB, ECU.
- **Lockstep dual-core**, ECC, watchdog, BIST, end-to-end CRC — non-negotiable.
- **Examples**: Engine ECU, TCU, ABS, ESC, ACU, ADAS, BCM, infotainment.
- **70–150 ECUs** in a modern car, networked over CAN/CAN-FD, LIN, FlexRay, 100BASE-T1.
- **Operating temperature**: −40 °C to +125 °C (AEC-Q100 Grade 1).
- **Lifecycle**: 15+ years; **load-dump transient**: up to 35 V.
- **Airbag deployment latency**: ≤ 10 ms (sensor trigger to squib firing).
- **Standards**: ISO 26262, AEC-Q100, IATF 16949, CISPR 25, ISO 7637-2.

### E. Cross-Domain Equations (Must Memorise)
- Frame time: $T_{frame} = (44 + 8N) / f_{CAN}$.
- Battery life: $T_{life} = E_{bat} / (\alpha P_{active} + (1-\alpha) P_{sleep})$.
- Nyquist: $f_s \ge 2 f_{max}$.
- RMS bound: $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- Total utilisation: $U = \sum C_i / T_i$.

### F. One-Line Definitions (High-Frequency Recall)
- **Embedded system**: A computer system dedicated to a specific function, embedded within a larger device, with strict real-time, cost, and power constraints.
- **Hard real-time**: Missing a deadline is a *system failure*.
- **Soft real-time**: Missing a deadline degrades *quality* only.
- **ASIL-D**: Highest automotive safety integrity level under ISO 26262.
- **Lockstep**: Two cores execute identical code, compared cycle-by-cycle for fault detection.
- **Sensor fusion**: Combining data from multiple sensors to produce a more accurate state estimate.
- **AUTOSAR**: AUTomotive Open System ARchitecture — a global standard for automotive ECU software.
- **FOC (Field-Oriented Control)**: A vector-control method for BLDC/PMSM motors that decouples torque and flux for smooth torque production.
- **CAN bus**: A multi-master, differential, message-prioritised serial bus invented by Bosch, dominant in automotive.

### G. Examiner's Final Tip
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **"Constraint-First" Answering**
> In every KTU 2024 answer on this topic, **lead with the constraint**, then the example, then the implementation. Example: *"A washing machine controller is a **cost-sensitive, soft-real-time** consumer embedded system that runs a **fuzzy-logic wash algorithm** on an 8051-class MCU..."* This structure instantly signals to the examiner that you understand the *application* (not just the device list) and fetches full marks.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Embedded System Architecture: Hardware - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Embedded System Architecture: Hardware - Processor, Controller, SoC, Memory, Peripherals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Embedded System Hardware Architecture?

An **Embedded System** is a purpose-built computing system that is an integral part of a larger mechanical or electrical system, dedicated to performing specific real-time functions with constraints on size, power, cost, and reliability. The **hardware architecture** defines the structural organization and interconnection of the physical electronic components that enable this dedicated functionality.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OECPT721)** syllabus, the hardware architecture of an embedded system is composed of **five major functional blocks**:

1. **Processor / Central Processing Unit (CPU)** — The computational brain.
2. **Controller** — A specialized processor with integrated peripherals (e.g., Microcontroller Unit — MCU).
3. **System on Chip (SoC)** — A highly integrated single-die package containing processor, memory, controllers, and peripherals.
4. **Memory Subsystem** — Volatile and non-volatile storage for program code and data.
5. **Peripherals** — External I/O devices, sensors, actuators, and communication interfaces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Verbatim Style):** "An embedded system is a microcontroller- or microprocessor-based, software-driven, reliable, real-time control system, designed for a specific dedicated function, embedded into a mechanical, electrical, or electromechanical host system."

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

To make this architecture intuitive, imagine an **embedded system as a digital human body** designed to perform one specific job (e.g., a digital thermometer reading body temperature):

| Body Part | Embedded System Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Brain (Cerebrum)** | **Processor (CPU/MPU)** | Performs arithmetic, logic, and control decisions |
| **Spinal Cord & Reflexes** | **Controller (MCU)** | Coordinates brain with limbs; handles local, fast control |
| **DNA (Complete Genetic Code)** | **SoC (System on Chip)** | Integrates *everything* — CPU + memory + peripherals — into one tiny chip |
| **Long-term Memory (Hippocampus)** | **ROM / Flash / Hard Disk** | Stores permanent program code and lookup tables |
| **Short-term Memory (Working RAM)** | **SRAM / DRAM** | Stores temporary variables, stack, heap, runtime data |
| **Five Senses (Touch, Sight, Sound)** | **Sensors & Input Peripherals** | Collect real-world data (temperature, light, pressure) |
| **Hands, Legs, Mouth** | **Actuators & Output Peripherals** | Take physical action (motors, LEDs, buzzers, displays) |
| **Nervous System (Signals)** | **Bus (Address, Data, Control Bus)** | Carries information between all components |

> [!NOTE]
> The key insight: Unlike a general-purpose computer (laptop/PC) which is a *generalist* (can do thousands of tasks), an embedded system is a **specialist** built with *just enough* hardware to do one job *extremely well, with minimal power and cost*.

---

## 1.3 Why Study Hardware Architecture?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** For Part B (14-mark) questions, examiners frequently test students on **identifying the correct hardware block** for a given embedded application, **comparing architectural models** (Harvard vs Von Neumann), and **calculating memory addressing ranges**. Mastery of these foundational blocks is therefore non-negotiable.

**Standard Engineering Metrics (Must Know in Bold):**
- **Clock Frequency (f):** Measured in **Hz, MHz, GHz** — dictates instruction execution speed.
- **Word Length (N):** **8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit, 64-bit** — number of bits processed per instruction.
- **Power Consumption (P):** Measured in **mW (milliwatts)** or **µW (microwatts)** for battery-operated devices.
- **Memory Capacity:** Measured in **Bytes, KB, MB, GB**.
- **Operating Voltage (V):** Typically **1.8 V, 3.3 V, 5 V** for modern embedded chips.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Processor — The "Brain" of the Embedded System

The **Processor (CPU / MPU)** is the master computational unit that fetches, decodes, and executes instructions. In an embedded context, processors come in several flavors:

### 2.1.1 Types of Processors Used in Embedded Systems

- **Microprocessor (MPU):** A CPU on a single IC. Requires **external** memory and peripherals. *Example:* Intel 8086 (legacy), ARM Cortex-A series.
- **Microcontroller (MCU):** CPU + Memory + Peripherals on a **single chip**. *Example:* 8051, PIC16F877A, ATmega328P, STM32.
- **Digital Signal Processor (DSP):** Specialized for high-speed numerical operations on continuous data streams. *Example:* Texas Instruments TMS320 series.
- **Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC):** Custom-designed for one product only; non-reprogrammable; highest performance and lowest unit cost.
- **Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA):** Hardware that can be reconfigured post-manufacturing. *Example:* Xilinx Spartan, Intel Cyclone.
- **System on Chip (SoC):** A complete computer system integrated onto one die. *Example:* Broadcom BCM2711 (Raspberry Pi), Qualcomm Snapdragon.

### 2.1.2 Processor Internal Architecture: Von Neumann vs Harvard

This is one of the **most repeated KTU topics**:

**A. Von Neumann Architecture (Princeton Architecture)**
- **Single shared bus** for both instructions and data.
- Cannot fetch instruction and data **simultaneously** → known as the **Von Neumann Bottleneck**.
- Simpler hardware, cheaper to design.
- *Used in:* Most general-purpose computers, early microcontrollers (e.g., 8086).

**B. Harvard Architecture**
- **Separate buses and memories** for instructions (code) and data.
- Enables **simultaneous** instruction fetch + data read/write (parallelism).
- Higher performance, more complex hardware.
- *Used in:* Most modern MCUs/DSPs (e.g., **8051, AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architecture" — this is a guaranteed **7 to 14 mark** question.

---

## 2.2 The Controller — The "Specialist" Processor

A **Controller** in embedded terminology almost always refers to a **Microcontroller Unit (MCU)**. It is essentially a "computer-on-a-chip" that integrates:
- **CPU Core** (8/16/32-bit)
- **Program Memory (Flash/ROM)**
- **Data Memory (SRAM)**
- **EEPROM** (for permanent user data)
- **I/O Ports**
- **Timers/Counters**
- **Communication Peripherals** (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB)
- **ADC / DAC**
- **Interrupt Controller**
- **Watchdog Timer**

### 2.2.1 RISC vs CISC Controller Cores

- **RISC (Reduced Instruction Set Computer):** Small, highly-optimized instruction set. Each instruction executes in **1 clock cycle** typically. *Examples:* ARM Cortex-M, AVR, MIPS, RISC-V.
- **CISC (Complex Instruction Set Computer):** Large, variable-length instruction set. Single instruction can perform multi-step operations. *Examples:* x86 (in PCs), 8051 (hybrid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Modern embedded controllers are predominantly **RISC-based** due to superior power efficiency, deterministic execution time, and suitability for **real-time** applications.

---

## 2.3 The System on Chip (SoC)

A **System on Chip (SoC)** is the pinnacle of embedded hardware integration. It integrates **almost all components of a complete electronic system** onto a single silicon die (chip).

### 2.3.1 Typical SoC Block Contents:

- **One or more Processor Cores** (CPU + GPU + DSP + NPU)
- **On-chip SRAM / Cache (L1, L2, L3)**
- **ROM / Boot ROM**
- **External memory controller** (DDR, Flash interfaces)
- **I/O Controllers** (USB, Ethernet, PCIe, HDMI)
- **Wireless radios** (Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, 4G/5G modems)
- **Image processors, video codecs**
- **Power Management Unit (PMU)**
- **Security/Encryption engines**

### 2.3.2 SoC vs Microcontroller — KTU Critical Distinction

| Feature | Microcontroller (MCU) | System on Chip (SoC) |
|---|---|---|
| **Integration Level** | CPU + Memory + Basic Peripherals | Full system including wireless, GPU, complex I/O |
| **Application** | Simple real-time control | Smartphones, IoT gateways, AI edge devices |
| **Cost** | Very low ($0.10 – $10) | Moderate to high ($5 – $500) |
| **Power** | Ultra-low (µW to mW range) | Higher (100 mW to several W) |
| **OS** | Bare-metal / RTOS (FreeRTOS) | Linux, Android, full OS support |
| **Examples** | 8051, STM32, ATmega | Raspberry Pi SoC, Snapdragon, Apple M1 |

---

## 2.4 The Memory Subsystem

Memory stores both the **program code (instructions)** and the **runtime data (variables)**.

### 2.4.1 Memory Hierarchy (from fastest/most expensive to slowest/cheapest):

1. **Registers** — Inside the CPU, fastest access (1 cycle).
2. **Cache (L1, L2, L3)** — Small, fast SRAM between CPU and RAM.
3. **Main Memory (Primary Storage)**
   - **SRAM (Static RAM):** Volatile, fast, used for cache. ~6 transistors per bit.
   - **DRAM (Dynamic RAM):** Volatile, slower, denser, needs refresh. ~1 transistor per bit.
4. **Secondary Storage (Mass Storage)**
   - **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Mask ROM — programmed at factory, unchangeable.
   - **PROM (Programmable ROM):** One-time programmable by user.
   - **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** UV-erasable, reprogrammable.
   - **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM):** Byte-level erase/write (e.g., 24Cxx I²C chips).
   - **Flash Memory:** Block-level erase/write, used in SSDs and embedded code storage.
5. **Tertiary Storage** — Hard Disk, SSD, SD cards (off-chip, slow).

### 2.4.2 Volatile vs Non-Volatile

- **Volatile:** Loses data when power is OFF. *Example:* SRAM, DRAM.
- **Non-Volatile:** Retains data without power. *Example:* ROM, Flash, EEPROM, FRAM.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In embedded systems, the program is typically stored in **Flash (non-volatile)** and copied into **SRAM (volatile)** for execution. Critical user data is stored in **EEPROM**.

---

## 2.5 The Peripherals

**Peripherals** are hardware components that allow the embedded system to **interact with the external world**.

### 2.5.1 Classification:

**A. Input Peripherals (Sensors / Receivers):**
- Temperature sensor (LM35, DHT11)
- Light sensor (LDR, photodiode)
- Accelerometer / Gyroscope (MPU6050)
- Keypad, switches, push buttons
- Microphone, camera

**B. Output Peripherals (Actuators / Transmitters):**
- LED, 7-segment display, LCD, OLED
- Buzzer, speaker
- DC motor, stepper motor, servo motor
- Relay, solenoid, heater

**C. Communication Peripherals:**
- **UART** (Universal Asynchronous Receiver/Transmitter) — serial, asynchronous.
- **SPI** (Serial Peripheral Interface) — synchronous, full-duplex, 4-wire.
- **I²C** (Inter-Integrated Circuit) — synchronous, half-duplex, 2-wire (SDA, SCL).
- **CAN** (Controller Area Network) — automotive.
- **USB, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, Bluetooth, ZigBee, LoRa**

**D. Timing Peripherals:**
- **Timers / Counters** — precise delays, event counting.
- **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** — resets system if software hangs.
- **Real-Time Clock (RTC)** — keeps wall-clock time.

**E. Analog Peripherals:**
- **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter)** — e.g., 10-bit, 12-bit.
- **DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter)**.
- **Comparators, Op-Amps** (sometimes integrated).

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize the following equations. They are tested every semester in KTU exams.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | CPU Execution Time | $T_{CPU} = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clock}}$ | Time to execute a program | Seconds (s) |
| 2 | Instruction Execution Time | $T_{inst} = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}} \times CPI$ | Per-instruction time, $CPI$ = cycles per instruction | Seconds (s) |
| 3 | CPU Performance (MIPS) | $MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6}$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS |
| 4 | Throughput | $T_{put} = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{T_{CPU}}$ | Instructions completed per second | Inst/s |
| 5 | Memory Address Range | $Range = 2^{N}$ | $N$ = number of address lines | Locations |
| 6 | Total Addressable Memory | $M_{total} = 2^{N} \times W$ | $W$ = word size (bits) | Bits |
| 7 | Memory Decoding — Bank Size | $B = 2^{n_{low}}$ | $n_{low}$ = low-order address bits | Locations |
| 8 | Number of Memory Chips | $k = \dfrac{M_{required}}{M_{per\_chip}}$ | For memory expansion | Integer |
| 9 | Address Decoding (16-to-8 decoder) | $N_{chips} = 2^{N}$ | $N$ = decoder input lines | Chips |
| 10 | ADC Resolution | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}}$ | $n$ = ADC bits, $V_{ref}$ = reference voltage | Volts (V) |
| 11 | ADC Digital Output | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor$ | Convert analog to digital | Decimal |
| 12 | Power Dissipation (CMOS) | $P = C \cdot V^2 \cdot f$ | Dynamic power of CMOS logic | Watts (W) |
| 13 | Baud Rate (UART) | $B = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{16 \times (UBRR+1)}$ | $UBRR$ = USART Baud Rate Register | Bits/s |
| 14 | Timer Overflow Frequency | $f_{overflow} = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{Prescaler \times (TOP+1)}$ | Timer interrupt rate | Hz |
| 15 | Interrupt Latency | $L = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clock}}$ | Delay between IRQ and ISR start | Seconds (s) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** If a question says "8051 with 11.0592 MHz crystal," the standard 16-bit Timer 1 reload value for **9600 baud** is **0xFD = 253** (giving UBRR = 253, since $B = \dfrac{11{,}059{,}200}{16 \times 254} \approx 9600$).

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The hardware architecture determines **every** critical product parameter:
- **Automotive (ECU, ABS, Airbag):** SoC + MCU, deterministic real-time, CAN bus.
- **Wearable Health Devices (Smartwatch, Pacemaker):** Ultra-low-power MCU, BLE SoC.
- **Industrial IoT (PLC, SCADA):** MCU + Ethernet SoC, RTOS scheduling.
- **Consumer Electronics (TV Remote, Washing Machine):** 8-bit MCU, simple peripherals.
- **Drones / Robotics (Flight Controller):** High-performance 32-bit MCU/SoC + IMU sensors + PWM actuators.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Memory Address Range & Total Addressable Memory

### Given:
- A microcontroller with an **N-bit address bus** and a **W-bit word size (data bus width)**.

### To Find:
- The total number of addressable memory locations.
- The total addressable memory in bytes.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1:** Each address line can carry one of two logic values: **0 or 1**. Therefore, the total number of unique binary combinations that can be formed by **N** address lines is given by counting in base-2.

$$
\text{Number of unique addresses} = 2^{N}
$$

**Step 2:** Each address points to one memory location. This location stores **W bits** of data (the word size). So the total memory capacity in **bits** is:

$$
M_{total\_bits} = 2^{N} \times W
$$

**Step 3:** Since **1 Byte = 8 bits**, the total memory capacity in **bytes** is:

$$
M_{total\_bytes} = \dfrac{2^{N} \times W}{8}
$$

**Step 4 (Worked Numerical Example):**
Consider the classic **Intel 8086** processor:
- Address bus: $N = 20$ bits
- Data bus: $W = 16$ bits

Number of addressable locations:

$$
2^{20} = 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ locations} = 1 \text{ MB locations}
$$

Total memory in bytes:

$$
\dfrac{2^{20} \times 16}{8} = \dfrac{16 \text{ Mbits}}{8} = 2 \text{ MB}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is why the 8086 is said to have a **1 MB address space** with a **16-bit data bus** (20-bit address). This is a classic **KTU numerical problem** in Module 3.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: CPU Execution Time and MIPS Rating

### Given:
- Clock frequency $f_{clock} = 50$ MHz
- Average CPI (Cycles Per Instruction) = 2
- Number of instructions in program = $10^6$

### To Find:
- (a) CPU execution time
- (b) MIPS rating of the processor

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 3.2.1 — Total Clock Cycles Required:**

$$
N_{cycles} = N_{inst} \times CPI = 10^6 \times 2 = 2 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 3.2.2 — Clock Period (T):**

$$
T = \dfrac{1}{f_{clock}} = \dfrac{1}{50 \times 10^6} = 20 \text{ ns}
$$

**Step 3.2.3 — CPU Execution Time:**

$$
T_{CPU} = N_{cycles} \times T = \dfrac{N_{cycles}}{f_{clock}} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^6}{50 \times 10^6} = 0.04 \text{ s} = 40 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 3.2.4 — MIPS Rating:**

By definition, MIPS is millions of instructions per second:

$$
MIPS = \dfrac{f_{clock}}{CPI \times 10^6} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^6}{2 \times 10^6} = 25 \text{ MIPS}
$$

**Step 3.2.5 — Verification via Throughput:**

$$
T_{put} = \dfrac{N_{inst}}{T_{CPU}} = \dfrac{10^6}{0.04} = 25 \times 10^6 \text{ Inst/s} = 25 \text{ MIPS} \quad \checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Logic:** If you state MIPS without showing the formula derivation, you **lose 1 mark**. Always show $MIPS = f_{clock} / (CPI \times 10^6)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Memory Expansion (Interleaving) — Numerical

### Problem:
Design a memory system for an 8-bit microcontroller (8085) that needs **16 KB of ROM** and **16 KB of RAM**, given that each memory chip is **4 KB × 8**.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 3.3.1 — Number of ROM chips needed:**

$$
k_{ROM} = \dfrac{16 \text{ KB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}
$$

**Step 3.3.2 — Number of RAM chips needed:**

$$
k_{RAM} = \dfrac{16 \text{ KB}}{4 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}
$$

**Step 3.3.3 — Total memory chips:**

$$
k_{total} = 4 + 4 = 8 \text{ chips}
$$

**Step 3.3.4 — Address lines per chip:**
Each 4 KB chip requires address lines to address $4 \text{ KB} = 4096 = 2^{12}$ locations, so it needs **12 address lines** (A0–A11).

**Step 3.3.5 — Address lines on 8085:**
The 8085 has a **16-bit address bus** (A0–A15). So upper 4 bits (A12–A15) are used to **select between the 8 chips** via a **3-to-8 decoder** (using A13, A14, A15 as decoder inputs, plus A12 as enable/extra decode).

**Step 3.3.6 — Memory Map:**

| Address Range (Hex) | Chip | Type |
|---|---|---|
| `0000H – 0FFFH` | ROM1 | ROM |
| `1000H – 1FFFH` | ROM2 | ROM |
| `2000H – 2FFFH` | ROM3 | ROM |
| `3000H – 3FFFH` | ROM4 | ROM |
| `4000H – 4FFFH` | RAM1 | RAM |
| `5000H – 5FFFH` | RAM2 | RAM |
| `6000H – 6FFFH` | RAM3 | RAM |
| `7000H – 7FFFH` | RAM4 | RAM |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention **address decoding** logic (3-to-8 decoder). Always state the decoder and the address line allocation — this fetches 2–3 extra marks.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: ADC Resolution for a Sensor Reading

### Problem:
A temperature sensor (LM35, 10 mV/°C) outputs 0–1000 mV. The embedded system uses a **10-bit ADC** with a **5 V reference voltage**. Calculate:
- (a) Step size (resolution) in volts
- (b) Digital output for an input of 250 mV
- (c) Minimum measurable temperature change.

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 3.4.1 — ADC Resolution (Step Size):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n}} = \dfrac{5}{2^{10}} = \dfrac{5}{1024} \approx 4.88 \text{ mV}
$$

**Step 3.4.2 — Digital Output for 250 mV input:**

$$
D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{LSB}} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor \dfrac{0.250}{0.00488} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 51.2 \right\rfloor = 51_{10} = 0x0033
$$

**Step 3.4.3 — Minimum measurable temperature change:**

The sensor produces **10 mV per °C**. The smallest distinguishable voltage is **4.88 mV**, so:

$$
\Delta T_{min} = \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} = \dfrac{4.88 \text{ mV}}{10 \text{ mV/°C}} \approx 0.488 \text{ °C}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> To achieve higher temperature resolution, you must use a higher-bit ADC (e.g., 12-bit gives 1.22 mV → 0.122 °C) or a lower reference voltage (e.g., 2.5 V instead of 5 V halves the step size).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Architecture Styles

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard | Modified Harvard | RISC | CISC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Memory** | Single, shared | Separate code/data | Separate but with shared features | N/A (CPU design) | N/A (CPU design) |
| **Bus** | 1 shared bus | 2 separate buses | Mixed | Internal | Internal |
| **Instruction Fetch & Data** | Sequential (one at a time) | Simultaneous (parallel) | Mostly parallel | 1 cycle/instruction | Variable cycles |
| **Speed** | Slower (bottleneck) | Faster | Fastest | Fast for given clock | Slower per clock, denser code |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low | High | High | Low | Very high |
| **Power** | Lower | Higher | Higher | Very low | Higher |
| **Example MCU/CPU** | 8086, x86 PC | 8051, AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M | ARM9, modern DSPs | ARM Cortex-M, RISC-V, MIPS | 8051, x86 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Strategy:** When asked to "select an architecture for an embedded application," always justify with: **power budget, real-time determinism, cost, and code density** — these are the four examiners' keywords.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 General Block Diagram of an Embedded System

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **top-level hardware architecture** of a typical embedded system, showing all five major blocks and their interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    POW[Power Supply and Clock Source] --> MCU[Central Block - Microcontroller or SoC]
    SENS[Input Sensors - Temperature, Light, Motion] --> MCU
    MCU --> ACT[Output Actuators - Motor, LED, Buzzer, Display]
    MCU --> COMM[Communication Peripherals - UART, SPI, I2C, Wi-Fi, CAN]
    EXT[External Storage - SD Card, Flash] --> MCU
    USER[User Interface - Keypad, Touch Screen, Buttons] --> MCU

    classDef coreBlock fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2545,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef ioBlock fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef commBlock fill:#b8860b,stroke:#8b6914,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef supportBlock fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#38006b,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    class MCU coreBlock
    class SENS,ACT,USER ioBlock
    class COMM commBlock
    class POW,EXT supportBlock
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **Central Block (MCU/SoC)** is the heart; everything connects to it.
- **Input Sensors** feed real-world analog/digital data into the MCU.
- **Output Actuators** receive control signals from the MCU to perform physical actions.
- **Communication Peripherals** allow data exchange with other systems.
- **Power Supply and Clock** are mandatory for any digital system to function.
- **External Storage** is optional, used when on-chip memory is insufficient.

---

## 4.2 Internal Architecture of a Microcontroller (MCU)

The following Mermaid diagram shows the **internal blocks of a generic microcontroller**, illustrating the Harvard architecture philosophy.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CPU[CPU Core - ALU, Registers, Control Unit] <--> BUS[System Bus - Address, Data, Control]
    BUS <--> FLASH[Program Memory - Flash or ROM - Non-Volatile]
    BUS <--> SRAM[Data Memory - SRAM - Volatile]
    BUS <--> EEPROM[EEPROM - User Persistent Data]
    BUS <--> IO[Parallel I/O Ports - GPIO]
    BUS <--> TIMER[Timers and Counters]
    BUS <--> UART[UART - Serial Port]
    BUS <--> SPI[SPI - Synchronous Serial]
    BUS <--> I2C[I2C - Two Wire Interface]
    BUS <--> ADC[ADC and DAC Converters]
    BUS <--> WDT[Watchdog Timer]
    BUS <--> INT[Interrupt Controller]
    BUS <--> RTC[Real Time Clock]

    classDef mem fill:#1565c0,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef cpu fill:#c62828,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px
    classDef peri fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    classDef bus fill:#6a1b9a,stroke:#4a148c,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px

    class CPU cpu
    class FLASH,SRAM,EEPROM mem
    class IO,TIMER,UART,SPI,I2C,ADC,WDT,INT,RTC peri
    class BUS bus
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip:** Notice the **System Bus** (purple) is the central spine. Memory blocks (blue) and peripherals (green) all share the same bus. In true Harvard architecture, there would be **two separate buses** — one for Flash access (instruction fetch) and another for SRAM access (data R/W). Most practical MCUs use a **Modified Harvard** compromise.

---

## 4.3 Von Neumann vs Harvard — Side-by-Side Mermaid Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VN[Von Neumann Architecture]
        V1[CPU] -- Single Shared Bus --> V2[Memory Unit - Holds Both Code and Data]
    end

    subgraph HV[Harvard Architecture]
        H1[CPU] -- Instruction Bus --> H2[Instruction Memory - Code Only]
        H1 -- Data Bus --> H3[Data Memory - Data Only]
    end

    classDef vnStyle fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000000
    classDef hvStyle fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000000
    classDef nodeStyle fill:#ffffff,stroke:#000000,color:#000000

    class V1,V2 vnStyle
    class H1,H2,H3 hvStyle
```

**Key Insight:**
- In **Von Neumann (left, red)**: One bus is a bottleneck — the CPU must wait if simultaneously fetching instruction and accessing data.
- In **Harvard (right, green)**: Two parallel buses allow **simultaneous** instruction fetch + data access → higher throughput, real-time friendly.

---

## 4.4 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid (Mermaid Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Level 1 - CPU Registers - Size 32 to 64 Words - Time 1 Cycle] --> L2[Level 2 - L1 Cache - Size 4 to 64 KB - Time 2 to 5 Cycles]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - L2 Cache or On Chip SRAM - Size 64 KB to 1 MB - Time 10 to 20 Cycles]
    L3 --> L4[Level 4 - Main Memory - DRAM or Off Chip SRAM - Size MB to GB - Time 50 to 200 Cycles]
    L4 --> L5[Level 5 - Secondary Storage - Flash SSD SD Card - Size GB to TB - Time 1000 to 1M Cycles]
    L5 --> L6[Level 6 - Tertiary Storage - Hard Disk Network Cloud - Size TB to PB - Time 10M Plus Cycles]

    classDef l1 fill:#b71c1c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef l2 fill:#e65100,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef l3 fill:#f9a825,color:#000000,stroke:#000000
    classDef l4 fill:#388e3c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef l5 fill:#1976d2,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef l6 fill:#6a1b9a,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000

    class L1 l1
    class L2 l2
    class L3 l3
    class L4 l4
    class L5 l5
    class L6 l6
```

**Reading the diagram:** As we go **down the pyramid**, **size increases** but **access speed decreases**. Embedded system designers must carefully allocate the most-frequently-used data in higher levels (faster) and bulk storage in lower levels (slower, larger).

---

## 4.5 Typical SoC Block Diagram (Modern IoT Edge Device)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    APPS[Application Processor Core - ARM Cortex A Series] --> INTC[Interconnect Fabric - NoC AMBA AXI]
    MC[Microcontroller Core - ARM Cortex M Series] --> INTC
    GPU[GPU - Graphics Processing Unit] --> INTC
    DSP[DSP - Digital Signal Processor] --> INTC
    NPU[NPU - Neural Processing Unit for AI] --> INTC
    INTC --> MEM[Memory Controller - DDR LPDDR Flash eMMC]
    INTC --> PERI[Peripheral Controllers - USB PCIe HDMI I2S I2C SPI UART]
    INTC --> WIRE[Wireless Radios - Wi-Fi 6 Bluetooth 5 5G Modem]
    INTC --> SEC[Security Engine - Crypto TRNG Secure Boot]
    INTC --> PMU[Power Management Unit - DVFS Clock Gating]
    INTC --> ISP[Image Signal Processor - Camera Pipeline]

    classDef cpuBlock fill:#c62828,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef bridgeBlock fill:#4527a0,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef featureBlock fill:#00695c,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000
    classDef powerBlock fill:#827717,color:#ffffff,stroke:#000000

    class APPS,MC,GPU,DSP,NPU cpuBlock
    class INTC,MEM bridgeBlock
    class PERI,WIRE,SEC,ISP featureBlock
    class PMU powerBlock
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Note:** The **NoC (Network-on-Chip)** is the modern equivalent of the system bus. It connects multiple heterogeneous cores efficiently. This is a **2024 Scheme** relevant addition — old syllabuses only mentioned shared buses.
