Always enable the bus clock *first*, then configure the GPIO.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Embedded Systems and Microcontrollers**, **Memory** refers to the physical electronic storage subsystem used by the microcontroller (MCU) or microprocessor (MPU) to permanently or temporarily hold the binary instructions (program code), data operands, and the stack frame required for real-time operation. Memory is organized as an array of addressable **register-cells (flip-flops or capacitors)**, each carrying one bit, and grouped into 8-bit bytes or 16/32-bit words.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim spirit):** *"Memory in an embedded system comprises volatile and non-volatile storage elements organized hierarchically. It is interfaced to the CPU through a tri-bus architecture (Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus) and is selected via hardware address-decoding logic."*

## 1.2 Classification of Memory (The Two Kingdoms)

Memory is broadly divided into two super-classes based on **power-retention behavior**:

| Class | Power Behavior | Typical Use in Embedded Systems |
|---|---|---|
| **Volatile Memory** | Loses contents when Vcc is removed | Temporary scratchpad, stack, heap, variables |
| **Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)** | Retains contents without power | Firmware storage, lookup tables, calibration data |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Desk" Model

Imagine your study table (a microcontroller development board):

- **The Notebook on the desk** = **Non-Volatile Memory (Flash/ROM)** → You close the book (power-off) and the notes are still there when you re-open.
- **The Whiteboard on the wall** = **Volatile Memory (SRAM/DRAM)** → Switch off the light (power-off) and everything is erased.
- **The Calculator's small register** = **CPU Registers** → The fastest, smallest "scratch space" right next to the CPU brain.
- **The Cupboard full of archived files** = **External Mass Storage (EEPROM / SD card)** → Slow to access, but holds huge amounts forever.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric — Access Time ($t_{acc}$):** The time delay between presenting a valid address on the address bus and the memory device placing valid data on the data bus. Typical values: **CPU Register ≈ 1–2 ns**, **On-chip SRAM ≈ 10–50 ns**, **External Flash ≈ 70–150 ns**.

## 1.4 KTU 2024 Cognitive Outcome Mapping

| CO ID | Course Outcome (Module 4 extract) | Bloom Level |
|---|---|---|
| **CO4** | Describe memory organization, addressing, and interfacing techniques with microcontrollers. | L2 – Understand |
| **CO5** | Design memory maps and address decoding circuits for embedded systems. | L4 – Analyze |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Pyramid of Memory

Embedded systems use a **Memory Hierarchy** to balance the conflicting goals of **speed**, **cost**, and **capacity**. As you move down the pyramid, capacity grows, but access time grows too.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Three Golden Rules of Memory Design (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> 1. Place the *most frequently accessed* data closest to the CPU.
> 2. Place the *largest and least critical* data far from the CPU.
> 3. Exploit **locality of reference** (temporal + spatial) to keep the hit ratio high.

## 2.2 Detailed Classification Breakdown

### A. Volatile Memory

1. **Static RAM (SRAM)**
   - Built using **6 transistors (6T) per bit cell** (two cross-coupled inverters + 2 access transistors).
   - Data is held *as long as power is applied* — no refresh needed.
   - Fastest volatile memory: access time **5–10 ns**.
   - Used as **cache (L1/L2)** and on-chip MCU RAM.

2. **Dynamic RAM (DRAM)**
   - Built using **1 transistor + 1 capacitor (1T1C) per bit**.
   - Capacitor leaks charge → must be **refreshed every ~64 ms**.
   - Cheaper and denser than SRAM, but slower (~50–70 ns).
   - Used in large external buffers (rare in small MCUs, common in Linux SoCs).

### B. Non-Volatile Memory (NVM)

1. **ROM (Mask ROM)** — Programmed at the **fabrication foundry**; cannot be modified.
2. **PROM (Programmable ROM)** — One-time programmable by the user via fuse-blowing.
3. **EPROM (Erasable PROM)** — Erasable by **UV light** through a quartz window (~20 minutes).
4. **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM)** — Byte-wise erasable electrically; ~1 million erase cycles.
5. **Flash Memory** — Block-wise (sector/page) erasable; foundation of modern MCU firmware storage. **NOR Flash** = random access (execute-in-place, XIP); **NAND Flash** = sequential (high density, SD cards).

## 2.3 Memory Organization Parameters

Three fundamental parameters govern how memory is mapped onto the CPU bus:

1. **Word Size (W)** — Width of the data bus in bits (typically 8, 16, or 32).
2. **Number of Address Lines (n)** — Width of the address bus.
3. **Total Addressable Locations** = $2^n$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter / Formula | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Total memory capacity | $C = 2^n \times W$ | bits | Size of memory chip |
| 2 | Number of addressable locations | $N = 2^n$ | locations | Number of unique bytes/words |
| 3 | Memory mapping range | $R = \text{End Address} - \text{Start Address} + 1$ | bytes | Address space occupied |
| 4 | Number of chips for expansion | $N_c = \dfrac{\text{Total Capacity}}{\text{Per-Chip Capacity}}$ | dimensionless | Hardware bill-of-materials |
| 5 | Address lines for capacity | $n = \log_2(N)$ | lines | PCB trace count |
| 6 | Address line splitting | $n = n_{\text{chip}} + n_{\text{decode}}$ | lines | Decoder design |
| 7 | Wait state insertion | $T_{ws} = \dfrac{t_{acc}^{\text{mem}} - t_{acc}^{\text{CPU}}}{T_{\text{clk}}}$ | integer | Timing synchronization |
| 8 | Refresh interval (DRAM) | $t_{ref} \le 64 \text{ ms}$ | ms | Data retention guarantee |
| 9 | Endurance (Flash/EEPROM) | $N_{cycles} \approx 10^5 \text{ – } 10^6$ | cycles | Lifetime estimation |
| 10 | Power retention (NVM) | $> 10 \text{ years @ } 85^\circ\text{C}$ | years | Data-sheet derating |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Trick — Never use `\|` inside markdown tables.** I have used $\vert$ or $\mid$ LaTeX commands to render absolute value without breaking table syntax.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Memory Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Automotive ECU (ABS, Airbag)** | NOR Flash + ECC SRAM | Deterministic boot (XIP), safety-critical reliability |
| **Wearable Fitness Band** | Internal Flash + Low-Power SRAM | Energy harvesting, deep-sleep retention |
| **IoT Edge Node** | External SPI Flash + SRAM | OTA firmware update, large logging buffer |
| **Smart Card / SIM** | EEPROM + ROM | Secure crypto-key storage, anti-tamper |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Total Memory Capacity

**Given:** A memory chip has $n = 15$ address lines and a word size $W = 8$ bits. Find the total capacity in **bytes** and **bits**.

**Step 1 — Identify the number of addressable locations.**

$$N = 2^n$$

$$N = 2^{15}$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the power of 2.**

$$2^{15} = 32768 \text{ locations}$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by word size to get total bits.**

$$C_{\text{bits}} = N \times W = 32768 \times 8$$

$$C_{\text{bits}} = 262144 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to bytes by dividing by 8.**

$$C_{\text{bytes}} = \dfrac{262144}{8} = 32768 \text{ bytes} = 32 \text{ KB}$$

**Conclusion:** A 15-line, 8-bit-wide chip stores **32 KB**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Address Lines for a Given Capacity

**Given:** A system designer needs $C = 64$ KB of memory with $W = 8$ bits. How many address lines are required?

**Step 1 — Compute number of locations.**

$$N = \dfrac{C}{W} = \dfrac{64 \times 1024 \times 8}{8} = 65536 \text{ locations}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the logarithmic relation.**

$$n = \log_2(N) = \log_2(65536)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the power of 2.**

$$2^{16} = 65536$$

$$n = 16 \text{ address lines}$$

**Conclusion:** **16 address lines** are needed to address 64 KB.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Memory Expansion (Chip Count Calculation)

**Given:** The target is 64 KB of memory using 16 KB × 8 chips. Find the number of chips.

**Step 1 — Calculate per-chip capacity in bytes.**

$$C_{\text{chip}} = 16 \text{ KB}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the chip-count formula.**

$$N_c = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{C_{\text{chip}}} = \dfrac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}}$$

$$N_c = 4 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 3 — Map address lines (14 for chip-internal + 2 for chip-select decode).**

$$n_{\text{internal}} = \log_2(16 \text{ K}) = 14$$

$$n_{\text{decode}} = \log_2(4) = 2$$

$$n_{\text{total}} = 14 + 2 = 16 \text{ lines} \quad \checkmark$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Wait State Calculation

**Given:** CPU clock period $T_{\text{clk}} = 125 \text{ ns}$ (8 MHz). External memory access time $t_{acc} = 200 \text{ ns}$. CPU bus cycle uses one clock period (no wait). Find the number of wait states.

**Step 1 — Express CPU's expected memory access window.**

$$t_{\text{CPU}} = T_{\text{clk}} = 125 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the deficit.**

$$\Delta t = t_{acc} - t_{\text{CPU}} = 200 - 125 = 75 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to integer wait states.**

$$T_{ws} = \dfrac{\Delta t}{T_{\text{clk}}} = \dfrac{75}{125}$$

$$T_{ws} = 0.6 \to \text{rounded up to } 1 \text{ wait state}$$

**Conclusion:** Insert **1 wait state** so the bus is stretched to $2 \times 125 = 250 \text{ ns} \ge 200 \text{ ns}$.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison Table for KTU

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM | Mask ROM | EPROM | EEPROM | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Volatile?** | Yes | Yes | No | No | No | No | No |
| **Cell elements** | 6T | 1T + 1C | Fuse / anti-fuse | Floating-gate MOS | Floating-gate MOS | Floating-gate MOS | Floating-gate MOS |
| **Read speed** | Very fast | Fast | Fast | Fast | Medium | Fast | Slow (sequential) |
| **Write speed** | Very fast | Fast (burst) | N/A | Slow | Medium | Medium (page) | Fast (page) |
| **Erase mechanism** | Electrical | Electrical | N/A | UV light | Electrical | Electrical (sector) | Electrical (block) |
| **Endurance (cycles)** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | N/A | Low | ~$10^6$ | ~$10^5$ | ~$10^5$ |
| **Density** | Low | High | Medium | Medium | Low | High | Very High |
| **Cost per bit** | High | Low | Very Low | Medium | High | Low | Very Low |
| **XIP support?** | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | Yes | **Yes** | No |
| **Typical embedded use** | Cache, MCU RAM | SoC main memory | Boot code | Legacy firmware | Calibration data | MCU firmware | Mass storage (SD) |

> [!NOTE]
> **XIP = Execute-In-Place.** The CPU can fetch instructions directly from NOR Flash without first copying them to RAM. This is the foundation of deterministic boot in automotive/industrial MCUs.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Memory Hierarchy Pyramid

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["REGISTERS inside CPU\n1 to 2 ns access time\nsize: 32 to 64 bytes"] --> B["L1 CACHE SRAM on chip\n2 to 5 ns\nsize: 4 to 64 KB"]
    B --> C["L2 CACHE / on chip SRAM\n5 to 20 ns\nsize: 64 KB to 1 MB"]
    C --> D["MAIN MEMORY DRAM or external SRAM\n50 to 100 ns\nsize: 1 MB to 1 GB"]
    D --> E["FLASH and NVM storage\n70 to 200 ns read\nsize: 1 MB to 1 GB"]
    E --> F["MASS STORAGE SD card or eMMC\nmilliseconds access\nsize: GB to TB"]
```

> **How to read this diagram:** Top of pyramid = fastest + smallest + costliest. Bottom = slowest + largest + cheapest. An embedded system designer must decide *where in this pyramid* each data block lives.

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of MCU Memory Subsystem

```mermaid
graph LR
    CPU["CPU CORE\nfetches and executes"] --> AB["ADDRESS BUS\nA0 to A15 or A0 to A31"]
    CPU --> DB["DATA BUS\nD0 to D7 or D0 to D31"]
    CPU --> CB["CONTROL BUS\nRD WR PSEN ALE"]

    AB --> DEC["ADDRESS DECODER\n74HC138 or GAL logic"]
    DEC --> CS1["CS of ROM chip"]
    DEC --> CS2["CS of RAM chip"]
    DEC --> CS3["CS of FLASH chip"]

    DB --> ROM["ROM or EPROM\nprogram memory"]
    DB --> RAM["SRAM\ndata memory"]
    DB --> FL["NOR FLASH\nfirmware storage"]

    CB --> ROM
    CB --> RAM
    CB --> FL
```

> **Engineering Insight:** The Address Decoder is the "traffic police" — it ensures that for any given address, *only one* memory chip activates its Chip Select (CS) line. The 74LS138 (3-to-8 line decoder) is the most common KTU-asked decoder.

## 4.3 Address Decoding — 3-to-8 Line Decoder for 8 Memory Blocks

```mermaid
graph TD
    IN["Higher Address Lines\nA13 A14 A15"] --> DEC3TO8["3 to 8 DECODER\n74LS138"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y0["Y0 selects ROM 1\n0000 to 1FFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y1["Y1 selects ROM 2\n2000 to 3FFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y2["Y2 selects RAM 1\n4000 to 5FFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y3["Y3 selects RAM 2\n6000 to 7FFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y4["Y4 selects IO port 1\n8000 to 9FFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y5["Y5 selects IO port 2\nA000 to BFFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y6["Y6 spare\nC000 to DFFF"]
    DEC3TO8 --> Y7["Y7 spare\nE000 to FFFF"]
```

> **KTU Exam Tip:** When A15 A14 A13 = `000`, Y0 is asserted low, selecting ROM 1. The lower 13 lines A0–A12 address 8 KB inside the chip.

## 4.4 Write Cycle Timing Waveform (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph Write_Cycle["MEMORY WRITE CYCLE TIMING"]
        T1["T1: CPU places address on bus"] --> T2["T2: CPU asserts WR low"]
        T2 --> T3["T3: data is latched by memory"]
        T3 --> T4["T4: WR goes high, cycle ends"]
    end
```

> **Pitfall Alert:** If the memory device's $t_{DS}$ (Data Setup time) > CPU's hold time, a **wait state** must be inserted between T2 and T3. This is a favourite KTU 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory. Give one example of each used in 8051 microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Volatile Memory | Non-Volatile Memory |
|---|---|---|
| **Power dependency** | Loses data on power-off | Retains data without power |
| **Read/Write** | Read + Write | Mostly Read; Write is slow/special |
| **Speed** | Faster (SRAM) | Slower (Flash) |
| **Use case in 8051** | Internal 128/256 bytes of data RAM (0x00–0x7F / 0x00–0xFF) | Internal 4 KB / 8 KB Flash program memory |

**Example:** Volatile → **Internal SRAM (0x00–0x7F)**. Non-Volatile → **Internal Flash ROM (0x0000–0x0FFF for 4 KB 8051)**.

*(3 marks: 1 mark for definition, 1 for differentiation, 1 for example)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is Execute-In-Place (XIP)? Which type of Flash memory supports it and why?**

**Model Answer:**
**XIP (Execute-In-Place)** is a memory architecture feature where the CPU fetches and executes instructions *directly* from non-volatile memory, without first copying them to RAM.
**NOR Flash** supports XIP because it provides:
1. **Random byte-level access** (similar to SRAM) using the standard address and data bus.
2. **Fast read access time** (~70–100 ns) suitable for CPU fetch cycles.
3. **Byte-wise addressability** — unlike NAND Flash, which is page/block oriented and sequential.

**NAND Flash** does *not* support XIP because it requires a controller and sequential page access.

*(3 marks: 1 XIP definition, 1 for NOR reason, 1 for NAND exclusion)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> **KTU Rule (2024 Scheme):** *"Answer ANY ONE full question from each module. Each Part B question carries 14 marks and must have two sub-parts of 7 marks each."*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal memory organization of the 8051 microcontroller. List the special function registers (SFRs) and their addresses.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An 8051-based system is to be expanded to **64 KB of external program memory** and **32 KB of external data memory** using **16 KB × 8 EPROM chips** and **8 KB × 8 SRAM chips** respectively. Design the address decoding scheme using a 74LS138. Show the address map.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A**

#### Part (a) — 8051 Internal Memory Organization

**Step 1 — Top-level split:** The 8051 has a **Harvard architecture** with two physically separate address spaces:

- **Program Memory (Code ROM)** — 64 KB max, accessed via `PSEN` signal.
- **Data Memory (RAM + SFR)** — 64 KB max, accessed via `RD` and `WR` signals.

**Step 2 — Internal program memory:** 4 KB (in 8051) or 8 KB (in 8052) of on-chip Flash, addresses **0x0000 to 0x0FFF** (or 0x1FFF). External program memory begins at 0x0000 and overlaps; the `EA` pin selects internal vs external.

**Step 3 — Internal data memory split:**

| Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lower RAM (00–7F)** | 0x00 – 0x7F | 128 B | Register banks (00–1F), bit-addressable (20–2F), general (30–7F) |
| **SFR (80–FF)** | 0x80 – 0xFF | 128 B | Control & status registers |

**Step 4 — Key SFRs (must memorize for KTU):**

| SFR | Address | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **ACC** | 0xE0 | Accumulator |
| **B** | 0xF0 | B register (multiplication/division) |
| **PSW** | 0xD0 | Program Status Word |
| **SP** | 0x81 | Stack Pointer |
| **DPTR (DPH+DPL)** | 0x83 / 0x82 | Data Pointer (16-bit) |
| **P0–P3** | 0x80, 0x90, 0xA0, 0xB0 | I/O Ports |
| **TCON, TMOD, TH0, TL0** | 0x88, 0x89, 0x8C, 0x8A | Timer control |
| **SCON, SBUF** | 0x98, 0x99 | Serial port |
| **IE, IP** | 0xA8, 0xB8 | Interrupt enable / priority |

*(7 marks: 2 for Harvard split, 2 for internal RAM, 2 for SFR table, 1 for program memory)*

---

#### Part (b) — Address Decoding Design

**Step 1 — Compute chip counts.**

$$N_{\text{EPROM}} = \dfrac{64 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

$$N_{\text{SRAM}} = \dfrac{32 \text{ KB}}{8 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

**Step 2 — Determine internal address lines per chip.**

$$n_{\text{EPROM}} = \log_2(16 \times 1024) = 14 \text{ lines (A0–A13)}$$

$$n_{\text{SRAM}} = \log_2(8 \times 1024) = 13 \text{ lines (A0–A12)}$$

**Step 3 — Determine decoder input lines.**

$$n_{\text{decode}} = \log_2(4) = 2 \text{ lines}$$

**Step 4 — Use A15 and A14 as decoder inputs (74LS138 has 3 inputs; tie A13 HIGH or use gating).**

| Chip | CS Line | Address Range | Size |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EPROM 1** | Y0 (`00`) | 0x0000 – 0x3FFF | 16 KB |
| **EPROM 2** | Y1 (`01`) | 0x4000 – 0x7FFF | 16 KB |
| **EPROM 3** | Y2 (`10`) | 0x8000 – 0xBFFF | 16 KB |
| **EPROM 4** | Y3 (`11`) | 0xC000 – 0xFFFF | 16 KB |
| **SRAM 1** | Y4 (via A15=0, A14=0, A13=1 gated) | 0x2000 – 0x3FFF | 8 KB |
| ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Step 5 — The 74LS138** has inputs C, B, A mapping to A15, A14, A13. Outputs Y0–Y7 go low when the corresponding input combination matches. The active-low CS of each chip is wired to one Y-output.

**Step 6 — Address Map Table (final answer):**

| Decoder Output | Chip | Address Range (Hex) |
|---|---|---|
| Y0 | EPROM 1 | 0000H – 3FFFH |
| Y1 | EPROM 2 | 4000H – 7FFFH |
| Y2 | EPROM 3 | 8000H – BFFFH |
| Y3 | EPROM 4 | C000H – FFFFH |
| Y4 | SRAM 1 | (Data memory) |
| Y5 | SRAM 2 | ... |
| Y6 | SRAM 3 | ... |
| Y7 | SRAM 4 | ... |

*(7 marks: 2 for chip count, 2 for decoder inputs, 2 for address map, 1 for chip-select logic)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE** *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the difference between **NOR and NAND Flash memory** in detail. Justify why NOR Flash is preferred for storing firmware in embedded systems.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An embedded system has a 16-bit address bus and 8-bit data bus. Calculate:
   (i) Total addressable memory.
   (ii) Number of address lines required for a 1 MB memory.
   (iii) Number of 256 KB × 8 chips needed to design a 1 MB memory system. Design the address map.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B**

#### Part (a) — NOR vs NAND Flash

| Feature | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|---|---|---|
| **Cell connection** | Cells in **parallel** (like NOR gate) | Cells in **series** (like NAND gate) |
| **Random access** | Yes — byte-wise | No — page/block only |
| **Read speed** | Fast (~70 ns) | Slower (page load first) |
| **Write speed** | Slow | Fast |
| **Erase** | Sector-wise | Block-wise (larger) |
| **Endurance** | ~100K cycles | ~100K cycles |
| **XIP support** | **Yes** | No |
| **Density per cell** | Lower | Higher (cheaper per bit) |
| **Use case** | Firmware, BIOS, boot code | SSD, SD cards, mass storage |

**Why NOR for firmware:** Deterministic random-access read latency, ability to execute code directly (XIP), and high reliability make NOR Flash the gold standard for storing bootloaders and application firmware.

*(7 marks: 4 for comparison table, 3 for justification)*

---

#### Part (b) — Memory Calculations

**(i) Total addressable memory with 16-bit address bus and 8-bit data bus:**

$$N = 2^{16} = 65536 \text{ locations}$$

$$C = N \times W = 65536 \times 8 \text{ bits} = 524288 \text{ bits} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

**(ii) Address lines for 1 MB memory:**

$$N = \dfrac{1 \text{ MB}}{1 \text{ byte}} = 2^{20} \text{ locations}$$

$$n = \log_2(2^{20}) = 20 \text{ address lines}$$

**(iii) Number of 256 KB × 8 chips for 1 MB system:**

$$N_c = \dfrac{1 \text{ MB}}{256 \text{ KB}} = 4 \text{ chips}$$

**Address Map Design:**

| Chip | Internal Address Lines | Decoder Input (A19 A18) | Address Range (Hex) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Chip 1** | A0–A17 (18 lines) | `00` | 0x00000 – 0x3FFFF |
| **Chip 2** | A0–A17 | `01` | 0x40000 – 0x7FFFF |
| **Chip 3** | A0–A17 | `10` | 0x80000 – 0xBFFFF |
| **Chip 4** | A0–A17 | `11` | 0xC0000 – 0xFFFFF |

A **2-to-4 line decoder** (or 74LS139) takes A19 and A18 and asserts the CS of one chip at a time.

*(7 marks: 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii) including address map)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing KB (kilobyte = 1024) with kB (kilobit-byte = 1000)** — Always use $2^{10} = 1024$.
> 2. **Forgetting that the 8051 SFR region (0x80–0xFF) is *separate* from internal data RAM (0x00–0x7F)** — they share the same address bus but are accessed via different instructions.
> 3. **Incorrect rounding of wait states** — Always round *up* (ceiling) when memory is slower than CPU.
> 4. **Mixing up Harvard vs Von Neumann** — 8051 is Harvard (separate code + data buses), 8086 is Von Neumann.
> 5. **Not labelling the 74LS138 outputs as active-LOW** — In KTU diagrams, CS lines with an overbar must be marked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Volatile vs Non-Volatile** is the *first* classification — RAM loses data, ROM/Flash retains it.
- **Memory Hierarchy** balances speed, cost, and capacity: Registers → Cache → SRAM → DRAM → Flash → Mass Storage.
- **SRAM = 6T cell, no refresh, fast.** **DRAM = 1T + 1C, needs refresh every 64 ms, dense.**
- **ROM → PROM → EPROM → EEPROM → Flash** is the evolution of non-volatile programmability.
- **NOR Flash** supports **XIP** (Execute-In-Place) — the basis of deterministic boot.
- **NAND Flash** is *block-oriented*, denser, and used for **mass storage** (SD cards, SSDs).
- **Address capacity formula:** $C = 2^n \times W$ (bits).
- **Address lines required:** $n = \log_2(N)$, where $N$ is the number of locations.
- **Chip count for expansion:** $N_c = C_{\text{total}} / C_{\text{chip}}$.
- **Address decoding** uses gates or decoder ICs (74LS138, 74LS139) to generate the **active-low CS** for each chip.
- **Wait states** are integer clock-cycle stretches inserted by the CPU when external memory is slower.
- **8051 has Harvard architecture** — 64 KB code + 64 KB data spaces, accessed by **PSEN** vs **RD/WR** respectively.
- **Key 8051 SFRs** to memorize: ACC (E0H), B (F0H), PSW (D0H), SP (81H), DPTR (82–83H), P0–P3 (80/90/A0/B0H).
- **Internal 8051 RAM regions:** Register Banks 0–3 (00–1FH), Bit-addressable (20–2FH), General-purpose (30–7FH).
- **EA pin** = External Access; when LOW, the 8051 fetches from external program memory only.
- **Decoder active levels:** 74LS138 outputs are **active LOW** — always draw a small bubble on the CS pin in your circuit diagram.
- **Endurance** of Flash/EEPROM is finite (~10⁵ to 10⁶ cycles) — design for wear-levelling if writing frequently.
- **Data retention** of NVM is guaranteed for **>10 years at 85 °C** by most vendors (Micron, Winbond, ST).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Programming Interface: Arduino IDE
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Programming Interface: Arduino IDE

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Arduino Integrated Development Environment (Arduino IDE)** is a cross-platform, open-source application written in Java and Processing that provides a unified graphical front-end for **editing, compiling, and uploading firmware (sketches)** to Arduino-based microcontroller boards. 

In the KTU 2024 OECPT721 syllabus context, the Arduino IDE serves as the canonical **Programming Interface** that bridges the developer's high-level C/C++ code with the low-level AVR (or ARM Cortex-M0+) instruction set executed by the on-board microcontroller. It abstracts the underlying complexities of the toolchain — `avr-gcc` compiler, `avr-libc` standard library, and `avrdude` programmer — into a single clickable workflow.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The Arduino IDE is examined under the broader umbrella of *Embedded System Programming Interfaces*. Students must know the IDE architecture, the **sketch lifecycle** (`setup()` and `loop()`), compilation pipeline, library management system, and the Board Manager — these directly map to **CO3: Develop embedded programs using standard IDEs and toolchains.**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of the Arduino IDE as a **fully equipped digital workshop** for microcontroller programming.

| Workshop Element | Arduino IDE Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Workbench / desk | The code editor window |
| Toolbox (screwdrivers, pliers) | The Library Manager (pre-built code modules) |
| Blueprint / plan | The **sketch** (your `.ino` source file) |
| Power tools (drill, saw) | The compiler (`avr-gcc`) |
| Delivery truck | The uploader (`avrdude`) carrying the binary to the board |
| Supervisor / foreman | The Serial Monitor for real-time debugging |
| Inventory of parts | The Board Manager (Uno, Nano, Mega, ESP32, etc.) |

When a beginner first opens the IDE, the experience is deliberately similar to a **simple text editor (like Notepad)** — but hidden beneath this deceptively simple interface is a complete GCC cross-compilation toolchain that translates your human-readable `setup()` and `loop()` blocks into machine code that the ATmega328P (or equivalent) microcontroller understands.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Sketch size limit:** Typically **32 KB of flash** for ATmega328P-based boards (e.g., Arduino Uno).
> - **Bootloader size:** **512 bytes** reserved at the top of flash memory.
> - **Default IDE version (2024):** Arduino IDE 2.x (Electron-based) supersedes the legacy 1.8.x (Java-based) version.

## 1.3 Why the Arduino IDE Matters in KTU Examinations

The Arduino IDE is more than just a software tool — it is a **representative example of a vendor-locked, abstracted embedded toolchain**. The KTU 2024 scheme deliberately introduces students to this environment because:

1. It hides the complexity of **cross-compilation** for AVR/ARM microcontrollers.
2. It demonstrates the **fetch → compile → upload → monitor** loop that is universal to almost every embedded IDE (Keil, MPLAB X, STM32CubeIDE).
3. It provides hands-on exposure to **libraries, APIs, and hardware abstraction** — concepts essential for module-end viva/practical exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Architectural Anatomy of the Arduino IDE

The IDE is structurally divided into **three functional layers**:

### Layer 1: User Interface (UI) Layer
- **Editor Pane:** Where you write the sketch. Features syntax highlighting, auto-indent, and bracket matching.
- **Toolbar Buttons (Verify, Upload, New, Open, Save, Serial Monitor).**
- **Status Bar:** Displays compilation errors, board port, and program size.
- **Tabs:** Multi-file sketch support (`.ino`, `.h`, `.cpp`).

### Layer 2: Build System / Toolchain Layer
When you click **Verify (✓)** or **Upload (→)**, the IDE orchestrates a sequence of background processes:

1. **Preprocessing:** Concatenates all `.ino` files in the sketch folder into a single translation unit and prepends `#include "Arduino.h"`.
2. **Compilation:** Invokes `avr-gcc` (for AVR boards) to produce an ELF object file.
3. **Assembly:** `avr-as` converts assembly instructions to relocatable object code.
4. **Linking:** `avr-ld` (or `avr-gcc` driver) links object files with `avr-libc` (the C standard library for AVR) and the Arduino core library (`core.a`).
5. **ELF → HEX conversion:** `avr-objcopy` produces the final **Intel HEX (.hex)** file containing the machine code.
6. **Uploading:** `avrdude` opens the serial port (e.g., `/dev/ttyUSB0` on Linux, `COM3` on Windows) and streams the `.hex` file into the board's flash via the **optiboot bootloader** using the STK500 protocol.

### Layer 3: Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)
The **Arduino Core** (`cores/arduino/`) provides the standardized C++ classes — `Serial`, `digitalWrite()`, `analogRead()`, `millis()`, `delay()` — that abstract away direct register manipulation. This is why a beginner can blink an LED without writing a single line of AVR assembly or configuring DDR/PORT registers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Core Hidden Files:** Under the hood, every Arduino sketch is actually a **C++ program**. The IDE silently generates a function prototype for any function you define *after* `loop()`, because C++ requires forward declarations. This is why `void blinkLED()` defined after `loop()` still works — a classic viva question!

## 2.2 The Sketch Lifecycle: `setup()` and `loop()`

The Arduino execution model is fundamentally different from a standard desktop C/C++ program. There is **no `main()` function** in the user's source code. Instead, the IDE supplies a hidden `main.cpp` (located in `cores/arduino/main.cpp`) that looks conceptually like this:

```cpp
int main(void) {
    init();                  // Initialize hardware timers, ADC, etc.
    setup();                 // User-defined initialization (runs ONCE)
    for (;;) {               // Infinite loop
        loop();              // User-defined repeating logic
    }
    return 0;
}
```

### Functional Breakdown:
- **`void setup()`** — Executes **exactly once** at power-on or reset. Used for: pin modes, `Serial.begin()`, sensor initialization, attaching interrupts.
- **`void loop()`** — Executes **repeatedly in an infinite `for(;;)` cycle** at the CPU clock rate (16 MHz for Uno). Used for: polling sensors, state machines, communication tasks.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world impact:** If `loop()` contains a blocking `delay(1000)`, the CPU is held idle for that second. The KTU examiner may ask how to achieve *non-blocking timing* using `millis()` — this is a **Module 4 high-priority question**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Symbol | Boundary / Limit | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sketch Flash Limit (Uno) | $F_{max} = 32{,}256$ bytes | $-512$ bytes bootloader | Bytes |
| SRAM (Uno) | $S_{RAM} = 2{,}048$ | $-317$ bootloader | Bytes |
| EEPROM (Uno) | $E_{EEP} = 1{,}024$ | 100,000 write cycles | Bytes / cycles |
| CPU Clock | $f_{CLK} = 16 \times 10^6$ | $\pm 1\%$ accuracy | Hz |
| `millis()` rollover | $t_{roll} = 2^{32} \approx 4.29 \times 10^9$ | After ~49.7 days | Milliseconds |
| ADC Resolution | $V_{step} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}-1} = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{1023}$ | 10-bit default | Volts per LSB |
| PWM Frequency (default) | $f_{PWM} \approx 490$ Hz (pins 3,9,10,11); $\approx 980$ Hz (pins 5,6) | Pins 5 and 6 doubled | Hz |
| Baud Rate Error Tolerance | $E_{baud} = \dfrac{\vert f_{actual} - f_{ideal} \vert}{f_{ideal}} \times 100$ | Must be $\lt 2\%$ for reliable UART | Percent |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility:** The PWM frequency formula and ADC step formula are **directly examinable** in Part B questions. Memorize the $V_{ref}/1023$ derivation — it is one of the most frequently asked derivations in OECPT721.

## 2.4 IDE Menus and Functional Roles (Examiner's Focus)

| Menu | Key Functions | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **File** | New, Open, Save, Examples (built-in library demos) | Demonstrates library usage |
| **Edit** | Cut/Copy/Paste, Find/Replace, Comment/Uncomment (`Ctrl+/`) | Productivity tools |
| **Sketch** | Verify/Compile, Upload, Include Library, Add File, Show Sketch Folder | Reveals toolchain pipeline |
| **Tools** | Board selection, Port selection, Programmer, Burn Bootloader | Board Manager concept |
| **Help** | Reference, Getting Started, Troubleshooting | Self-learning resource |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The Arduino IDE's abstracted approach is foundational to **rapid prototyping** in industries such as:
- **IoT product development** (ESP32, ESP8266 boards).
- **Wearable electronics** (Arduino Nano, LilyPad).
- **Robotics education** (Arduino Mega + motor shields).
- **Industrial sensor prototyping** (Modbus, CAN bus libraries).

Although production-grade embedded systems migrate to professional IDEs (Keil µVision, STM32CubeIDE, MPLAB X), the **conceptual workflow — write → compile → flash → debug** — remains identical.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: ADC Step Size (10-bit Resolution)

The ATmega328P's ADC is a **successive approximation register (SAR)** type with a default resolution of **10 bits**. This means the analog input range is divided into $2^{10} = 1024$ discrete digital codes (0 to 1023).

### Given:
- Reference voltage: $V_{ref} = 5.0 \text{ V}$ (default for Arduino Uno)
- ADC resolution: $n = 10$ bits

### Step 1: Total number of quantization levels
$$N_{levels} = 2^n = 2^{10} = 1024$$

### Step 2: Number of intervals between codes
Since digital codes range from 0 to $2^n - 1$, the number of intervals is:
$$N_{intervals} = 2^n - 1 = 1023$$

### Step 3: Voltage per Least Significant Bit (LSB)
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{N_{intervals}} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n - 1}$$

Substituting values:
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{5.0 \text{ V}}{1023} \approx 4.888 \text{ mV per LSB}$$

### Step 4: Digital-to-Analog conversion (reconstruction)
For a digital reading $D$, the equivalent analog voltage is:
$$V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{1023}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $2^n - 1$ and not $2^n$?** This is a classic trap! The 10-bit ADC produces codes **0 through 1023** (which is 1024 codes total), but the *width* of each step is determined by the *number of intervals* between adjacent codes, which is $1023$. This is a guaranteed KTU Part A or Part B sub-question.

## 3.2 Derivation: PWM Frequency for ATmega328P

The PWM output on Arduino Uno pins 5 and 6 uses **Timer0** with a prescaler of $64$ and a TOP value of $255$ (Fast PWM, 8-bit mode).

### Given:
- System clock: $f_{CLK} = 16 \text{ MHz}$
- Prescaler: $N = 64$
- Timer TOP value: $TOP = 255$ (for 8-bit PWM)

### Step 1: Timer tick frequency
$$f_{tick} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{N} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{64} = 250{,}000 \text{ Hz}$$

### Step 2: PWM frequency
The timer overflows (and resets the PWM cycle) every $(TOP + 1)$ ticks:
$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{tick}}{TOP + 1} = \frac{250{,}000}{256}$$

$$f_{PWM} \approx 976.5625 \text{ Hz} \approx 980 \text{ Hz}$$

> This matches the IDE's documented value of **~980 Hz** for pins 5 and 6.

For pins 3, 9, 10, 11 (using Timer1 or Timer2 with TOP = 255, prescaler = 64):
$$f_{PWM} = \frac{16 \times 10^6}{64 \times 256} \approx 976.5625 \text{ Hz}$$

Wait — for standard Uno, pins 3, 9, 10, 11 actually use a prescaler of 64, but Timer1 has 16-bit capability. The IDE configures it to **Fast PWM 8-bit mode** with prescaler 64, yielding $\approx 490$ Hz due to phase-correct mode. The simplified formula remains:
$$f_{PWM} = \frac{f_{CLK}}{N \times (TOP + 1)}$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: IDE Build Process (Step-by-Step)

| Step | Tool Invoked | Input | Output | File Extension |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Sketch Preprocessor** | All `.ino` files in folder | Concatenated C++ source | (in-memory string) |
| 2 | **Preprocessor** (`cpp`) | Concatenated source + `Arduino.h` | Expanded source with macros resolved | `.i` (intermediate) |
| 3 | **Compiler** (`avr-gcc`) | Expanded C++ source | Assembly code | `.s` |
| 4 | **Assembler** (`avr-as`) | Assembly code | Relocatable object code | `.o` |
| 5 | **Linker** (`avr-ld` via `avr-gcc`) | Object files + `core.a` + `libc.a` | ELF executable | `.elf` |
| 6 | **Object Copy** (`avr-objcopy`) | ELF executable | Intel HEX | `.hex` |
| 7 | **Uploader** (`avrdude`) | Intel HEX + serial port | Written to flash via STK500 protocol | (programmed into MCU) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state that the IDE compiles the sketch into a `.exe` or `.bin` file. The **correct final output** that gets uploaded to the board is the **Intel HEX (`.hex`)** file, generated from the ELF via `avr-objcopy`. The binary `.bin` is only used for newer ARM-based boards (e.g., Arduino Due, ESP32).

## 3.4 The `millis()` Rollover Analysis

The `millis()` function returns the number of milliseconds since the Arduino began running the current program, stored in an `unsigned long` (32-bit) variable.

### Step 1: Maximum value of unsigned 32-bit integer
$$t_{max} = 2^{32} - 1 = 4{,}294{,}967{,}295 \text{ ms}$$

### Step 2: Conversion to days
$$t_{days} = \frac{2^{32} - 1}{1000 \times 60 \times 60 \times 24} \approx 49.71 \text{ days}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for KTU answer:** Naive code like `if (millis() - lastTime > interval)` works correctly across the rollover **only because of unsigned integer arithmetic wrap-around** (modulo $2^{32}$). Writing `if (millis() > lastTime + interval)` is a common bug — the KTU examiner will penalize this. This is a classic **CO3 / CO4 level question** (Apply / Analyze).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Arduino IDE Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of the Arduino IDE, showing the interactions between the UI, toolchain, and hardware layers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    U1[User clicks Verify or Upload] --> P1[Sketch Preprocessor]
    P1 --> P2[Concatenate all .ino files]
    P2 --> P3[Inject Arduino.h header]
    P3 --> C1[avr-gcc Compiler]
    C1 --> A1[avr-as Assembler]
    A1 --> L1[avr-ld Linker]
    L1 --> O1[avr-objcopy ELF to HEX]
    O1 --> D1[avrdude Uploader]
    D1 --> H1[Arduino Board Flash Memory]
    H1 --> M1[Serial Monitor Debug Output]
    C1 --> E1[Error and Warning Messages]
    E1 --> U1
    L1 --> L2[Link with core.a and libc.a]
    L2 --> L1
    A1 --> A2[Object Files .o]
    A2 --> L1
    D1 --> P2a[Serial Port COM or ttyUSB]
    P2a --> D1
```

## 4.2 Sketch Execution Lifecycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Power On or Reset] --> B[Hardware Init by bootloader]
    B --> C[Call hidden main function]
    C --> D[init function configures timers ADC etc]
    D --> E[Call user defined setup function]
    E --> F[setup executes exactly once]
    F --> G[Enter infinite for loop]
    G --> H[Call user defined loop function]
    H --> I[loop body executes]
    I --> J{loop returns}
    J --> K[Return to top of for loop]
    K --> H
    J -.-> G
```

## 4.3 IDE Build and Upload Pipeline (Detailed Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[sketch.ino File 1] --> M[Merge into single TU]
    S2[sketch.ino File 2] --> M
    S3[header.h File] --> M
    M --> C2[Preprocess cpp]
    C2 --> C3[Compile to asm via avr-gcc]
    C3 --> C4[Assemble to object via avr-as]
    C4 --> L3[Link with Arduino core and libc]
    L3 --> E2[Generate ELF executable]
    E2 --> O2[Convert ELF to Intel HEX]
    O2 --> U2[avrdude streams HEX over UART]
    U2 --> F1[Flash written to ATmega328P]
    F1 --> R1[Reset vector triggers sketch start]
```

## 4.4 Memory Map of ATmega328P (Sketch Storage)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0[Flash Memory 32 KB total] --> M1[0x0000 Interrupt Vectors]
    M1 --> M2[0x002A Sketch Code]
    M2 --> M3[0x7FFE End of Sketch]
    M3 --> M4[0x7FFF Bootloader Section 512 bytes]
    S0[SRAM 2 KB total] --> S1[0x0100 Registers and I O]
    S1 --> S2[0x0100 to 0x08FF Static and Heap]
    S2 --> S3[Stack grows downward]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]
**Q: Define the Arduino IDE. List any four of its key features.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** The Arduino IDE is a cross-platform, open-source desktop application used to write, compile, and upload code (called *sketches*) to Arduino microcontroller boards.
- **Key features (2 marks — any four):**
  1. Cross-platform support (Windows, macOS, Linux).
  2. Built-in library manager for adding hardware drivers and protocol stacks.
  3. One-click **Verify** (compile) and **Upload** (flash) workflow.
  4. Integrated **Serial Monitor** for real-time debugging.
  5. **Board Manager** supporting Uno, Nano, Mega, ESP32, and hundreds of third-party boards.
  6. Extensive **built-in examples** accessible from `File → Examples`.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]
**Q: Differentiate between the `setup()` and `loop()` functions in an Arduino sketch.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
| Aspect | `setup()` | `loop()` |
|---|---|---|
| **Invocation** | Called **once** at startup/reset | Called **repeatedly** in an infinite loop |
| **Purpose** | Initialization of pins, peripherals, communication | Continuous execution of main logic |
| **Return type** | `void` | `void` |
| **Typical use** | `pinMode()`, `Serial.begin()`, sensor init | `digitalRead()`, state machine, `delay()` |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]

**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of the Arduino IDE** and describe the **compilation-to-upload pipeline** step by step. Name the tools invoked at each stage.

**(b) [7 marks]** Derive the **ADC step size** for a 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage of $5.0 \text{ V}$. If the digital reading from `analogRead(A0)` is $427$, calculate the corresponding input voltage.

#### Model Solution to (a):
**Block diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.1 above; describe the UI layer, Build System layer, and HAL.)

**Compilation-to-Upload pipeline (7 marks):**
1. **(1 mark)** **Sketch Preprocessing:** The IDE concatenates all `.ino` files in the sketch folder into a single translation unit and prepends `#include "Arduino.h"`.
2. **(1 mark)** **Preprocessing:** The C preprocessor (`cpp`) expands all `#include` and macro directives.
3. **(1 mark)** **Compilation:** `avr-gcc` translates the expanded C++ source into AVR assembly.
4. **(1 mark)** **Assembly:** `avr-as` converts the assembly code into relocatable object files (`.o`).
5. **(1 mark)** **Linking:** `avr-ld` (driven by `avr-gcc`) links the object files with the Arduino core library (`core.a`) and the AVR C standard library (`libc.a`), producing an ELF executable.
6. **(1 mark)** **Object Copy:** `avr-objcopy` extracts the program bytes from the ELF and produces an **Intel HEX (`.hex`)** file.
7. **(1 mark)** **Uploading:** `avrdude` opens the serial port (e.g., `COM3`) and uses the **STK500 protocol** to stream the `.hex` file into the ATmega328P's flash memory via the **optiboot bootloader**.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 (1 mark):** Number of discrete digital codes produced by a 10-bit ADC:
$$N_{codes} = 2^{10} = 1024 \text{ codes (0 to 1023)}$$

**Step 2 (1 mark):** Number of voltage intervals (the divisor for step size):
$$N_{intervals} = 2^{10} - 1 = 1023$$

**Step 3 (2 marks):** ADC step size (voltage per LSB):
$$V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0 \text{ V}}{1023} \approx 4.888 \text{ mV/LSB}$$

**Step 4 (2 marks):** Input voltage corresponding to digital reading $D = 427$:
$$V_{in} = D \times V_{LSB} = 427 \times \frac{5.0}{1023}$$
$$V_{in} = 427 \times 0.004888 \approx 2.087 \text{ V}$$

**Step 5 (1 mark):** Conclusion — the analog input is approximately **2.09 V**, well within the 0–5 V range.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the **role of the Arduino bootloader**. How does `avrdude` interact with the bootloader to flash a sketch? What happens if the bootloader is corrupted?

**(b) [7 marks]** A student writes the following sketch intended to blink an LED every 1 second without using `delay()`. Identify the **bug** and rewrite the corrected version. Justify each line.

```cpp
unsigned long lastTime = 0;
void setup() {
  pinMode(13, OUTPUT);
}
void loop() {
  if (millis() > lastTime + 1000) {
    digitalWrite(13, HIGH);
    delay(50);  // <-- intentional issue
    digitalWrite(13, LOW);
    lastTime = millis();
  }
}
```

#### Model Solution to (a):
**Role of the bootloader (3 marks):**
- The **optiboot bootloader** is a small (512-byte) program pre-loaded in the **top of flash memory** at address `0x7E00` (for ATmega328P).
- When the Arduino resets, control jumps to the bootloader, which listens on the UART for new sketch data using the **STK500 protocol**.
- If new data is received within a short timeout (~1 second), it overwrites the application flash and reboots into the new sketch.
- If no data arrives, it jumps to the existing sketch's reset vector (`0x0000`) and runs the previously uploaded program.

**`avrdude` interaction (2 marks):**
- The IDE invokes `avrdude -c arduino -p atmega328p -P COM3 -b 115200 -U flash:w:sketch.hex`.
- The `-c arduino` flag selects the STK500 protocol over serial.
- The bootloader receives each HEX record and writes it page-by-page (64-byte pages) into internal flash.

**Bootloader corruption (2 marks):**
- If the bootloader is corrupted (e.g., by an accidental full-chip erase or a faulty ISP programmer), the board **cannot self-reprogram** via USB.
- Recovery: use an **ISP programmer** (e.g., USBasp, Arduino as ISP) to re-burn the bootloader via `Tools → Burn Bootloader`.
- This is also why `Tools → Burn Bootloader` exists in the IDE.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Bug identification (3 marks):**
The `if (millis() > lastTime + 1000)` condition fails after **~49.7 days** when `millis()` rolls over from $2^{32} - 1$ back to $0$. At that moment, `lastTime` holds a large value (e.g., $4 \times 10^9$), and `lastTime + 1000` *overflows* the unsigned long, wrapping to a small number. Subsequently, `millis()` (now near 0) is *less than* this wrapped sum, so the condition is **false for 49+ days** — the LED stops blinking. Additionally, using `delay(50)` inside the `if` block is acceptable here, but a true non-blocking design would eliminate it.

**Corrected sketch (3 marks):**
```cpp
unsigned long lastTime = 0;
const unsigned long interval = 1000;

void setup() {
  pinMode(13, OUTPUT);
}

void loop() {
  unsigned long current = millis();
  if (current - lastTime >= interval) {  // Overflow-safe subtraction
    digitalWrite(13, !digitalRead(13));   // Toggle LED
    lastTime = current;
  }
  // Other non-blocking tasks can run here
}
```

**Justification (1 mark):**
- The expression `current - lastTime` uses **unsigned integer arithmetic modulo $2^{32}$**, which is overflow-safe — it correctly measures the elapsed time even across the rollover.
- `!digitalRead(13)` toggles the LED state without needing a separate flag variable.
- No `delay()` is used, so the CPU is free to execute other non-blocking tasks in the main loop.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (Arduino IDE Section):**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse** the IDE's output binary format — write `.hex` (Intel HEX), not `.bin` or `.exe`. Examiners deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Do NOT write** "Arduino uses C language" — it is technically **C++** (C with classes). This distinction is tested.
> 3. **Do NOT omit** the function prototype rule — the IDE auto-generates prototypes for functions defined after `loop()`. This is a favorite 2-mark question.
> 4. **Do NOT claim** that `analogRead()` returns voltage — it returns a **raw integer (0–1023)**. The student must multiply by $V_{ref}/1023$ to get volts.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Arduino IDE** = cross-platform Java/Electron app for writing, compiling, uploading Arduino sketches.
- [x] **Sketch** = program file with `.ino` extension, consisting of `setup()` and `loop()`.
- [x] **Hidden `main()`** is supplied by the IDE core; user never writes it.
- [x] **`setup()`** runs **once**; **`loop()`** runs **forever** in an infinite `for(;;)` cycle.
- [x] **Compilation pipeline:** preprocess → compile (`avr-gcc`) → assemble (`avr-as`) → link (`avr-ld`) → `avr-objcopy` → `.hex` → `avrdude` → flash.
- [x] **Bootloader** (optiboot, 512 bytes) handles self-programming over UART using the **STK500 protocol**.
- [x] **ADC resolution:** 10 bits → $V_{LSB} = V_{ref} / 1023$ (NOT 1024).
- [x] **`millis()`** rolls over after $\approx 49.7$ days; use **subtraction-based timing** to avoid bugs.
- [x] **PWM frequency:** $\approx 490$ Hz (default pins) or $\approx 980$ Hz (pins 5, 6) on Uno.
- [x] **Arduino language** = C++ (not pure C), with the Arduino core providing hardware abstraction.
- [x] **Board Manager** and **Library Manager** are accessible from the **Tools** and **Sketch** menus.
- [x] **Serial Monitor** is the primary debugging tool for printing variables and sensor data.
- [x] **Flash size limit** for Uno: 32 KB (minus 512-byte bootloader = 31.5 KB usable).
- [x] **SRAM** is the bottleneck — 2 KB on Uno, with stack, heap, and static variables all competing.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sample Code: LED, Switch, DC Motor, Stepper Motor Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECPT721 - EMBEDDED SYSTEMS AND MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sample Code: LED, Switch, DC Motor, Stepper Motor Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Defining Embedded I/O Interfacing

**Embedded System I/O Interfacing** is the discipline of electrically and logically connecting peripheral devices (actuators like LEDs/Motors and sensors like switches) to a microcontroller's General Purpose Input/Output (GPIO) ports such that firmware can observe and control the physical world in real-time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *"Interfacing of I/O devices such as LED, Switch, DC Motor, and Stepper Motor with a microcontroller (8051/ARM Cortex-M) using C/Assembly language involves configuring port direction registers, writing drive logic for actuators, and reading input registers for sensors."*

For KTU's OECPT721 (Embedded Systems and Microcontrollers), the canonical platform is the **Intel 8051** 8-bit microcontroller (and by extension, the modern **ARM Cortex-M** family). The four canonical I/O exercises are:
1. **LED Interfacing** → Digital Output (Sink/Source current)
2. **Switch Interfacing** → Digital Input (Active-High / Active-Low)
3. **DC Motor Control** → PWM-based speed + Direction control via H-Bridge
4. **Stepper Motor Control** → Multi-coil sequential excitation

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Postman" Model

Imagine your microcontroller as a **postman working inside a post office (the IC)**. The post office has several letterboxes (ports P0, P1, P2, P3). Each letterbox slot can either:

- **Receive a letter (Input)** → A switch telling the postman "door is open, turn on light"
- **Send out a letter (Output)** → A letter to the motor saying "spin left, spin right"

| Component | Postman Analogy | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **Port Pin** | A specific letterbox slot | One wire on the IC (bit-addressable) |
| **SFR (Special Function Register)** | The postman's delivery register | Memory-mapped control byte |
| **LED** | A light the postman switches on | Visual indicator (load ~10 mA) |
| **Switch** | A letter from the outside world | Boolean input (Logic 0 or 1) |
| **DC Motor** | A ceiling fan with a regulator | Continuous rotation, variable speed |
| **Stepper Motor** | A clock's second hand | Precise angular steps (e.g., 1.8°/step) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Microcontrollers cannot directly drive high-current devices. Just as a postman cannot move a heavy truck alone, the microcontroller pin (~10 mA source) needs a **driver IC** (transistor, relay, L293D, ULN2003) to control high-current loads.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Logic HIGH (Logic 1)** = **+5V** (for 8051 standard; **+3.3V** for many ARM Cortex-M boards)
- **Logic LOW (Logic 0)** = **0V** (GND)
- **8051 Sink Current per pin** = **~10 mA** (can sink more than it can source)
- **8051 Source Current per pin** = **~60 µA** (very weak)
- **Standard LED forward current** = **10–20 mA** (typically 15 mA)
- **LED forward voltage (V_f)** = **~2V (red)**, **~3.2V (blue/white)**
- **Stepper Motor step angle** = **1.8°** (200 steps/revolution) for NEMA 17
- **DC Motor rated voltage** = typically **5V, 12V, 24V**
- **PWM Frequency for DC Motor** = typically **1 kHz to 25 kHz** (above audible range)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Examiner Highlight:** Students often forget that the 8051's Port 0 has **open-drain outputs** and requires **external pull-up resistors** (typically **10 kΩ**). Port 1, 2, 3 have internal pull-ups. This distinction is a frequent **2-mark question** in Part A.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The 8051 GPIO Architecture (Foundation for All I/O Code)

The 8051 microcontroller has **four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0, P1, P2, P3), each consisting of:
- A **latch register** (SFR address: `0x80`, `0x90`, `0xA0`, `0xB0`)
- A **direction configuration** (achieved implicitly by writing 0 or 1)
- **Pin driver transistors**

The 8051 has **no separate Direction Register** (unlike modern ARM). Direction is controlled *per-instruction*:

- To make a pin **Output**: Write `1` to the latch (port SFR)
- To make a pin **Input**: Write `1` to the latch, then **read the pin** (this disables the lower driver)

> [!NOTE]
> **The Read-Modify-Write Problem:** Instructions like `P1 = P1 | 0x01;` (in C) or `SETB P1.0` (in ASM) are essential because a plain `MOV P1, A` would overwrite the entire port, causing a **"glitch"** on the other pins.

### Special Function Registers (SFRs) Relevant to I/O

| SFR Name | Address (Hex) | Function |
|---|---|---|
| `P0` | `0x80` | Port 0 (Open-drain; needs pull-up; also used as AD0-AD7) |
| `P1` | `0x90` | Port 1 (Internal pull-up; general-purpose) |
| `P2` | `0xA0` | Port 2 (Internal pull-up; also A8-A15) |
| `P3` | `0xB0` | Port 3 (Internal pull-up; alternate functions: RxD, TxD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD) |
| `TMOD` | `0x89` | Timer Mode register |
| `TCON` | `0x88` | Timer Control register |

---

## 2.2 LED Interfacing — The "Hello World" of Embedded

**Circuit Topology:**
- **Active-Low connection** is preferred (8051 sinks current better than it sources)
- LED anode → **+5V** (via current-limiting resistor)
- LED cathode → **Microcontroller pin**

When pin is **LOW (0V)**, current flows: LED is **ON**.
When pin is **HIGH (+5V)**, no current: LED is **OFF**.

**Why use a current-limiting resistor?**

Using Ohm's law to compute the required series resistance:

$$ R_{LED} = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}} $$

For a red LED with V_f = 2V, I_LED = 15 mA, V_CC = 5V:

$$ R_{LED} = \frac{5 - 2}{15 \times 10^{-3}} = 200 \;\Omega \;\;\text{(use standard 220 }\Omega\text{)} $$

---

## 2.3 Switch Interfacing — Two Canonical Topologies

### (a) Active-Low Switch (Most common, recommended)
- Switch connected between pin and **GND**
- **External pull-up resistor** (10 kΩ) to +5V
- When switch is **OPEN** → Pin reads **HIGH (1)**
- When switch is **CLOSED** → Pin reads **LOW (0)**
- **Advantage:** No floating voltage; immune to noise

### (b) Active-High Switch
- Switch connected between pin and **+5V**
- **External pull-down resistor** (10 kΩ) to GND
- When switch is **OPEN** → Pin reads **LOW (0)**
- When switch is **CLOSED** → Pin reads **HIGH (1)**

> [!WARNING]
> **Board Exam Pitfall:** If a switch is connected *without* a pull-up/pull-down resistor, the pin will read **random floating values** (electromagnetic noise), causing unpredictable behavior. This is a guaranteed **2-mark question** in KTU exams.

**Software Debouncing:** Mechanical switches "bounce" for ~5–20 ms. Software must wait and re-read:

```
if (switch == PRESSED) {
    delay_ms(20);  // debounce
    if (switch == PRESSED) { /* valid press */ }
}
```

---

## 2.4 DC Motor Control — PWM + H-Bridge

A DC motor requires two independent control dimensions:
1. **Speed** → Controlled by **Pulse Width Modulation (PWM)** duty cycle
2. **Direction** → Controlled by **H-Bridge polarity** (L293D, L298N)

### The L293D Motor Driver IC (Industry Standard)

| L293D Pin | Function |
|---|---|
| `1,2EN` (Pin 1) | Enable for Left H-Bridge; tie HIGH or to PWM |
| `1A, 2A` | Logic inputs from microcontroller |
| `1Y, 2Y` | Outputs to motor terminals |
| `3,4EN` (Pin 9) | Enable for Right H-Bridge |
| `VCC1` (Pin 16) | Logic supply (**+5V**) |
| `VCC2` (Pin 8) | Motor supply (**+12V or +24V**) |
| `GND` (Pins 4,5,12,13) | Common ground |

### Truth Table for One H-Bridge (per motor)

| IN1 | IN2 | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | **Stop** (coast) |
| 1 | 0 | **Rotate Forward** |
| 0 | 1 | **Rotate Reverse** |
| 1 | 1 | **Brake** (fast stop) |

### PWM Duty Cycle Formula

$$ \text{Duty Cycle} \; (\%) = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON} + T_{OFF}} \times 100 $$

$$ \text{Average Voltage} = V_{CC} \times \text{Duty Cycle} $$

$$ \text{Speed} \propto V_{avg} \propto \text{Duty Cycle} $$

---

## 2.5 Stepper Motor Control — Open-Loop Precision

A stepper motor rotates in **discrete angular steps** (typically **1.8° per step = 200 steps/rev** for NEMA 17). No feedback sensor is required — the motor *guarantees* step completion (or stalls, which is a known limitation).

**Two Excitation Sequences:**

### Full-Step Sequence (4-step wave drive)

| Step | A | B | A' | B' |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| 2 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 |
| 3 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 |
| 4 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 |
| **Hex** | **0x09** | **0x0C** | **0x06** | **0x03** |

### Half-Step Sequence (8-step, smoother)

| Step | A | B | A' | B' | Hex |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0x09 |
| 2 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0D |
| 3 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0x0C |
| 4 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0 | 0x0E |
| 5 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0x06 |
| 6 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 1 | 0x07 |
| 7 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x03 |
| 8 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 1 | 0x0B |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stepper Motor Direction:**
> - **Clockwise (CW):** Sequence 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 (forward through array)
> - **Counter-Clockwise (CCW):** Sequence 4 → 3 → 2 → 1 (reverse through array)
> **Direction reversal = array indexing reversal**, NOT changing the step pattern itself.

### Step Angle Formula

$$ \theta_{step} = \frac{360°}{N_{steps/rev}} $$

$$ N_{steps} = \text{Rotations desired} \times \frac{360°}{\theta_{step}} $$

For one full revolution with a 1.8° motor:

$$ N_{steps} = 1 \times \frac{360}{1.8} = 200 \;\text{steps} $$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LED Series Resistor | $R = \frac{V_{CC} - V_f}{I_{LED}}$ | Ω | Typically 220 Ω–470 Ω |
| 2 | PWM Duty Cycle | $D = \frac{T_{ON}}{T_{ON}+T_{OFF}} \times 100$ | % | Range: 0–100% |
| 3 | Average Motor Voltage | $V_{avg} = V_{CC} \times D$ | V | Linear control region |
| 4 | Step Angle | $\theta = \frac{360°}{N}$ | degrees | N = steps per revolution |
| 5 | Steps for Rotation | $S = R \times \frac{360°}{\theta}$ | steps | R = number of rotations |
| 6 | 8051 Timer Frequency | $f_{timer} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Hz | For 11.0592 MHz crystal → 921.6 kHz |
| 7 | Timer Reload Value | $TH0 = 256 - \text{count}$ | Hex | For 8-bit auto-reload |
| 8 | Timer Delay | $T = \frac{12 \times (65536 - X)}{f_{osc}}$ | seconds | For 16-bit mode |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Application | Component Used | Industry |
|---|---|---|
| **Dashboard indicators** | LEDs (active-low) | Automotive, IoT |
| **Keypads & limit switches** | Switches (active-low) | Industrial automation, Elevators |
| **Robotic wheels, conveyor belts** | DC Motor + PWM | Robotics, Manufacturing |
| **CNC machines, 3D printers, disk drives** | Stepper Motor | Precision automation |
| **Hard disk read head** | Voice-coil + stepper (combined) | Storage industry |
| **Surveillance camera pan-tilt** | Stepper (precise angular control) | Security systems |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Code Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> **Platform Used:** All code examples target the **8051 microcontroller** in **Embedded C** (using Keil µVision / SDCC syntax), which is the **mandatory programming paradigm** for KTU OECPT721. Pin mappings follow the standard **8051 Development Board** (P1 for LEDs, P3 for switches/motors).

---

## 3.1 Sample Code 1 — LED Blinking (Heartbeat of Embedded)

**Hardware Assumption:** 8 LEDs connected to **Port 1** (P1.0 to P1.7), active-low configuration.

```c
#include <reg51.h>   // 8051 SFR definitions

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);   // Tuned for 11.0592 MHz crystal
}

void main(void) {
    P1 = 0x00;        // Step 1: All LEDs ON (active-low → 0 = ON)
    
    while (1) {       // Step 2: Infinite loop (embedded super-loop)
        P1 = 0x00;        // All LEDs ON
        delay_ms(500);    // 500 ms visible
        P1 = 0xFF;        // All LEDs OFF (1 = OFF in active-low)
        delay_ms(500);    // 500 ms visible
    }
}
```

### Step-by-Step Logic Walkthrough

| Line | Action | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| `#include <reg51.h>` | Includes SFR definitions | Lets compiler recognize `P1` as SFR `0x90` |
| `P1 = 0x00;` | Writes 0x00 to Port 1 latch | In active-low: 0V on pin = current flows = LED ON |
| `delay_ms(500);` | Software delay loop | Creates human-visible blink rate (≥100 ms) |
| `P1 = 0xFF;` | Writes 0xFF to Port 1 latch | +5V on pin = no current = LED OFF |
| `while(1)` | Super-loop | Microcontroller runs forever; no OS to return to |

> [!TIP]
> **Alternative Bit-Level Control:** Instead of `P1 = 0xFF`, you can use `P1 = ~0x01;` to blink only the **least significant bit (P1.0)**.

---

## 3.2 Sample Code 2 — Switch-Controlled LED (Input → Output)

**Hardware Assumption:** Switch on **P3.0** (active-low, with 10 kΩ pull-up); LED on **P1.0** (active-low).

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit SW   = P3^0;     // Define switch pin (P3.0)
sbit LED  = P1^0;     // Define LED pin (P1.0)

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}

void main(void) {
    SW = 1;           // Step 1: Make P3.0 input (write 1 to latch, then read pin)
    LED = 0;          // LED initially OFF (active-low: 1 = OFF)
    
    while (1) {
        if (SW == 0) {                    // Switch PRESSED (active-low)
            delay_ms(20);                 // Debounce delay
            if (SW == 0) {                // Confirm still pressed
                LED = 0;                  // Turn LED ON
            }
        } else {                          // Switch RELEASED
            LED = 1;                      // Turn LED OFF
        }
    }
}
```

### Analytical Matrix — Switch-LED Mapping

| Switch State (P3.0) | Logic Level | After Debounce | LED State (P1.0) | LED Visual |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open (not pressed) | HIGH (1) | — | HIGH (1) | **OFF** |
| Just pressed (bouncing) | Oscillating | Skipped by debounce | (unchanged) | (unchanged) |
| Stable pressed | LOW (0) | Confirmed | LOW (0) | **ON** |
| Just released (bouncing) | Oscillating | Skipped by debounce | (unchanged) | (unchanged) |
| Stable released | HIGH (1) | — | HIGH (1) | **OFF** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Trick:** The line `SW = 1;` is NOT a useless statement. It is **mandatory** to make P3.0 an input pin on 8051. Forgetting this will cause the port pin to output LOW (because 8051 ports default to FF on reset, but the *input mode* is only activated when you read the port). Always initialize input pins by writing 1.

---

## 3.3 Sample Code 3 — DC Motor Control (Direction + Speed via PWM)

**Hardware Assumption:** 
- DC motor connected to **L293D outputs 1Y, 2Y**
- L293D inputs **1A = P3.0**, **2A = P3.1**
- L293D enable pin **1,2EN = P3.2** (PWM input)
- Timer 0 generates PWM

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit M1   = P3^0;     // L293D input 1A  (direction bit 1)
sbit M2   = P3^1;     // L293D input 2A  (direction bit 2)
sbit PWM  = P3^2;     // L293D enable    (PWM output)

void timer0_init(void) {
    TMOD = 0x02;      // Step 1: Timer 0, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)
    TH0  = 0x00;      // Step 2: Reload value for ~100% duty cycle baseline
    TL0  = 0x00;      // Step 3: Start count from 0
    TR0  = 1;         // Step 4: Start Timer 0
}

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}

void set_speed(unsigned char duty) {
    // duty: 0-100 (percentage)
    // We use a simple software PWM: ON for `duty` ms, OFF for (100-duty) ms
    unsigned int on_time  = duty * 10;
    unsigned int off_time = (100 - duty) * 10;
    
    PWM = 1;
    delay_ms(on_time);
    PWM = 0;
    delay_ms(off_time);
}

void main(void) {
    M1 = 0; M2 = 0; PWM = 0;
    
    // ----- FORWARD with 70% speed -----
    M1 = 1; M2 = 0;    // Direction: FORWARD
    set_speed(70);
    
    // ----- REVERSE with 50% speed -----
    M1 = 0; M2 = 1;    // Direction: REVERSE
    set_speed(50);
    
    // ----- STOP -----
    M1 = 0; M2 = 0;
    PWM = 0;
    while (1);         // Idle forever
}
```

### Direction & Speed Truth Table (Verified)

| M1 (P3.0) | M2 (P3.1) | PWM (P3.2) | L293D Output | Motor Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | Both low, disabled | **Stop (coast)** |
| 0 | 0 | 1 | Both low, enabled | Stop (active hold) |
| 1 | 0 | 1 | 1Y=H, 2Y=L | **Forward** |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 1Y=L, 2Y=H | **Reverse** |
| 1 | 1 | 1 | Both high | **Brake** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hardware PWM vs Software PWM:** The example above uses a simple **software PWM** for clarity (KTU accepts both). Industrial code uses **Timer 0 in Mode 2** with `TH0 = 256 - duty_count` to generate true hardware PWM, freeing the CPU for other tasks.

---

## 3.4 Sample Code 4 — Stepper Motor Control (Full-Step Drive)

**Hardware Assumption:**
- Stepper motor coils: **A → P1.0, B → P1.1, A' → P1.2, B' → P1.3**
- Driver: **ULN2003** (for unipolar) or **L293D** (for bipolar)
- Full-step sequence used (wave drive, 4 steps)

```c
#include <reg51.h>

// Lookup table: Full-step sequence for CW rotation
//  Bit 3  Bit 2  Bit 1  Bit 0
//   B'      A'     B      A
unsigned char code step_table[4] = {0x09, 0x0C, 0x06, 0x03};
//            1     0     0     0     → Step 0
//            0     1     1     0     → Step 1
//            0     1     1     0     → ... etc

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}

void rotate_cw(unsigned int steps) {
    unsigned int i;
    for (i = 0; i < steps; i++) {
        P1 = step_table[i % 4];    // Step through table modulo 4
        delay_ms(10);              // Step delay (10 ms = 100 steps/sec)
    }
}

void rotate_ccw(unsigned int steps) {
    int i;
    for (i = steps - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
        P1 = step_table[i % 4];    // Reverse index
        delay_ms(10);
    }
}

void main(void) {
    P1 = 0x00;        // All coils OFF initially
    
    // Rotate 1 full revolution (200 steps) clockwise
    rotate_cw(200);
    delay_ms(500);    // Pause
    
    // Rotate half revolution (100 steps) counter-clockwise
    rotate_ccw(100);
    
    while (1);        // Done
}
```

### Step-by-Step Execution Trace (First 5 iterations of CW loop)

| Iteration (i) | i % 4 | step_table[i%4] | P1 binary | Coil A | Coil B | Coil A' | Coil B' | Angle |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0x09 | `1001` | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | 0° |
| 1 | 1 | 0x0C | `1100` | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | 1.8° |
| 2 | 2 | 0x06 | `0110` | OFF | ON | ON | OFF | 3.6° |
| 3 | 3 | 0x03 | `0011` | OFF | OFF | ON | ON | 5.4° |
| 4 | 0 | 0x09 | `1001` | ON | OFF | OFF | ON | 7.2° |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Speed Control via Delay:** The `delay_ms(10)` value directly controls the **stepping rate**:
> - `delay_ms(10)` → 100 steps/sec → 1.8° × 100 = 180°/sec
> - `delay_ms(1)` → 1000 steps/sec → 1800°/sec (very fast)
> - `delay_ms(50)` → 20 steps/sec → 36°/sec (slow, high torque)

---

## 3.5 Advanced — Half-Step Sequence (Bonus KTU Concept)

```c
// Half-step table (8 steps, smoother rotation)
unsigned char code half_step[8] = {0x09, 0x0D, 0x0C, 0x0E, 
                                   0x06, 0x07, 0x03, 0x0B};

void rotate_half_step_cw(unsigned int steps) {
    unsigned int i;
    for (i = 0; i < steps; i++) {
        P1 = half_step[i % 8];
        delay_ms(10);
    }
}
```

**Why half-step?** It doubles the resolution (**0.9°/step = 400 steps/rev**) and reduces mechanical resonance (less vibration), at the cost of ~30% lower torque.

---

## 3.6 Combined Integrated Program — All Four I/Os Together

```c
#include <reg51.h>

sbit LED     = P1^0;
sbit SW      = P3^0;
sbit M1      = P3^1;
sbit M2      = P3^2;
sbit PWM     = P3^3;

unsigned char code step_table[4] = {0x09, 0x0C, 0x06, 0x03};

void delay_ms(unsigned int ms) {
    unsigned int i, j;
    for (i = 0; i < ms; i++)
        for (j = 0; j < 1275; j++);
}

void main(void) {
    unsigned int i;
    P1 = 0x00; P3 = 0x00;
    
    while (1) {
        // --- Task 1: Switch-controlled LED ---
        if (SW == 0) {
            delay_ms(20);
            if (SW == 0) LED = 0;   // LED ON
        } else {
            LED = 1;                  // LED OFF
        }
        
        // --- Task 2: DC motor forward at variable speed ---
        M1 = 1; M2 = 0;
        PWM = 1; delay_ms(7);
        PWM = 0; delay_ms(3);  // 70% duty cycle
        
        // --- Task 3: Stepper motor, one step at a time ---
        for (i = 0; i < 4; i++) {
            P1 = (P1 & 0xF0) | (step_table[i] & 0x0F);  // Preserve upper nibble
            delay_ms(10);
        }
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** The `P1 = (P1 & 0xF0) | ...` line uses **read-modify-write** to protect the LED bit (P1.0) from being disturbed by stepper operations on P1.0–P1.3. A plain `P1 = step_table[i];` would clobber the LED state — a guaranteed **3-mark loss** in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
