## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part A questions are **direct, definition-based or short-comparison** questions. Answers should fit in **one half-page to one full page** of the answer book. No lengthy derivations required.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Define a Task in an RTOS. List the typical fields stored in a Task Control Block (TCB).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **task** in an RTOS is an independent, schedulable thread of execution that the kernel can dispatch onto the CPU. Each task has its own stack, register context, and priority. A task is the fundamental unit of work scheduled by the kernel.

**Typical fields in a TCB (list any 4 for full marks):**

1. **Task ID / Name** — unique identifier.
2. **Task State** — Ready / Running / Blocked / Suspended.
3. **Task Priority** — used by the scheduler.
4. **Stack Pointer** — points to top of task's private stack.
5. **Saved CPU Registers** — for context restoration.
6. **Delay / Event Wait Counters** — for blocked state.
7. **Linked-List Pointers** — for queue insertion.

*(1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing the TCB fields.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between Preemptive and Non-Preemptive (Co-operative) scheduling. Which is preferred in hard real-time embedded systems and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Preemptive | Non-Preemptive (Co-operative) |
|---|---|---|
| **CPU Takeover** | Kernel can force-stop a running task | Task voluntarily yields CPU |
| **Response Time** | Bounded and predictable | Unbounded (depends on longest task) |
| **Implementation** | More complex (needs context save on every preemption) | Simpler |
| **Overhead** | Higher | Lower |
| **Use Case** | Hard real-time systems | Soft real-time, low-power MCUs |

**Preferred in hard real-time embedded systems: Preemptive scheduling** (1 mark), because it guarantees **bounded worst-case response time** (1 mark) and ensures a high-priority event (e.g., airbag deploy) is serviced within its deadline regardless of the current task (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Part B questions are **analytical / design questions**. They are split into sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying 7 marks. You may be given an *internal choice* — answer either OR. Each sub-part requires either a derivation, a diagram, or a worked-out numerical example.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the various **states of a task** in an RTOS with the help of a neat state-transition diagram. Describe in detail the role of the **Task Control Block (TCB)** in context switching. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Three periodic real-time tasks are scheduled under **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** on a single-core processor:

| Task | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Deadline $D_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 1 | 5 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 10 | 10 |

Determine whether the task set is **schedulable** using: (i) the Liu–Layland utilization bound, and (ii) exact response-time analysis. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Task States — Explanation (4 marks):**

A task in an RTOS is always in one of the following discrete states:

- **Dormant:** Task is created in memory but the kernel has not yet activated it for scheduling. The TCB exists but the task is not in any ready queue.
- **Ready:** Task is fully prepared to run and sits in the *Ready Queue*, waiting for the scheduler to dispatch it.
- **Running:** Task currently holds the CPU and is executing instructions. On a single-core MCU, only one task can be in this state at any instant.
- **Blocked:** Task has voluntarily given up the CPU (e.g., by calling a delay, semaphore wait, or message-queue receive) and is waiting for an event.
- **Suspended (optional):** Task is *frozen* by explicit suspend API and will not be scheduled until explicitly resumed.

**State-Transition Diagram (1 mark):**

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> dormantA
    dormantA --> readyA : Activate
    readyA --> runningA : Dispatch
    runningA --> readyA : Preempted
    runningA --> blockedA : Wait event
    blockedA --> readyA : Event occurred
    runningA --> suspendedA : Suspend
    suspendedA --> readyA : Resume
    runningA --> [*] : Terminate
```

**Role of TCB in Context Switching (2 marks):**

The **Task Control Block (TCB)** is the kernel's "memory" about each task. During a context switch:

1. The kernel **saves** the outgoing task's CPU registers (PC, SP, LR, R0–R12, xPSR on ARM) by pushing them onto the outgoing task's private stack, and stores the new stack pointer into the outgoing task's TCB.
2. The kernel **loads** the incoming task's saved stack pointer from its TCB and **restores** its CPU registers by popping them from its stack.
3. The kernel updates the TCB's `TaskState` field for both tasks.
4. Execution seamlessly continues inside the new task, as if it had been preempted just one clock cycle ago.

The TCB is therefore **indispensable** for preemptive multitasking — without it, the CPU would have no record of where a task left off.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 1 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 2 | 10 |

**(i) Liu–Layland Utilization Bound (3 marks):**

Total utilization:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i} = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} = 0.25 + 0.20 + 0.20 = 0.65
$$

Upper bound for $n = 3$:

$$
U_{bound}(3) = 3 \cdot \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \cdot (1.2599 - 1) = 3 \cdot 0.2599 \approx 0.7798
$$

Since $U = 0.65 \le 0.7798 = U_{bound}(3)$, the **sufficient condition is satisfied**. The task set is *guaranteed schedulable* under RMS by this test.

**(ii) Exact Response-Time Analysis (4 marks):**

Compute $R_1, R_2, R_3$ and check $R_i \le D_i$.

**For $\tau_1$** (highest priority, no higher-priority tasks):

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 1\ \text{ms} \le D_1 = 4\ \text{ms}\ \checkmark
$$

**For $\tau_2$** ($hp(2) = \{1\}$):

Iteration 0: $R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 1$ ms.

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{1}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 + 1 = 2\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 1 + 1 = 2\ \text{ms}\ \text{(converged)}
$$

$R_2 = 2\ \text{ms} \le D_2 = 5\ \text{ms}\ \checkmark$

**For $\tau_3$** ($hp(3) = \{1, 2\}$):

Iteration 0: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 2$ ms.

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{2}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4\ \text{ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{4} \right\rceil \cdot 1 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil \cdot 1 = 2 + 1 + 1 = 4\ \text{ms}\ \text{(converged)}
$$

$R_3 = 4\ \text{ms} \le D_3 = 10\ \text{ms}\ \checkmark$

**Conclusion:** All three tasks satisfy $R_i \le D_i$. The task set is **schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling with significant slack (60% of CPU time is free).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the architecture of a **preemptive RTOS kernel scheduler**. Describe the **steps of a context switch** in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A real-time system has **two periodic tasks**:

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 2 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 4 | 10 |

(i) Check schedulability using the **Rate Monotonic utilization bound**. (ii) If EDF (Earliest Deadline First) is used instead, determine the maximum schedulable utilization. Comment on the result. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Preemptive RTOS Kernel Scheduler Architecture (4 marks):**

The kernel scheduler is the heart of an RTOS. Its architecture consists of:

- **Tick Generator** (hardware timer, e.g., SysTick on Cortex-M): produces periodic interrupts at the configured tick rate (typically 1 kHz).
- **Tick ISR (Interrupt Service Routine):** increments a system tick counter and invokes the scheduler.
- **Scheduler Core:** examines the ready queue and selects the highest-priority task.
- **Ready Queue / Priority Bitmap:** holds all tasks in the Ready state, ordered by priority.
- **Context Switch Module:** performs register save/restore.

```mermaid
graph TD
    TickGen[Hardware Tick Generator] --> TickISR[Tick ISR]
    TickISR --> SchedCore[Scheduler Core]
    SchedCore --> ReadyQ[Ready Queue / Priority Bitmap]
    SchedCore --> BlockedQ[Blocked Queue]
    SchedCore --> ContextSwitch[Context Switch Module]
    ContextSwitch --> CPU[CPU Core]
    CPU -.saves.-> TCB_A[Outgoing Task TCB]
    CPU -.restores.-> TCB_B[Incoming Task TCB]
```

**Steps of a Context Switch (3 marks):**

1. **Tick fires** or **blocking event** calls the scheduler.
2. The scheduler **saves the outgoing task's CPU registers** (PC, SP, LR, general-purpose regs, status register) onto the outgoing task's stack. The stack pointer is stored in the outgoing TCB.
3. The scheduler **updates the outgoing task's state** in its TCB (Ready or Blocked as appropriate) and removes/inserts it into the correct queue.
4. The scheduler **selects the highest-priority Ready task** by scanning the priority bitmap.
5. The scheduler **loads the incoming task's stack pointer** from its TCB.
6. The kernel **restores the incoming task's CPU registers** from its stack (POP / exception-return sequence on Cortex-M).
7. The incoming task **resumes execution** as if it had been preempted one cycle ago. The interrupt-return mechanism automatically updates PC, SP, and PSR atomically.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 2 | 5 |
| $\tau_2$ | 4 | 10 |

**(i) Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound (3 marks):**

Total utilization:

$$
U = \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{4}{10} = 0.4 + 0.4 = 0.8
$$

RMS upper bound for $n = 2$:

$$
U_{bound}(2) = 2 \cdot (2^{1/2} - 1) = 2 \cdot (\sqrt{2} - 1) = 2 \cdot 0.4142 \approx 0.8284
$$

Since $U = 0.8 \le 0.8284 = U_{bound}(2)$, the task set is **schedulable under RMS** by the sufficient bound test. (1 mark)

**(ii) Maximum EDF Schedulable Utilization (3 marks):**

Under **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** scheduling, the necessary and sufficient schedulability condition is:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le 1
$$

Here $U = 0.8 \le 1$, so the task set is **schedulable under EDF** with **20% CPU headroom** (idle time). (1 mark)

**Comment (1 mark):** EDF is theoretically optimal for uniprocessors — it can achieve up to 100% utilization, whereas RMS is limited to $U_{bound}(n) \le \ln 2 \approx 69.3\%$ in the worst case (large $n$). However, RMS has lower *implementation overhead* (fixed priorities are easy to assign at design time) and *better transient overload behavior* than EDF, which is why RMS is more commonly used in commercial embedded products despite the lower theoretical bound.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing `|` in tables with markdown syntax:** Never write `$|x|$` inside a markdown table — use `$\vert x\vert$` or `$\mid x\mid$` or simply write "absolute value of x" in words.
> 2. **Confusing Task vs. Process:** Marks lost if the answer does not distinguish thread-based tasks (FreeRTOS, uC/OS) from heavy processes (Linux).
> 3. **Forgetting iteration in response-time analysis:** Students often write the formula and the first iteration only. The KTU examiner expects **at least 2 iterations** or **explicit convergence statement**.
> 4. **Mixing up RMS and EDF bounds:** $U_{bound}^{RMS}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ is for **fixed priority RMS** only. EDF has the simple $U \le 1$ condition.
> 5. **Omitting the state diagram:** A 7-mark question on task states *always* expects a labeled diagram, not just text.
> 6. **Using deadline = period implicitly:** Always state the assumption $D_i = T_i$ explicitly before solving.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Final 60-second revision list — pin this in your brain before the KTU exam.**

- **Task** = schedulable unit of execution. Has its own stack, TCB, and priority.
- **Process vs. Task:** Process = heavy (own address space). Task = light (shares memory, used in embedded RTOS).
- **Five task states:** Dormant, Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended. Know all transitions cold.
- **TCB** stores: TaskID, State, Priority, Stack Pointer, Saved Registers, Event Wait Flags, List Pointers.
- **Context switch** = save outgoing registers + restore incoming registers. On Cortex-M this takes **1–10 µs**.
- **Preemptive kernel** = kernel can force-stop a running task. **Hard real-time** systems always use preemptive.
- **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):** shorter period ⇒ higher priority. Sufficient bound:
  $$U = \sum \frac{C_i}{T_i} \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$$
- **Earliest Deadline First (EDF):** dynamic priority; sufficient and necessary condition $U \le 1$ on a single processor.
- **Response-Time Recurrence** (exact test):
  $$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
  Iterate until $R_i$ converges; schedulable if $R_i \le D_i$.
- **Idle Task** runs when no other task is ready. It is also a *task* (lowest priority).
- **Tick rate** of the scheduler is typically **1 ms** (1000 Hz). Lower tick rate ⇒ less overhead but coarser timing.
- **Stack sizing rule of thumb:** $S_{stack} = S_{call} + S_{local} + S_{ISR} + S_{margin}$ with $S_{margin} \ge 256$ bytes.
- **Common RTOS in KTU labs:** FreeRTOS (open-source, most likely exam reference), uC/OS-II, Keil RTX.
- **Liu & Layland (1973)** is the seminal paper — KTU loves asking "who proposed RMS bound?" or "what is the bound for n=3?"
- **Co-operative (non-preemptive) scheduling** is simpler but **not suitable for hard real-time** because worst-case response time is unbounded.
- **Round-robin** is used for *equal-priority* tasks; each gets a time slice (quantum) of the CPU.
- **Hard real-time** = missing a deadline is a system failure. **Soft real-time** = missing a deadline degrades quality only.
- **Magic number to remember:** $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ — the asymptotic lower bound of RMS for $n \to \infty$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Closing Tip:** In the KTU 2024 ESE, almost every Module-4 question will combine a **state diagram** with a **numerical utilization/response-time calculation**. Practice drawing the state diagram from memory, and memorize the four key formulas (RMS bound, EDF bound, Response-Time recurrence, CPU Utilization). These four alone fetch ~10 of the 14 marks in most Part-B questions.
